Oklahoma Governor Mary Fallin signed a bill into law on Monday, banning cities across the state from setting their own minimum wage, vacation, or sick-day requirements. The legislation, passed by the state's Republican-led House and Senate, comes as a response to an ongoing grassroots effort in Oklahoma City to raise the city's minimum wage to $10.10 per hour, a rate supported by President Barack Obama on the national level. Critics argue that the bill specifically targets Oklahoma City, where organizers have been collecting signatures on a petition to put the issue to a citywide vote. According to David Slane, the lawyer behind the petition, the state's decision to pass the bill undermines the right of citizens to decide on minimum wage. With a goal of collecting 4,000 signatures by the end of April, the organizers aim to reach the required 80,000 signatures to bring the issue to a statewide vote.

This week, Governor Fallin's office defended the bill, stating that raising the minimum wage is not an effective way to combat poverty. However, this assertion contradicts findings from the Economic Policy Institute, which reports that nearly 90 percent of workers who would benefit from a minimum wage increase are over 20 years old, with an average age of 35, and more than a quarter have children to support. Furthermore, over half of these workers are employed full-time. Supporters of the ban claim that it will protect local businesses and maintain economic competitiveness. State Representative Randy Grau argued that an artificially raised minimum wage could harm local economies within months. Senator Dan Newberry echoed similar sentiments, suggesting that the bill creates a safer business environment that allows Oklahoma to remain economically competitive and foster job growth.

The bill's passage has sparked concerns about the impact on low-income workers, with some lawmakers labeling the current minimum wage of $7.25 per hour as "immoral" due to its inability to lift individuals above the poverty line despite working full-time. In contrast, Governor Fallin pointed to a Congressional Budget Office report indicating that a nationwide minimum wage hike could result in the loss of approximately half a million jobs. The governor also claimed that most minimum wage workers are young, single individuals working part-time or entry-level jobs, often living with their parents in middle-class families. However, this characterization has been disputed by experts, who argue that it perpetuates a common myth about low-wage workers.

A petition being circulated in the city called for raising the local minimum wage to $10.10—the level President Obama is pushing—from the current federal requirement of $7.25, the Oklahoman reports. The coalition behind the petition says it is considering a constitutional challenge to the new law. Gov. Mary Fallin, who signed the bill into law, rejected the argument that allowing cities to raise the minimum wage would bring people out of poverty, saying having different minimum wages across the state could scare off businesses and a higher minimum wage would cost jobs. "Most minimum wage workers are young, single people working part-time or entry-level jobs," she said. "Many are high school or college students living with their parents in middle-class families." Numerous studies, however, have found this to be untrue, Al Jazeera notes. A recent Economic Policy Institute report found that almost 90% of minimum wage workers are over 20, a quarter have children to support, and more than half work full time.

A scandal surrounding US Representative Anthony Weiner erupted when a young woman leaked screenshots of her post-resignation sexting conversations with him on the gossip website TheDirty.com. Initially, the woman wished to remain anonymous, but due to the nature of the internet, her identity was quickly revealed by BuzzFeed, which obtained images from her Facebook and Formspring profiles that matched the blurred shots posted on TheDirty.com. The woman, later identified as Sydney Elaine Leathers, was found to be an Indiana resident in her early twenties who worked as a field organizer for Obama for America and listed Weiner as one of her top heroes. On social media platforms, Leathers expressed her support for Weiner, stating that he "did nothing wrong" and could continue sending explicit photos "every single day" if he continued to serve as a legislator.

According to a high school friend, Leathers engaged in raunchy online conversations with Weiner, during which he allegedly promised to relocate her to either Chicago or New York. This friend claimed that Leathers believed herself to be the most important person in the relationship, even surpassing Huma Abedin, Weiner's wife. Abedin initially defended her husband at a press conference, but in a subsequent statement, she explained her decision to stay with Weiner, describing him as a "smart, caring, and dedicated person."

reference: – BuzzFeed has identified Anthony Weiner's newly revealed sexting partner as Sydney Elaine Leathers, a progressive activist in her early 20s who lives in Indiana. Pictures on her Facebook and Formspring accounts look pretty close to the blurred images offered up on The Dirty when it broke the sexting story, and she's talked about Weiner quite a bit online (often on accounts that are now deleted). For example, in a June 2011 Facebook post, she declared: "Rep. Weiner can continue sending dick pics every single day for the rest of his life as long as he continues to legislate like he does. I decided." Her TwitPic account identifies her as an Obama for America field organizer. She also listed Weiner as one of her heroes, wrote at least one blog post about his resignation, and said on Facebook that he shouldn't have resigned. An anonymous source confirms BuzzFeed got the ID right and says he or she saw the messages exchanged a year ago, but a BuzzFeed reporter asked Weiner last night if he knew Leathers and—shocker—got no response. The source, who says he or she has known Leathers since high school, says she "felt she was the important one" to Weiner, even more important than wife Huma Abedin. As for Abedin, Daily Intel notes that she came to a "prescient conclusion" in September's Harper's Bazaar: "People have said many things about my husband—some nice, some not so nice. And that will surely continue. Launching this campaign was not an easy decision for our family to make. Putting yourself out there comes with a cost." (Meanwhile, "cubicle guy" has become a viral sensation after Weiner's press conference last night.)

final_summary:
On Friday, President Donald Trump made a sudden and dramatic change in leadership within his administration, announcing that he had replaced his chief of staff, Reince Priebus, with Homeland Security Secretary John Kelly. According to Priebus, he had submitted his resignation to the President privately on Thursday, but the news was only confirmed publicly by Trump via a tweet shortly after he landed on Air Force One at Joint Base Andrews outside Washington, as reported by CNN. 

Priebus, who had held the position since June, described the move as a "reset button" that the President had a right to hit, and expressed his continued loyalty to Trump, stating that he would still be a "Trump fan" and work towards achieving the President's goals. However, sources close to Priebus initially claimed that he had not resigned, sparking confusion and speculation about the circumstances surrounding his departure.

The move marked the end of a tumultuous six-month tenure for Priebus, who had faced intense scrutiny for his inability to stem the flow of leaks and impose order within the chaotic White House. Despite being brought on board by Trump due to his Washington connections, Priebus bore significant blame for the administration's legislative failures, including the collapse of efforts to pass a healthcare replacement bill.

Rumors of internal conflicts within the White House had been simmering for months, culminating in a public feud between Priebus and newly appointed Communications Director Anthony Scaramucci, who had launched a scathing attack on Priebus in a recent interview, calling him a "paranoid schizophrenic." Trump failed to defend his top aide, further exacerbating tensions.

In contrast, Trump praised Kelly, describing him as a "Great American" and a "Great Leader," and highlighting his impressive background as a nearly five-decade veteran of the US military, including stints as chief of Southern Command and senior assistant to the Secretary of Defense. Kelly will take over as chief of staff on Monday, participating in a meeting of Trump's cabinet, and will oversee a transition period alongside Priebus.

The shake-up marks the shortest tenure of a White House chief of staff in modern history, with Priebus lasting just 189 days in the role. His departure follows a series of high-profile exits from the administration, including the departures of Press Secretary Sean Spicer and Deputy Chief of Staff Katie Walsh, both of whom were close allies of Priebus.

As news of Priebus' departure broke, Trump traveled to Long Island for an event on gang violence, where he was accompanied by Priebus and Representative Peter King. There was reportedly no indication of the impending shake-up during the trip, with Priebus maintaining a "poker face" aboard Air Force One.

Trump's family, including daughter Ivanka and son-in-law Jared Kushner, had reportedly lost confidence in Priebus, with one source claiming they urged the President to make a change. Meanwhile, White House Chief Strategist Steve Bannon is expected to remain in his position, despite rumors of his potential departure.

The sudden shift in leadership comes as the White House continues to grapple with controversy and infighting, with Trump facing mounting pressure to address pressing policy issues and restore stability within his administration.

final_summary:
On April 10, a remarkable incident unfolded at the Royal Burgers Zoo in Arnhem, Netherlands, showcasing the intelligence and resourcefulness of a chimpanzee named Tushi. A Dutch TV crew was filming a documentary using a camera-equipped drone, which caught the attention of the chimpanzees in their 1.7-acre enclosure. Two females, Tushi and Raime, climbed onto scaffolding, each grasping a 6-foot-long branch, while others nearby picked up smaller twigs.

As the drone drew closer, Tushi, with a deliberate and determined expression, swiped at it twice with her branch, successfully knocking the device to the ground on the second attempt. The camera captured the moment, revealing Tushi's grimacing face and tense posture, indicating a calculated and forceful action rather than a fearful or reflexive response.

Researchers Jan van Hooff and Bas Lukkenaar, whose study was published in the journal Primates, analyzed the incident and concluded that Tushi's actions suggest forward planning and deliberation. The pair noted that Tushi had collected the branch before the drone approached and carried it to a location where it could be effectively used as a weapon, demonstrating a level of premeditation.

According to van Hooff and Lukkenaar, this behavior is consistent with previous observations of the chimpanzees at the Royal Burgers Zoo, who have shown a propensity for innovative tool use and problem-solving. The researchers pointed out that the chimps have not been explicitly taught how to use various tools but have learned through observation of humans handling different implements.

In an interview, van Hooff commented on the uniqueness of Tushi's actions, stating that the use of the stick as a weapon in this context appeared deliberate, given the decision to collect and position it for optimal effect. Lukkenaar added that this incident contributes to the understanding that chimpanzees engage in forward planning of tool-use acts, highlighting their ability to think critically and adapt to new situations.

The incident occurred in New York | Heidelberg on April 10, and the study was later published in the journal Primates. The findings have significant implications for our understanding of chimpanzee cognition and behavior. The collaboration between the Royal Burgers Zoo and the Japan Monkey Centre, with support from Springer, a leading publisher of scientific research, made this study possible.

That's according to a new study in Primates. "This episode adds to the indications that chimpanzees engage in forward planning of tool-use acts," researcher Bas Lukkenaar says in a press release. The Christian Science Monitor reports a Dutch TV crew was using the drone to film a documentary on the chimps at the Royal Burgers' Zoo in April. After a test-run with the drone, the chimps started collecting sticks and climbing scaffolding. When the drone returned, a chimp named Tushi was ready, swiping it out of the air with a 6-foot branch. While studying footage from the busted drone, researchers focused on Tushi's facial expression during the attack, NBC News reports. They found the grimace she makes before and during the incident is similar to what humans do while carrying out a predetermined use of force. According to the press release, the lack of fear in Tushi's expression means she wasn't simply attacking out of reflex. The Monitor explains it's the difference between a calculated plan and swatting at a mosquito. This isn't unusual behavior for the chimps at Burgers' Zoo; they've taught themselves to use 13 different types of tools, the press release states. (Now find out how chimps are more advanced than humans.)

final_summary:
Charlo Greene, a 28-year-old former TV reporter named Charlene Egbe, is facing a potential 54-year prison sentence for running the Alaska Cannabis Club, a business she founded to provide marijuana to patients in Alaska after the state legalized recreational pot in 2014. Greene's case has sparked controversy and raised questions about the ongoing war on drugs, particularly in light of the increasing trend of states moving towards marijuana legalization. 

Greene's journey began when she worked as a reporter for KTVA, a CBS affiliate in Alaska, covering crime and courts, including marijuana-related stories. Her passion for marijuana advocacy grew after meeting activists in Colorado and Washington, the first states to legalize recreational marijuana, and learning about its medicinal benefits. In 2014, Greene revealed her ownership of the Alaska Cannabis Club on live television, using a profanity-laced statement that made headlines worldwide. However, instead of supporting her cause, many people seem to have forgotten about her plight.

Despite Alaska's voter-approved initiative to legalize marijuana, the state failed to establish clear regulations for the industry. As a result, Greene's club operated under a unique business model, offering "memberships" in exchange for donations, which allowed customers to receive marijuana. This approach drew the attention of authorities, who conducted multiple undercover operations and raided the club twice, resulting in Greene being charged with 14 felony and misdemeanor offenses. Prosecutors argue that the club and Greene are one entity, citing the sole proprietorship status of the business.

Court records show that Greene was not directly involved in any of the transactions that led to the raids, but she is still facing severe penalties, potentially spending decades in prison. Critics argue that the charges are excessive and that Greene should have received a fine or citation, similar to those issued for selling alcohol without a license. Experts believe it's unlikely Greene will serve the maximum sentence, but the uncertainty weighs heavily on her mind.

Greene's case has drawn comparisons to a "modern-day lynching," highlighting the disproportionate impact of the war on drugs on communities of color. The raids, carried out by the Anchorage Police Department, have also been criticized, with allegations of misconduct and abuse of power. Greene's plea for support from the cannabis community has fallen on deaf ears, leaving her feeling isolated and uncertain about her future.

As Alaska continues to navigate the complexities of regulating its marijuana industry, Greene's case serves as a stark reminder of the challenges faced by entrepreneurs and advocates in the space. With her trial looming, Greene remains hopeful that the cannabis community will rally behind her, providing the support she needs to overcome this difficult chapter in her life.

Charlo Greene, a 28-year-old former TV reporter named Charlene Egbe, is facing a potential 54-year prison sentence for running the Alaska Cannabis Club, a business she founded to provide marijuana to patients in Alaska after the state legalized recreational pot in 2014. Greene's case has sparked controversy and raised questions about the ongoing war on drugs, particularly in light of the increasing trend of states moving towards marijuana legalization. 

Greene's journey began when she worked as a reporter for KTVA, a CBS affiliate in Alaska, covering crime and courts, including marijuana-related stories. Her passion for marijuana advocacy grew after meeting activists in Colorado and Washington, the first states to legalize recreational marijuana, and learning about its medicinal benefits. In 2014, Greene revealed her ownership of the Alaska Cannabis Club on live television, using a profanity-laced statement that made headlines worldwide. However, instead of supporting her cause, many people seem to have forgotten about her plight.

Despite Alaska's voter-approved initiative to legalize marijuana, the state failed to establish clear regulations for the industry. As a result, Greene's club operated under a unique business model, offering "memberships" in exchange for donations, which allowed customers to receive marijuana. This approach drew the attention of authorities, who conducted multiple undercover operations and raided the club twice, resulting in Greene being charged with 14 felony and misdemeanor offenses. Prosecutors argue that the club and Greene are one entity, citing the sole proprietorship status of the business.

Court records show that Greene was not directly involved in any of the transactions that led to the raids, but she is still facing severe penalties, potentially spending decades in prison. Critics argue that the charges are excessive and that Greene should have received a fine or citation, similar to those issued for selling alcohol without a license. Experts believe it's unlikely Greene will serve the maximum sentence, but the uncertainty weighs heavily on her mind.

Greene's case has drawn comparisons to a "modern-day lynching," highlighting the disproportionate impact of the war on drugs on communities of color. The raids, carried out by the Anchorage Police Department, have also been criticized, with allegations of misconduct and abuse of power. Greene's plea for support from the cannabis community has fallen on deaf ears, leaving her feeling isolated and uncertain about her future.

As Alaska continues to navigate the complexities of regulating its marijuana industry, Greene's case serves as a stark reminder of the challenges faced by entrepreneurs and advocates in the space. With her trial looming, Greene remains hopeful that the cannabis community will rally behind her, providing the support she needs to overcome this difficult chapter in her life.

reference: – Charlo Greene made headlines in 2014 when the then 26-year-old memorably quit her TV reporting gig on air while announcing her intention to push for the legalization of marijuana in Alaska: She said, "F--- it, I quit," before walking out of view. But as the Guardian reports, her ensuing off-screen plight has been largely ignored, even though she faces more than a half-century in prison. That's because Greene, whose legal name is Charlene Egbe, isn't just a cannabis advocate but the owner of the Alaska Cannabis Club, which she created on April 20, 2014, a full six months before Alaska voted to legalize the adult use of cannabis. In other words, she was receiving "donations" for marijuana through club "memberships" before it was legal to do so. Police raided the club twice and detectives made several undercover purchases, and though Greene wasn't directly involved in them, state prosecutors are charging her because the club is registered under her name. She's pleaded not guilty to charges of "misconduct involving a controlled substance," and the original indictment listed eight offenses that amounted to a possible 24 years in jail. The Guardian broke the news to Greene that six offenses have since been added, raising the total to 54 years. In the Weed Blog, Greene calls this a "modern-day lynching," and tells High Times that she hopes the cannabis community rallies behind her. "I need help more than ever," she says. "Now that I could lose the rest of my life because of cannabis, it feels like the people I fought for have abandoned me." (Alaskans can't smoke weed in public.)

TEHRAN | TEHRAN (Reuters) - Iranian authorities have delayed the trial of two Americans arrested near the Iraqi border in July 2009, a judiciary spokesman said on Monday, less than a week before they were due their first day in court. 

Shane Bauer and Josh Fattal, who say they were hiking in northern Iraq and were not aware they had crossed into Iran, are at the center of a much bigger dispute between the United States and Tehran, which have been bitter foes since the 1979 Islamic Revolution. 

Iran's judiciary spokesman said the trial could not go ahead as scheduled because of the absence of a third suspect, Sarah Shourd, who was arrested with the men but released on $500,000 bail in September this year and returned to the United States. 

"Due to the absence of the third person, the court has delayed the trial time so that in between this time the third person can return to Iran or, in the event of her refusal to appear, her case be dealt with differently," Qolamhossein Mohseni-Ejei was quoted as saying by the semi-official Fars news agency. 

Their lawyer said he was not aware the trial had been postponed. "It is illogical. If they want to delay the trial, they should announce it in a notification to me or to Sarah Shourd, but they didn't," Masoud Shafiee told Reuters. "So the trial should be held on Saturday then the judge will decide whether the trial will be delayed or not."

Shourd was released shortly before President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad attended the U.N. General Assembly in New York. He said letting her leave jail was a humanitarian gesture due to concerns about her health. She denied the spying charge. 

The trial delay comes as Washington and other powers are trying to prod Tehran to return to talks this month about its nuclear activities which they fear are aimed at making an atomic bomb. 

Iran, which says its nuclear ambitions are peaceful, has said it is ready to restart the talks which stalled last year, but has yet to agree a date and has even suggested the talks should not address the nuclear issue. 

Ahmadinejad suggested in February that the Americans might be released as part of a prisoner swap for Iranians he says have been abducted or tricked into going to the United States and jailed without due legal process. 

One of those Iranians, a woman jailed for trafficking defense hardware to Iran, gave a telephone interview to Iran's state-run English language television channel on Monday in which she said she had been tortured. 

"They took me to the prison and they started to torture me in every possible way, more especially, you know, mentally," Shahrazad Mir Gholikhan told Press TV. She did not give any further details of her ill-treatment. 

Foreign Ministry spokesman Ramin Mehmanparast said he hoped publicity about the case would help secure release of Iranian prisoners in U.S. jails. "Iran will pursue all channels regarding the matter," he was quoted by Fars news agency as saying. He did not mention the possibility of a prisoner swap. 

Besides stating that the three hikers were captured in Iraq, the American military report, by an anonymous official, also said, “The lack of coordination on the part of these hikers, particularly after being forewarned, indicates an intent to agitate and create publicity regarding international policies on Iran.” 

Ms. Shourd said that she was mystified by that conclusion. The three had no idea they were near the border and had not been warned about anything, she said. “Those claims are illogical and unsubstantiated. It is ridiculous to claim that mountain climbers would be agitating along a border.” 

The United States State Department has never suggested the version published by WikiLeaks, she said, always maintaining that it did not know how their arrest happened. 

The State Department spokesman, Philip J. Crowley, confirmed that on Sunday. “We don’t know whether they had two feet on one side or the other or one foot on each,” he said. “All we know is Iran has held them far too long.” 

Ms. Shourd described what began as a relaxed overnight camping trip, undertaken by three reunited friends from Berkeley, happy to escape to the fresh, green Kurdish mountains from the sweltering Syrian plains. 

She had been teaching English in Damascus, Syria, where Mr. Bauer was working as a freelance journalist while both studied Arabic. Mr. Fattal came to visit, and they set off to Kurdistan after reading on a Web site that it was safe and listening to a friend rave about the place. 

Various Kurds suggested they visit Ahmed Awa, a spectacular mountain waterfall where local people camp overnight. The hikers had no idea it abutted Iran, Ms. Shourd said, and twice encountered Kurdish pesh merga soldiers who greeted them warmly. The music and laughter around scores of campfires at the waterfall gave no sense of imminent danger. 

The next day, they

In July 2009, Shane Bauer, Josh Fattal, and Sarah Shourd embarked on a hiking trip in northern Iraq, unaware that they had inadvertently crossed into neighboring Iran. The trio, all friends from Berkeley, California, had planned a relaxing camping trip to the Kurdish mountains, seeking refuge from the scorching heat of the Syrian plains. They had been warned about potential dangers in the area, but none of them anticipated crossing the border.

Their adventure turned into a nightmare when they were apprehended by Iranian authorities. According to Shourd, the group had no intention of creating any controversy or agitation; they simply wanted to enjoy the scenic beauty of the mountains. However, Iranian officials accused them of espionage, claiming that their actions indicated an intent to provoke and create publicity about international policies on Iran.

The incident sparked a diplomatic row between Iran and the United States, with the latter expressing concern over the prolonged detention of the three Americans. In September 2009, Shourd was released on $500,000 bail, returning to the United States, leaving Bauer and Fattal behind in Evin prison. 

As the trial date approached, Iranian authorities announced a delay, citing the absence of Shourd as the reason. Judiciary Spokesman Qolamhossein Mohseni-Ejei stated that the trial would be rescheduled once Shourd either returns to Iran or her case is handled separately. However, Shourd's lawyer, Masoud Shafiee, expressed surprise at the announcement, claiming that he had not received any notification about the postponement.

The delay coincided with efforts by the international community to persuade Iran to resume talks about its nuclear program, which the US and other nations believe is aimed at developing an atomic bomb. Iran maintains that its nuclear ambitions are peaceful. Meanwhile, Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad hinted at a possible prisoner swap, suggesting that the release of the three Americans could be part of a deal to free Iranians detained in the US.

On May 20, 2010, a photo opportunity showed the families of the detained Americans, including Shourd, meeting with her mother in Tehran. The incident highlighted the ongoing tensions between Iran and the West, with both sides engaging in a war of words over the fate of the three hikers. As the situation remained unresolved, the US continued to express its concern over the prolonged detention of Bauer and Fattal, with State Department Spokesman Philip J. Crowley stating that the US did not know how the three Americans had crossed the border and that they had been held for too long.

reference: – The three American hikers detained by Iran weren’t captured in Iraq, says Sarah Shourd: They were beckoned over the border by an armed soldier, she tells the New York Times in an effort to set the WikiLeaks version of the story straight. “We did not actually enter Iran until he gestured to us. We were confused and worried and wanted to go back." In Shourd's fullest account of her ordeal to date, she goes into detail about their fateful overnight camping trip, describing "scores of campfires" near the waterfall they were hiking to—which abutted Iran. “I think we were extremely unlucky,” says Shourd. “I guess I never believed there would be so many hundreds of people close to a border.” Her interview with the Times comes ahead of a court date for her fellow hikers, which was set for Saturday but has been delayed by Iran until Shourd “can return to Iran or,” if she refuses, her case can “be dealt with differently,” said an Iranian official. But her lawyer says the trial should go forward; neither he nor Shourd, he tells Reuters, were notified of the change.

final_summary:
In the film adaptation of Elizabeth Gilbert's bestselling memoir "Eat Pray Love", Julia Roberts stars as Liz Gilbert, a successful New York writer struggling to find meaning in her life after a painful divorce and an unsuccessful affair. Seeking spiritual enlightenment, Liz embarks on a journey to Italy, India, and Indonesia, where she indulges in the local culture, cuisine, and spirituality. The film, directed by Ryan Murphy and written by Murphy and Jennifer Salt, follows Liz's transformative experiences in each destination, as she learns valuable lessons about embracing life, forgiveness, and love.

In Italy, Liz spends several months enjoying the local food and lifestyle, learning to let go of her fears and live in the moment. She is mentored by a Scandinavian woman and a handsome Italian language coach, who teach her to appreciate the simple pleasures of life. In India, Liz visits an ashram, where she meets a Texan named Richard Jenkins, who shares his struggles with self-forgiveness and helps Liz to confront her own emotional baggage. Meanwhile, in Bali, Indonesia, Liz meets Felipe, a charming Brazilian man who becomes her love interest and changes her original plan to focus solely on finding balance in her life.

Throughout the film, Julia Roberts shines as Liz Gilbert, bringing warmth and authenticity to the role. However, despite her efforts, the movie fails to capture the essence of Gilbert's writing style, which relies heavily on private, silent moments between the author and her reader. The film's script often feels like a tourist brochure, with too much emphasis on picturesque settings and not enough depth in the characters' emotions and relationships.

One of the few standout performances in the film is Richard Jenkins, who brings a sense of authenticity and vulnerability to his portrayal of a fellow meditation student at the Indian ashram. His chemistry with Julia Roberts is palpable, and their scenes together are some of the most memorable in the film. Unfortunately, the rest of the supporting cast, including Billy Crudup as Liz's ex-husband and James Franco as her brief love interest, feel more like caricatures than fully fleshed-out characters.

Despite its flaws, the film remains visually stunning, with breathtaking landscapes and vibrant colors capturing the essence of each destination. The cinematography, costume design, and editing all contribute to a dreamy, escapist quality that makes the film easy to watch. However, beneath its surface-level charm, the movie lacks substance, relying too heavily on familiar tropes and clichés about self-discovery and spiritual growth.

Ultimately, "Eat Pray Love" is a film that promises more than it delivers, relying on Julia Roberts' charisma and the picturesque settings to carry the story forward. While it may appeal to fans of romantic comedies and travel films, viewers seeking a deeper exploration of the human experience may find themselves disappointed.

reference: – Eat, Pray, Love looks lovely but lacks both the spice and the depth of the best-selling memoir it's based on, say critics. Julia Roberts stars as a jaded New York divorcee traveling the world to seek enlightenment. Roberts' Liz says she wants to move outside her comfort zone, writes Kirk Honeycutt at the Hollywood Reporter, but as she journeys through Italy, India, and Indonesia, "the film never ventures, even once, into a situation that does not reek of comfy familiarity." The movie "is shameless wish-fulfillment, a Harlequin novel crossed with a mystic travelogue," complains Roger Ebert at the Chicago Sun-Times, though he notes that the overwhelmingly female audience he watched it with appeared quite moved. Roberts manages to make the "2 hours and 15 minutes of eating, praying and loving pleasant enough," writes Roger Moore at the Orlando Sentinel, who decides Eat, Pray, Love "isn’t a bad movie, just a spiritually dead one." Roberts is a great choice to play Liz but "her warmth, coupled with Javier Bardem's scruffy sexiness" as love interest Felipe, aren't enough to compensate for "the folded-map flatness of this production," writes Lisa Schwarzbaum at Entertainment Weekly.

final_summary:
In Denver, on Tuesday, Tom Clements, the executive director of the Colorado Department of Corrections, was shot and killed at his home in Monument, a suburb north of Colorado Springs. The incident sparked a massive manhunt for the perpetrator, with authorities scouring the area for clues and witnesses. According to Lieutenant Jeff Kramer of the El Paso County Sheriff's Office, investigators have not ruled out any possible motives, including the possibility that the attack was linked to Clements' position as the state's top corrections official.

Clements, who had served as the head of the DOC since January 2011, was a prominent figure in Colorado's correctional system, overseeing both state and private prisons, as well as parole operations. His killing has raised concerns about the safety of high-ranking officials in the state government. The FBI and the Colorado Bureau of Investigation have joined forces with local authorities to investigate the case.

The shooting occurred just after Clements denied a request by Homaidan al-Turki, a Saudi national serving a lengthy prison sentence for sex crimes, to be transferred back to his home country to complete his sentence. Al-Turki, who claimed his conviction was politically motivated, had previously owned a company that sold CDs featuring sermons by Anwar al-Awlaki, a radical cleric killed in a US drone strike in 2011.

On Wednesday, Colorado Governor John Hickenlooper announced plans to sign sweeping new gun control laws, despite initial reservations about the measure. The legislation, which includes expanded background checks and limits on ammunition clips, marks a significant shift for the state, which had previously relaxed its gun laws. Hickenlooper, a moderate Democrat, had faced intense pressure from gun rights groups and opponents, but ultimately decided to support the measures after conducting extensive research and consulting with experts.

The governor's decision reflects a broader trend in Colorado, where voters have increasingly favored stricter gun regulations. A recent survey found that 80 percent of Coloradans agree with expanded background checks, and 61 percent back limits on ammunition clips. However, the issue remains contentious, with many gun owners and dealers opposing the new laws. As Hickenlooper prepares for his re-election bid next year, he faces criticism from Republican opponents who vow to challenge his stance on gun control.

In a bizarre coincidence, the Internet Archive, a digital preservation project, had crawled the web and archived pages related to Clements' killing, ensuring that the original content would be preserved even if the original links became broken. The archive serves as a testament to the importance of preserving online information and providing access to historical records.

reference: – Tom Clements, the executive director of Colorado’s department of corrections, was shot dead last night—but the who and why remain a mystery. Clements was shot fatally in the chest as he answered the door of his home in Monument, Colo., reports NBC, but nobody else in the house was harmed. Although there's no clear connection, the timing is notable: the shooting comes on the eve of tough and controversial new gun laws in the state, following the movie theater massacre in Aurora last year. Colorado Gov. John Hickenlooper is expected to sign new legislation today that will restrict magazine capacity and expand background checks on private and online firearms sales. But his first duty this morning was instead to pen a letter to Department of Corrections employees. "I can hardly believe it, let alone write words to describe it," wrote Hickenlooper. "I am so sad. I have never worked with a better person than Tom, and I can't imagine our team without him."

final_summary:
Over the last few decades, the rise of rotisserie chickens offered by grocery stores has caught the attention of many consumers, often priced lower than buying and cooking a whole chicken themselves. Investigative research by Cat Vasko revealed that these affordable options are made possible by grocery stores utilizing unsold chicken nearing its expiration date, thereby minimizing losses and maximizing profits. This practice is not limited to chicken; stores also employ similar strategies to reduce waste across various departments, including produce and meat sections. By repurposing near-expired products, stores can maintain a wide range of fresh offerings without incurring significant costs.

The economics behind this approach is rooted in the concept of reducing spoilage and optimizing inventory management. Stores like Ralphs, Gelson's, and Whole Foods utilize various methods to minimize waste, such as chopping up expired produce for salad bars or cooking unsold meat for hot counters. According to a vendor of commercial rotisserie ovens, their equipment is designed to aid in minimizing food shrinkage and waste, further underscoring the importance of this strategy.

A couple of years ago, I got it into my head that I wanted to roast a whole chicken, just because. However, after wandering around my local Ralphs for a few minutes looking for poultry that hadn't already been turned into individually shrink-wrapped meat units, I realized that the store had already done the legwork for me – offering rotisserie chickens at the front of the store. I ended up buying the raw chicken anyway, but it turned out that it wasn't notably better than a store-bought rotisserie chicken, and with the other ingredients factored in, it cost significantly more. At Ralphs, an uncooked chicken currently costs $9.87, whereas a rotisserie chicken is priced at $6.99; at Gelson's, a cooked chicken costs $8.99, while a whole chicken from the butcher counter is $12.67 per pound; and at Whole Foods, a rotisserie chicken is $8.99, while a whole chicken from the butcher counter is $12.79 per pound.

Interestingly, the notion that prepared meals are always more expensive than cooking from scratch is not entirely accurate. While this may hold true for some instances, the case of rotisserie chickens serves as an exception. Furthermore, the widespread adoption of this practice highlights the innovative approaches employed by grocery stores to stay competitive and meet customer demands.

This is the sort of thing that no one talks about when they talk about innovation – the likes of Facebook and Google – and yet, it's a major way in which our economy has become more efficient over the last few decades. Reducing spoilage means grocery stores can sell us raw chickens at lower prices – and that we can get fresh, delicious prepared food at even lower prices. It's a win for the grocer and the consumer.

It's worth noting that sell-by, use-by, and best-by dates were never intended as indicators of food safety, but rather as estimates of food quality. The USDA itself says that food product dating is intended to "help the purchaser to know the time limit to purchase or use the product at its best quality. It is not a safety date." Further, it's pretty well-documented that these estimates are no substitute for boring old human discretion. Sites like StillTasty.com aim to help consumers get the most out of their groceries by educating them on the real shelf lives of thousands of foods as well as ways to ascertain quality that have nothing to do with the numbers stamped on the package.

In fact, in spite of their creative uses of items that have passed their sell-by dates, grocery stores are still being conservative enough when it comes to food safety to waste plenty of usable meat and produce – around $900 million in inventory annually, according to a 2001 study. And as the Harvard report points out, major retailers aren't generally wont to take a loss, meaning their waste "ultimately could be a cost born by consumers in the price of goods."

So not only do you have nothing to fear from that grocery store rotisserie chicken, you could actually be doing a triple good deed by purchasing it – making your life easier, keeping prices down for your fellow shoppers, and helping the environment. This column does not necessarily reflect the opinion of Bloomberg View's editorial board or Bloomberg LP, its owners and investors.

reference: – Those who like chicken might have noticed a seemingly weird quirk in the grocery store: It's generally cheaper to buy a fully cooked rotisserie chicken than to buy a raw chicken and prepare it yourself at home, writes Megan McArdle at Bloomberg View. Cooking food yourself is typically cheaper, so why the exception here? McArdle cites a post at KCET that explains what's going on: Supermarkets use chickens that are about to hit their sell-by expiration date for those rotisseries. In fact, they do it not just with chicken but with many of the offerings in the prepared-food department. Underhanded? No, writes McArdle. It's actually kind of brilliant. Instead of taking a loss on the still-fine food, the stores cook it up and serve it hot. "This is the sort of thing that no one talks about when they talk about innovation—and yet, it’s a major way in which our economy has become more efficient over the last few decades," she writes. The practice benefits both the store and the consumer, and it reduces spoilage to boot. Click for her full post.

final_summary:
On Wednesday evening, a bizarre incident unfolded in Mendon, leaving a Toyota Corolla suspended precariously from a telephone guy wire. According to eyewitness accounts and official statements from State Police, the driver, identified as Nabila Altahan, reacted impulsively to a GPS navigation system's instruction to turn around. The GPS device had apparently directed Altahan to make a U-turn after she had already passed her intended destination.

As a result, Altahan veered off the westbound lane of Route 4 in Mendon, causing her vehicle to become lodged in the supporting wire of a telephone pole situated directly opposite the Sugar & Spice Restaurant. Fortunately, neither Altahan nor her passenger sustained injuries during the incident.

Ray Ault, a passing motorist, witnessed the aftermath of the accident and reported seeing Altahan exit the vehicle as it remained suspended high above the ground. State Police promptly responded to the scene, ensuring that the situation was brought under control without any further complications.

reference: – Police in Vermont say a car ended up almost vertical when the driver swerved quickly in response to her GPS ordering her to "turn around." The car was suspended almost vertically on guide wires attached to a utility pole (see a photo here) in Mendon on Wednesday night, reports the AP. Police say 30-year-old Nabila Altahan of Dorchester, Mass., was headed west on US Route 4 when she passed her intended destination and reacted quickly to the instructions, leaving the road at a significant enough speed to propel the vehicle up the wires. She wasn't injured. (This driver followed her GPS into a lake.)

final_summary:
On Friday, March 10, 2017, an incident occurred at Brookland-Cayce High School involving a substitute teacher who allegedly appeared to be under the influence of alcohol while working at the school. According to the Lexington County Sheriff's Department, the individual, identified as Judith Elizabeth Richards-Gartee, 52, of Irmo, was arrested after an administrator at the school contacted a resource officer due to concerns over her erratic behavior. 

Richards-Gartee, who worked through Kelly Services, a company contracted by Lexington School District Two to provide substitute teachers, was removed from the classroom and subsequently found in possession of an open container of alcohol. She was charged with disorderly conduct and released into the care of paramedics, who then transported her to the hospital.

In response to the incident, the Administration of Brookland-Cayce School promptly notified the authorities and cooperated fully with their investigation. On Monday, the school district issued a statement confirming that Richards-Gartee was not a district employee, but rather a contractor working through Kelly Services. The district emphasized its commitment to maintaining a safe learning environment for students and staff.

The Lexington County Sheriff's Department has since stated that no further information regarding the incident will be released. Meanwhile, efforts continue to preserve online content and prevent broken links through initiatives such as those undertaken by the Internet Archive, which aims to maintain a record of web pages as they exist at a particular point in time, allowing users to access historical versions of websites even if original links become outdated or deleted.

reference: – A substitute teacher was found throwing up at South Carolina's Brookland-Cayce High School, but a stomach bug wasn't to blame. Judith Richards-Gartee has been charged with disorderly conduct after allegedly drinking boxed wine in her classroom. Administrators say they found the 52-year-old vomiting and incapable of standing when they came to her classroom at 9:45am Friday, reports the State. She was removed via wheelchair, and a Lexington County deputy who was on the clock as the school resource officer says he found the boxed wine in her purse. Students reported that she was consuming it in class. WIS reports that in a statement Lexington School District 2 made clear that Richards-Gartee was not its employee, but had been hired by the company the district contracts with. (These students in Arizona can probably relate.)

final_summary:
In Adelaide, Australia, a unique coffee shop called Viscous Cafe has gained attention for its extremely potent coffee drink, dubbed the "Ass Kicker." According to its creator, Steve Benington, the Ass Kicker contains the caffeine equivalent of 80 standard cups of coffee, making it a beverage not for the faint of heart. Initially crafted for an emergency department nurse who required a stimulating drink to stay awake during long shifts, the original version of the Ass Kicker proved so effective that it kept the nurse alert for three days after being consumed over a two-day period.

Benington, the owner of Viscous Cafe, has since toned down the recipe for public consumption, but still warns customers about the potential risks associated with the drink. He advises patrons to sip the Ass Kicker over a three-to-four-hour period to avoid adverse reactions. In an interview with Mashable, Benington mentioned that the nurse sipped the drink over two days, keeping her awake for three days in total. Despite its potency, the drink has gained popularity among locals, with Benington taking care to educate customers about its intense caffeine content before serving it to them.

The Ass Kicker consists of four espresso shots, two types of cold-drip coffee, and milk, resulting in a flavor profile described as similar to toffee rather than traditional coffee. With a price tag of around $12 for a 16-ounce serving, the drink is marketed as a unique experience for adventurous coffee enthusiasts. 

According to Maggie Sweeney, a researcher at Johns Hopkins University, the 5 grams of caffeine in the Ass Kicker is a substantial amount, potentially causing negative side effects in individuals sensitive to the stimulant. She estimates that a lethal dose of caffeine could be as low as 10 grams, double the amount found in the Ass Kicker. Sweeney's research focuses on caffeine dependency and its impact on daily life, highlighting the varying levels of tolerance among individuals.

A CNN article highlighted the issue with the in-app browser on Facebook failing to recognize previously set cookies, leading users to encounter difficulties when accessing certain websites. To resolve this issue, users can opt to open links externally using their device's default browser. 

As for the global consumption of caffeine, a 2012 FDA report states that the average adult consumes approximately 300 milligrams of the stimulant per day. While excessive caffeine intake can lead to adverse health effects, moderate consumption has been linked to several health benefits, including improved cognitive function and a lower risk of certain diseases.

reference: – One cafe owner in Adelaide, Australia, is playing around with the world's most popular drug, and experts aren't feeling terribly perky about it. Steve Benington, owner of Viscous Coffee, has created the Asskicker, a drink consisting of four shots of espresso, four 48-hour brewer cold drip ice cubes (each containing a bit more caffeine than is found in two shots of espresso), 120ml of 10-day brewed cold drip, and another four of those cold drip ice cubes to finish it off, reports Adelaide Now. All told, it has 5 grams of caffeine, about 80 times as a typical espresso, and it provides 18 hours of "sustained up time," as Benington puts it. "Some people love it and some are broken by it, but it’s all in the name of fun," he says. Benington developed the drink after an emergency room nurse asked for something that would get her through a long shift. It comes with a warning for people with heart or blood pressure issues, and he has "quite a detailed talk" with anyone who orders it because "if I can talk them out of it, they're not ready," he tells CNN. One area dietitian is not amused. "Caffeine is a drug, not a game or a toy," she says. The author of a book espousing the benefits of caffeine also chimes in that this drink goes too far. "I would be very wary of consuming it." Mashable notes that Benington does suggest people sip the drink over three to four hours to "lift you up and keep you there." It also notes that a fatal dose of caffeine is generally considered to be 18 grams over one or two hours. (There is such a thing as fatal caffeine intoxication.)

final_summary:
On Wednesday, Republican candidate Richard Mourdock found himself at the center of controversy after making a remark that sparked outrage across the nation during a televised debate in Indiana. During the debate, Mourdock stated that pregnancies resulting from rape were "something God intended." This comment drew widespread criticism, prompting calls for him to apologize.

Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.) addressed the issue on CNN, saying his support for Mourdock depended on whether the Indiana Senate candidate would apologize for his comments. McCain emphasized the importance of owning up to mistakes and seeking forgiveness when necessary. However, in a subsequent clarification, McCain's office stated that he still supported Mourdock, citing the Indiana Senate candidate's apology for any perceived misunderstanding of his original statement.

President Barack Obama weighed in on the issue, criticizing Mourdock's stance and emphasizing that "rape is rape" and should not be subject to interpretation. He further highlighted the importance of women being able to make their own healthcare decisions, free from interference by politicians. In a separate statement, Obama noted that the outcome of the presidential election could impact the fate of Roe v. Wade, a landmark Supreme Court decision on abortion rights.

Mitt Romney attempted to distance himself from Mourdock's comments, stating through his spokesperson Andrea Saul that he disagreed with the Indiana Senate candidate's views on exceptions for rape and incest. However, Romney's campaign chose not to withdraw its support for Mourdock, despite growing pressure from Democrats to do so. The Obama campaign responded by releasing a web video highlighting the disconnect between Romney's endorsement of Mourdock and the Indiana Senate candidate's extreme views on reproductive rights. The video aimed to portray Romney as a staunchly conservative candidate who shares Mourdock's views on women's health issues.

In the midst of a heated US Senate election in Indiana, Republican candidate Richard Mourdock found himself at the center of controversy after making a remark that sparked outrage across the nation. During a televised debate on Tuesday, Mourdock stated that pregnancies resulting from rape were "something God intended." This comment drew widespread criticism, prompting calls for him to apologize.

President Barack Obama weighed in on the issue, criticizing Mourdock's stance and emphasizing that "rape is rape" and should not be subject to interpretation. He further highlighted the importance of women being able to make their own healthcare decisions, free from interference by politicians. In a separate statement, Obama noted that the outcome of the presidential election could impact the fate of Roe v. Wade, a landmark Supreme Court decision on abortion rights.

Meanwhile, Republican presidential candidate Mitt Romney attempted to distance himself from Mourdock's comments, stating through his spokesperson Andrea Saul that he disagreed with the Indiana Senate candidate's views on exceptions for rape and incest. However, Romney's campaign chose not to withdraw its support for Mourdock, despite growing pressure from Democrats to do so.

Arizona Senator John McCain initially expressed reservations about continuing to support Mourdock, suggesting that he would reconsider his backing if the Indiana candidate issued an apology. McCain emphasized the importance of owning up to mistakes and seeking forgiveness when necessary. However, in a subsequent clarification, McCain's office stated that he still supported Mourdock, citing the Indiana Senate candidate's apology for any perceived misunderstanding of his original statement.

As the controversy surrounding Mourdock's comments intensified, the Obama campaign released a web video highlighting the disconnect between Romney's endorsement of Mourdock and the Indiana Senate candidate's extreme views on reproductive rights. The video aimed to portray Romney as a staunchly conservative candidate who shares Mourdock's views on women's health issues. history.draft:
On Wednesday, Republican candidate Richard Mourdock found himself at the center of controversy after making a remark that sparked outrage across the nation during a televised debate in Indiana. During the debate, Mourdock stated that pregnancies resulting from rape were "something God intended." This comment drew widespread criticism, prompting calls for him to apologize.

Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.) addressed the issue on CNN, saying his support for Mourdock depended on whether the Indiana Senate candidate would apologize for his comments. McCain emphasized the importance of owning up to mistakes and seeking forgiveness when necessary. However, in a subsequent clarification, McCain's office stated that he still supported Mourdock, citing the Indiana Senate candidate's apology for any perceived misunderstanding of his original statement.

President Barack Obama weighed in on the issue, criticizing Mourdock's stance and emphasizing that "rape is rape" and should not be subject to interpretation. He further highlighted the importance of women being able to make their own healthcare decisions, free from interference by politicians. In a separate statement, Obama noted that the outcome of the presidential election could impact the fate of Roe v. Wade, a landmark Supreme Court decision on abortion rights.

Mitt Romney attempted to distance himself from Mourdock's comments, stating through his spokesperson Andrea Saul that he disagreed with the Indiana Senate candidate's views on exceptions for rape and incest. However, Romney's campaign chose not to withdraw its support for Mourdock, despite growing pressure from Democrats to do so. The Obama campaign responded by releasing a web video highlighting the disconnect between Romney's endorsement of Mourdock and the Indiana Senate candidate's extreme views on reproductive rights. The video aimed to portray Romney as a staunchly conservative candidate who shares Mourdock's views on women's health issues.

reference: – President Obama slammed Senate candidate Richard Mourdock's rape comments on the Tonight Show last night. "I don't know how these guys come up with these ideas," Obama said. "Let me make a very simple proposition. Rape is rape. It is a crime." He noted "Roe vs. Wade is probably hanging in the balance" of the election, Politico reports. Mourdock's comments that pregnancies from rape are "something God intended" are "exactly why you don't want a bunch of politicians, mostly male, making decisions about women's health care decisions," Obama said. "Women are capable of making these decisions in consultation with their partners, with their doctors." Meanwhile, asked by Anderson Cooper whether he still supports Mourdock, John McCain said it "depends on what he does," the Huffington Post reports. "If he apologizes and says he misspoke and he was wrong and he asks the people to forgive him, then obviously I’d be the first" to support him. Mourdock has in fact issued an apology. Mitt Romney, for his part, still supports Mourdock, though his campaign says he "disagrees" with the Indiana Republican's comments: "We disagree on the policy regarding exceptions for rape and incest but still support him." The campaign says it hasn't called on Mourdock to withdraw ads featuring Romney.

final_summary:
Today, Don't count Jay Z out of the streaming wars just yet. Two years ago, the rap star and entrepreneur purchased Tidal, a small streaming service, for $56 million with ambitious plans to create an online music empire offering exclusive content from fellow celebrities. However, the service faced intense scrutiny and mounting losses, leading many in the music industry to speculate about its demise or potential takeover by a major player such as Apple. Despite these doubts, Jay Z secured a crucial lifeline through a deal announced on January 23, where Sprint agreed to acquire a 33 percent stake in Tidal for an undisclosed sum. This partnership provides much-needed investment for Tidal and enables it to compete against dominant players like Spotify and Amazon in the streaming market. According to Mark Mulligan, an analyst at Midia Research, the deal grants Tidal access to Sprint's vast customer base, free marketing opportunities, and a substantial war chest to challenge established streaming services.

As part of this agreement, Tidal will become accessible to Sprint's 45 million retail customers, and both companies will collaborate on exclusive content from Tidal's artist-owners. Sprint CEO Marcelo Claure will join Tidal's board of directors, expressing enthusiasm for the partnership, citing Jay Z's dedication to creating a high-quality music streaming platform. The collaboration also involves establishing a dedicated marketing fund with an annual budget of $75 million to support artist initiatives and exclusives. Existing Tidal subscribers, estimated to be around one million people, will not see changes to their current service. Jay Z views the partnership as a means to revolutionize the creative industry, allowing artists to connect directly with their fans and achieve their full potential. With this deal, Tidal appears poised to continue competing in the streaming market, despite initial concerns about its viability.

reference: – It looks like a good day for Jay-Z: Sprint is buying a 33% stake in his music-streaming service, Tidal. Billboard reports the deal was for $200 million. Under the terms, Jay-Z and other big-name celebs—including Beyonce, Madonna, and Kanye West—will remain part-owners. Sprint, meanwhile, will be able to provide exclusive music to its customers. The deal may be a life-saver for Tidal, which has been struggling to compete with Apple Music, Pandora, and Spotify, reports the New York Times. Its holding company lost about $28 million in 2015. (Jay-Z maintains that having artists own the site is what sets Tidal apart.)

final_summary:
Tensions continue to escalate as the international community grapples with the aftermath of the alleged chemical attack in Syria. In the Mediterranean, the regional commander of the Navy's fleet postponed a scheduled port call in Naples, Italy, for one of its destroyers, opting instead to keep the vessel in close proximity to another destroyer carrying Tomahawk cruise missiles. According to Pentagon officials, this decision was made without explicit instructions from Washington, but it suggests a heightened state of readiness in case the US decides to launch military action against Syria.

The United Nations has called for access to the site of the alleged attack, located east of Damascus, to investigate the incident. However, diplomatic sources indicate that Russia's move does not signify a shift in its stance on supporting President Bashar al-Assad or opposing punitive measures in the UN.

Russia's foreign ministry has placed the blame squarely on the shoulders of Syrian opposition forces, claiming they failed to provide secure access to the site of the alleged attack. In a statement, the ministry also suggested that the attack might have been a provocation orchestrated by the rebels themselves, pointing to online criticism of government troops posted prior to the incident. Aleksandr K. Lukashevich, a spokesperson for the foreign ministry, stated that "more and more evidence" points to the attack being a pre-planned operation.

However, Lukashevich appears to have been misled by YouTube's time-stamping policy, which takes into account the time in California rather than the local time zone. Given that the attack occurred early Wednesday in Syria, it would have been Tuesday in California for several hours, potentially casting doubt on the timing of the online posts cited by the Russians.

Lukashevich went on to praise the Assad government for welcoming Carla del Ponte, a member of a United Nations commission on Syria who suggested in May that the rebels had used chemical weapons, while accusing the opposition of obstructing the UN investigation. The Syrian government remained silent on the matter.

Separately, CBS News reported that US intelligence agencies detected unusual activity at known chemical weapons sites in Syria ahead of the alleged attack, which some officials believe could have been preparations for the assault. As the situation unfolds, the world waits with bated breath for further developments in this complex and volatile conflict.

reference: – A government backed by Russia is accused of killing its own civilians. That applies not only to the current situation in Syria but to the 1999 situation in Kosovo. And as the New York Times reports, top US officials are keeping Kosovo very much in mind as they weigh their options. President Obama would likely never get the approval of the UN Security Council for a military response because of Russia's veto power. When Bill Clinton was in the same boat in 1999, he turned to NATO and got its blessing for 78 days of airstrikes. “It’s a step too far to say we’re drawing up legal justifications for an action, given that the president hasn’t made a decision,” a government official tells the Times. “But Kosovo, of course, is a precedent of something that is perhaps similar.” Obama was meeting today with national security aides to determine next steps if the US concludes that Bashar al-Assad used chemical weapons. (Assad in turn accused the rebels of doing the same today.) Meanwhile, the US Navy is keeping two destroyers positioned in the Mediterranean in case the green light is given for a cruise-missile attack, reports the Wall Street Journal.

final_summary:
In a shocking stunt aimed at sparking debate about the treatment of animals in Denmark's agriculture sector, Radio24syv, a Danish radio station, killed a baby rabbit named Allan on-air. The rabbit was struck multiple times with a bicycle pump by Asger Juhl, one of the station's hosts, before being skinned and eaten by Juhl and his family, including his children, along with fellow host Kristoffer Eriksen. According to Juhl, the stunt was meant to highlight the hypocrisy of individuals who claim to care about animal welfare yet consume meat, as demonstrated by their actions yesterday, every day, when purchasing and eating meat without directly experiencing the consequences of animal slaughter.

The idea behind the stunt was inspired by a conversation with a zookeeper from Aalborg Zoo, who revealed that he regularly kills baby rabbits using an iron stick to feed the snakes at the zoo. Juhl used a bicycle pump made of iron, similar to the stick, to kill Allan, claiming it was the most humane method available to him. However, critics argue that bludgeoning an animal to death is far from humane.

Linse Kessler, a reality star and outspoken animal rights advocate, appeared on the program but objected to the killing of Allan, attempting to intervene and even chasing Juhl around the studio. Despite her concerns, the hosts proceeded with the stunt, which sparked widespread outrage and calls for a boycott of the radio station. As reported by TV 2, the incident drew significant attention from the public and media outlets.

Asger Juhl defended the decision, stating that the stunt was necessary to expose the harsh realities of the meat industry, where millions of animals are raised and slaughtered daily under often inhumane conditions. He pointed out that Danes allow chicken farms to keep 13 chickens per square meter and tolerate the transportation of animals to slaughterhouses, resulting in significant animal suffering. Speaking to Newsbeat, Juhl acknowledged that he and his family ate the rabbit after the stunt, describing it as a "nice" meal. He also shared details of skinning the rabbit in his garden in front of his children, who showed mixed emotions about the experience.

According to Newsbeat, the host claimed that his bosses at Radio24syv fully support him, despite the backlash, and he intends to address the criticism on-air. The stunt has drawn comparisons to the controversy surrounding the killing of a giraffe named Marius at Copenhagen Zoo, which garnered international attention. Critics have condemned the stunt, labeling it as insensitive and cruel, while others have praised Juhl's willingness to confront the harsh realities of the meat industry. Asger Juhl has received numerous death threats, including from Russian nationals, and has expressed concern for his safety and that of his family.

reference: – A Danish radio DJ chose a controversial way of drawing attention to what he sees as the flawed debate over animal welfare: He hit a 9-week-old rabbit named Allan three times with an iron bicycle pump while on the air Monday, then wrung its neck. Later, Asger Juhl of Radio24syv ate it for dinner. Angry listeners have since called for a boycott of the station, and much worse for Juhl himself, reports the BBC. "I did it to show the hypocrisy in the debate," he says. "Many people are outraged by what I did, at the same time many of these people will eat meat. And when they eat meat, an animal will die." Juhl says he consulted with Aalborg Zoo on a humane way to kill rabbits and was told it used an "iron stick." The zookeeper "hits several baby rabbits every week [to feed] the snakes," Juhl explains, per the Local. His employer is unapologetic. The New York Times quotes station editor-in-chief Jorgen Ramskov as saying the rabbit "was taken good care of, it had a nice life, and it was killed in a decent way." Animals endure "horrific suffering on their way to our dinner tables … without it invoking any strong reactions or calls for boycott," a station rep tells Sky News. Perhaps fueling the outcry on social media, per the Times: The station posted two videos on its Facebook page: a 20-second clip showing Juhl cradling and petting a very-alive Allan, and a 10-second clip showing what the station described as pieces of Allan, cooking in a pan. Those two parts are important, argued the station in a Facebook post yesterday. "We knew that we would be accused of provocation. But it is not an empty provocation; the presenters of the program ate the animal after killing it." (It's not the first controversial animal killing in Denmark.)

final_summary:
In Parma, Ohio, a heated confrontation unfolded between Doug 'Deo' Odolecki, a vocal advocate for police accountability, and two local law enforcement officials, Lieutenant Brian McCann and Officer James Manzo, on a Friday evening. Odolecki, known for using a white poster board to warn drivers about impending drunken-driving checkpoints, found himself at odds with the authorities after refusing to alter his sign, which read "Check point ahead! Turn now!" McCann requested Odolecki remove the directive to turn, citing obstruction of police business, but Odolecki declined, resulting in a citation issued by Manzo.

This incident took place near the intersection of Brookpark Road and State Road, approximately a half-mile away from the actual checkpoint site at Tuxedo Avenue and State Road. Notably, Odolecki claimed that he had utilized the same sign for two years without encountering any issues prior to this encounter. His lawyer, John Gold, a strong supporter of the police accountability activist movement, has taken on the case pro bono, arguing that Odolecki's actions were protected under the First Amendment.

Gold emphasized that the problem lay not with the sign itself, but rather with the specific instruction to turn, which the officers deemed problematic. He contended that Odolecki's actions did not constitute obstruction of an investigation or pose a safety hazard, as he was standing on the sidewalk and the checkpoint was merely underway. In contrast, Ohio law mandates that police provide public notice of drunken-driving checkpoints a week in advance, along with the exact location, start time, and end times a few hours beforehand.

A pending bill in the Ohio House of Representatives seeks to permit drivers to inform other motorists about upcoming checkpoints through the use of their headlights. Interestingly, Officer James Manzo was previously involved in a $40,000 police brutality lawsuit earlier this year, adding another layer of complexity to the situation. Despite the citation, Odolecki remains committed to continuing his efforts to raise awareness about police activities, indicating that he will resume warning drivers about future checkpoints.

reference: – An Ohio man warning drivers about a looming DUI checkpoint would have avoided a ticket if not for the last two words on his sign, reports Raw Story. "Turn now!" Doug "Deo" Odolecki got the citation Friday night while standing on a street corner with his sign that began, "Check point ahead!" Police, who are obligated to make such checkpoints public in Ohio, were OK with the sign in general but told Odolecki to remove the advice to "turn now," reports the Cleveland Plain Dealer. When he refused, officers gave him the ticket on the grounds that he was obstructing police business. (Ohio is poised to let drivers flash their lights at one another to warn of police, and judges elsewhere in the country have blessed that move in the name of free speech.)

final_summary:
In the small city of Upland, California, a bizarre standoff between a 64-year-old live-in nanny, Diane Stretton, and her former employers, the Bracamonte family, has captivated national attention since it became national news on Friday. The situation unfolded after Stretton, who was hired via Craigslist to care for the Bracamontes' three children in exchange for room and board, abruptly stopped working and refused to vacate the premises. Despite being fired on June 6, Stretton claimed she was never formally terminated and continued to occupy the family's home, sparking concerns about her mental state and motives.

Ralph Bracamonte, along with his wife Marcella, initially praised Stretton's work, but her performance declined significantly within the first few months. They alleged that she slowed down, stopped helping with household chores, and eventually confined herself to her room, citing health issues as the reason. However, the family suspected that Stretton's behavior was a ruse to avoid leaving the residence. By the next week, things got worse, and the family realized that something was amiss.

Stretton's refusal to depart led to a series of tense exchanges with the Bracamontes, who sought to resolve the situation through the proper legal channels due to the terms of their employment agreement. As a live-in nanny, Stretton was entitled to a room, and the family was obligated to provide basic necessities such as lights and running water. Nevertheless, the Bracamontes feared that Stretton might try to exploit these provisions to prolong her stay.

In a surprising twist, Stretton recently sent an email to the Bracamontes' lawyer, Marc Cohen, stating that she intended to leave the premises by July 4, contingent upon certain conditions. She cited the impending heatwave and the presence of media personnel outside the house as reasons for her delay. However, Marcella Bracamonte remains skeptical, suspecting that Stretton may attempt to create a scene when she returns from a planned trip on July 2, potentially using the absence of the family as leverage to gain sympathy and attention.

ABC News reported that Stretton was spotted driving past the house by the assembled media, and her belongings remained in her room. The Bracamontes have enlisted the assistance of their relatives to keep an eye on the property while they are away, ensuring that Stretton does not exploit the situation to her advantage. As the standoff continues, the community remains fascinated by the enigmatic figure of Diane Stretton, a woman whose actions have left many questioning her sanity and intentions. With the clock ticking toward July 4, the nation waits with bated breath to see how this peculiar saga will unfold.

Despite being fired on June 6, Stretton claimed she was never formally terminated and continued to occupy the family's home, sparking concerns about her mental state and motives.

As reported by People magazine, the Bracamontes initially praised Stretton's work, but her performance declined significantly within the first few months. They alleged that she slowed down, stopped helping with household chores, and eventually confined herself to her room, citing health issues as the reason. However, the family suspected that Stretton's behavior was a ruse to avoid leaving the residence.

Stretton's refusal to depart led to a series of tense exchanges with the Bracamontes, who sought to resolve the situation through the proper legal channels due to the terms of their employment agreement. As a live-in nanny, Stretton was entitled to a room, and the family was obligated to provide basic necessities such as lights and running water. Nevertheless, the Bracamontes feared that Stretton might try to exploit these provisions to prolong her stay.

In a surprising twist, Stretton recently sent an email to the Bracamontes' lawyer, Marc Cohen, stating that she intended to leave the premises by July 4, contingent upon certain conditions. She cited the impending heatwave and the presence of media personnel outside the house as reasons for her delay. However, Marcella Bracamonte remains skeptical, suspecting that Stretton may attempt to create a scene when she returns from a planned trip on July 4, potentially using the absence of the family as leverage to gain sympathy and attention.

Stretton's past behavior has raised eyebrows, with multiple instances of litigation against her family members and others documented in court records. Her inclusion on California's Vexatious Litigant List, which identifies individuals who repeatedly file frivolous or harassing lawsuits, further fuels concerns about her motivations. The Bracamontes have enlisted the assistance of their relatives to keep an eye on the property while they are away, ensuring that Stretton does not exploit the situation to her advantage.

As the standoff continues, the community remains fascinated by the enigmatic figure of Diane Stretton, a woman whose actions have left many questioning her sanity and intentions. With the clock ticking toward July 4, the nation waits with bated breath to see how this peculiar saga will unfold.
