reference: – A man suspected of killing one of former President George HW Bush's doctors may have been seeking revenge for his mother, who died on the doctor's operating table more than 20 years ago, authorities said Wednesday. Joseph James Pappas, 62, should be considered armed, dangerous, and possibly suicidal, Houston police Chief Art Acevedo said at a news conference Wednesday afternoon. "There was a lot of planning that went into this," Acevedo said of the July 20 attack on Dr. Mark Hausknecht, adding that it appears a "20-year-old grudge" led to the attack, per CNN. Hausknecht was gunned down while riding his bike to work at Houston Methodist Hospital, which is part of the busy Texas Medical Center. Authorities say the shooter rode past Hausknecht before turning around and firing. A tip on Tuesday led police to suspect Pappas, the chief said, though he didn't elaborate as to the nature of the tip. Acevedo said Pappas hadn't been seen in 36 to 48 hours, the AP reports. He said the last anyone had heard from Pappas was in a Tuesday morning text message in which Pappas wrote that he was going to kill himself. Acevedo said police searched his home at 4am Wednesday and found evidence that ties Pappas to the killing, but he declined to elaborate as to the nature of the evidence. The chief said Pappas, who was a peace officer in Harris County for three decades per the Houston Chronicle, is white and very fit, and that he's likely getting around on his 10-speed bicycle, which he rides "extensively and almost exclusively." Friends and family have described Hausknecht as a humble and generous man who was adored by his patients, volunteered in his community and cared about the environment. He was also an avid cyclist and rode his bike to work each morning, as he lived less than 2 miles from his office.

final_summary:
In a shocking revelation, Lana Wood, the sister of late actress Natalie Wood, has accused her former brother-in-law, Robert Wagner, of plotting to blacklist her from Hollywood following Natalie's mysterious death in 1981. According to Lana, she had a phone conversation with Hollywood executive Rowland Perkins shortly after Natalie's passing, where Perkins informed her that she had been blacklisted by Wagner, making it impossible for her to secure a job in production or acting. This marked the end of Lana's promising career in Hollywood, which included a notable role in the 1971 film Diamonds Are Forever.

Natalie Wood's death occurred while she was on a pleasure cruise with her husband Robert Wagner and actor Christopher Walken on their yacht, Splendour, off the coast of Catalina Island in the Pacific. Initially, her death was ruled an accidental drowning, but in 2012, the cause was revised to drowning with undetermined factors. The Los Angeles Sheriff's Department has repeatedly reopened the investigation, labeling the death suspicious and seeking witness testimony.

Dennis Davern, the former captain of the yacht, has now come forward claiming that he believes Natalie's death was murder, contradicting his initial statement to police. He alleges that he witnessed a heated argument between Natalie and Robert Wagner on the night of her disappearance and that Wagner attempted to prevent him from calling for help. Lana Wood has also expressed suspicion about Wagner's involvement in her sister's death, stating that he has "something to hide."

Lana Wood's accusations against Wagner are not new, as she previously appeared on the "Megyn Kelly Today" show in July, stating that she believed Wagner was responsible for her sister's murder. However, Wagner has consistently denied any wrongdoing and has refused to cooperate with investigators. His representative, Alan Nierob, has condemned Davern and Lana Wood for capitalizing on Natalie's death.

The case remains unsolved, with the Los Angeles Sheriff's Department still seeking information and witnesses. The Dr. Phil show has also covered the story, featuring interviews with Davern and Lana Wood, further fueling speculation about the circumstances surrounding Natalie Wood's tragic death. The story was featured in an exclusive interview with Us Weekly, which is now available for download on iTunes as part of the 12-part audio documentary "Fatal Voyage: The Mysterious Death of Natalie Wood." Host Phil McGraw spoke with Davern and Lana Wood on his show, discussing the latest developments in the case.

reference: – For nearly 40 years, the death of Natalie Wood has remained a mystery. First the 43-year-old actress was ruled to have died of accidental drowning after disappearing from a yacht she was on with husband Robert Wagner and actor Christopher Walken. Then, in 2012, that ruling shifted to drowning plus "other undetermined factors," with LA County Sheriff's Department investigators suspicious there was more to the case. Wood's sister, Lana Wood, has contended for years that Wagner was responsible for her sibling's death, and this week, Dr. Phil McGraw added oxygen to that theory by having Lana Wood and the yacht's captain, Dennis Davern, on his show for two days of interviews on the subject, USA Today reports. "You believe Robert Wagner murdered Natalie Wood?" McGraw asked Davern, who'd initially stuck with the accidental drowning story. "Yes, I really do," Davern answered. He says he heard Wood and Wagner fighting, and when the noise ceased, he went up on the deck to find Wagner standing there solo; Davern says Wagner insisted they say Wood likely slipped and fell into the water. Lana Wood sticks by that, too. "Are you suggesting that [Wagner] knocked her out and threw her in the water?" McGraw asked. "Something like that, absolutely," she answered. Per Us Weekly, Lana Wood also recently appeared on a podcast in which she says that, shortly after her sister's death, Hollywood exec Rowland Perkins informed her Wagner was keeping her out of Tinseltown. "He said, 'Stop trying to get a job in production or acting. You've been blacklisted by Robert Wagner," Lana Wood said. A rep for Wagner, now 88, tells USA Today that Davern and Lana Wood are "despicable human beings" and that "they should be ashamed of themselves."

final_summary:
Viewers who luxuriated in a good cry after "The Fault in Our Stars" may be gratified to know that it's not time to stop sobbing. The perfect bookend to a tear-stained summer, "If I Stay" has arrived in theaters with the same good taste, modest cool factor, and shameless tear-jerking that made its predecessor such a multi-hankie hit. Adapted by R.J. Cutler ("A Perfect Candidate," "The September Issue") from Gayle Forman's novel, "If I Stay" hews largely to the same formula as "The Fault in Our Stars": An attractive young couple grapples with first love and looming mortality against a scenic backdrop, the mechanics of sexual awakenings safely couched in soft-focus ellipses ("Adam, I've never..."). As in "The Fault in Our Stars," this story is told from the point of view of an exceptionally self-aware young woman staring down impending death. Here, though, she's anxiously watching over herself as she lies in a coma, trying to decide whether life is still worth living after nearly losing it and everything else in a horrifying car crash.

It's a maudlin, potentially mawkish setup, but first-time feature director Cutler and his screenwriter, Shauna Cross, put an amber glow on even the toughest aspects of a story in which everything is just about perfect (until it most decidedly isn't). Chloe Grace Moretz plays Mia Hall, a 17-year-old cello prodigy whose happy, laid-back life with her former punk-rocker parents is upended when she meets a handsome guitarist named Adam (Jamie Blackley). Their mildly piquant romance — she idolizes Yo-Yo Ma, he's more of a Ramones guy — is of the classic opposites-attract variety, spiced by dashes of O. Henry's lyrical irony. At a Halloween party, eager to please each other, she dons a platinum wig as Debbie Harry and he comes dressed as Beethoven.

It's all played for minimum dramatic tension and maximum aestheticized wish fulfillment, heightened by Cutler's tendency to film everything slightly out of focus and Cross's to write fuzzy-wuzzy dialogue like "Adam, that song was great." Even more idealized is Mia's family, headed by a mom and dad who clearly trained at the Pitchfork School for Cool Hipster Parenting. Played with warmth and easy understatement by Mireille Enos and Joshua Leonard, Mia's'rents casually name-check Iggy Pop and Kim Gordon as they roam around their brightly painted Portland bungalow, bedecked with the requisite number of cred-signifying posters and musical detritus. It's all too flawless to be believed — we never see Mia argue with her parents, much less with her Alice Cooper-quoting little brother — but the colorful, cozy domestic portrait makes it that much more unsettling when, on a snowy road one day, it all comes to a shatteringly violent end.

"If I Stay" toggles between two worlds: Mia's desperate, barefooted sojourn at the hospital and flashbacks to the life she may or may not decide to leave behind. Although the emotionalism begins to drag the narrative down as her anguished vigil goes on and on and on, Cutler smoothly navigates Mia's double-sided experience, albeit in a way that recalls the "Nashville" episodes he has directed more than the ethereal otherworldliness of, say, "The Lovely Bones."

Moretz, best known for her spiky, moodily effective turns in such tough films as "Kick-Ass" and "Let Me In," submerges her most interesting contours in service to a blandly edge-free character. Blackley is slightly more convincing as the sweaty, discreetly pierced frontman of an on-the-rise, safely pseudo hard-rockin' band. Granted, the group isn't channeling Joey Ramone as much as Clear Channel's pop heartthrob du jour. But the musical sequences — including an impromptu Smashing Pumpkins jam in Mia's back yard — bring some welcome verve to talky scenes of soulful heart-to-hearts and teary hand-wringing. Rest assured, there's plenty of that, too, in "If I Stay." But even at its most wrenchingly painful, the film readily delivers generous dollops of pleasure.

PG-13. At area theaters. Contains thematic elements, some sexual material, and mild profanity. 106 minutes.

history.draft:
"If I Stay", a cinematic adaptation of Gayle Forman's novel, follows the story of Mia Hall, a 17-year-old cello prodigy portrayed by Chloë Grace Moretz, who finds herself grappling with first love and impending mortality after a devastating car crash leaves her in a coma. Directed by R.J. Cutler, the film employs a similar formula to "The Fault in Our Stars", another tear-jerking tale of young love and mortality, with Mia's narrative unfolding from her perspective as she watches over herself from beyond the brink of death. 

In the film, Mia's idyllic life with her former punk-rocker parents, played by Mireille Enos and Joshua Leonard, is turned upside down upon meeting Adam, a charming guitarist played by Jamie Blackley. Their romance, marked by a dash of O. Henry-esque irony, is set against the picturesque backdrop of Portland, where Mia's family resides in a brightly painted bungalow adorned with music memorabilia. However, their happiness is short-lived, as a fateful car crash on a snowy road brings Mia's world crashing down.

As Mia struggles to decide whether to stay alive or succumb to death, the narrative toggles between her desperate, barefooted sojourn at the hospital and flashbacks to the life she may be leaving behind. While the film's emotionalism occasionally drags the narrative down, Cutler skillfully navigates Mia's dual existence, drawing inspiration from his work on TV episodes rather than the ethereal quality of films like "The Lovely Bones".

Moretz, known for her edgy performances in "Kick-Ass" and "Let Me In", tones down her intensity to portray a blandly relatable character, while Blackley fares better as Adam, bringing a sense of authenticity to the role. The film's musical sequences, featuring an impromptu Smashing Pumpkins jam in Mia's backyard, inject a welcome dose of energy into the otherwise sentimental narrative. Despite its flaws, "If I Stay" delivers generous doses of pleasure, making it a fitting companion piece to "The Fault in Our Stars".

reference: – If I Stay tells the story of a teen girl with a big future who's nearly killed in a car crash. Informed by out-of-body experiences, she must decide whether to stick around on Earth or head to Heaven. It'll make you cry, but it doesn't offer much otherwise, critics say. (Audiences, however, seem to disagree: 80% like it at Rotten Tomatoes, twice the figure for critics). Some examples: It's up to audiences "whether the movie's many moments of genuine sweetness and affecting tenderness can survive the persistent silliness of Mia's out-of-body excursions," writes Joe Morgenstern at the Wall Street Journal. "At its best, If I Stay evokes the primacy of friendship, the warmth of family … and the urgency of adolescent love. But the production as a whole is awfully clumsy." "Full disclosure: I went through half a pack of Kleenex watching If I Stay," writes Elizabeth Weitzman in the New York Daily News. "But know this: Those tears are no more honestly earned than if director RJ Cutler had merely been chopping onions in front of me. … The movie is designed not to explore the experience of illness, or first love, or adolescence, but merely to make us swoon, sigh, and sob." The movie "hews largely to the same formula as The Fault in Our Stars," another book-based film dealing with teens facing death, notes Ann Hornaday in the Washington Post. But If I Stay offers "the perfect bookend to a tear-stained summer": "Even at its most wrenchingly painful, the film readily delivers generous dollops of pleasure." In the Los Angeles Times, Kenneth Turan calls the film "a flat-out, all-in fantasy romance, an unashamed tear-jerker that is unafraid of glossy emotions." Sure, "every moment in If I Stay is not all that it might be"—but when its leads "are looking into each other's eyes, you are not going to care."

final_summary:
In the unforgiving waters surrounding Antarctica, a remarkable adaptation has allowed certain fish species to thrive in temperatures as low as 29 degrees Fahrenheit. Known as notothenioid fish, these creatures owe their survival to antifreeze proteins (AFPs) that bind to ice crystals and prevent them from growing, thereby preventing the fish's blood from freezing. However, a recent study led by Paul Cziko, a doctoral student at the University of Oregon's Institute of Ecology and Evolution, has uncovered a paradoxical side effect of these proteins - they also inhibit the melting of ice crystals.

Researchers had previously believed that AFPs only prevented ice from forming, allowing the fish to avoid freezing. However, Cziko's team discovered that the proteins also prevent ice from melting, effectively creating "superheated" ice within the fish's bodies. This phenomenon was observed in both laboratory experiments and field studies conducted in McMurdo Sound, Antarctica, where the team collected temperature data over a period of 13 years, specifically from 1999-2012, a long-term record of a high-latitude Antarctic fish habitat.

The study found that AFP-stabilized ice resisted melting at temperatures above the expected equilibrium freezing/melting point (eqFMP), both in vitro and in vivo. In fact, superheated ice was directly observed in notothenioid serum samples and in solutions of purified AFPs. Moreover, ice persisted inside live fishes at temperatures more than 1°C above their eqFMP for at least 24 hours, and at a lower temperature for at least several days. Field experiments conducted in Eugene, Ore., confirmed that superheated ice occurs naturally inside wild fishes, contradicting the assumption that seasonal summer warming would reliably eliminate internal ice in most summers.

According to Cziko, who collaborated with Christina Cheng and Arthur DeVries, a husband-and-wife team of animal biologists at the University of Illinois, the ice crystals accumulate in the fish's spleens, leading researchers to speculate that there may be a mechanism to clear the ice from the circulation. While the long-term consequences of this phenomenon are still unknown, the study highlights the complex interplay between the benefits and drawbacks of AFPs in notothenioid fish. 

The findings were published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences and shed light on the intricate relationship between the fish's adaptations and their environment. As Cziko noted, "Every good evolutionary innovation comes with some potentially bad or unintended consequences." This study serves as a reminder that evolution is a stepwise process, where progress often involves trade-offs and unforeseen outcomes.

reference: – How do the fish that thrive in the waters around Antarctica prevent their blood from turning to ice? Turns out at least some of them don't. Scientists have long known that the group of fish species known as notothenioids have an antifreeze protein in their blood that prevents them from being frozen to death, but new research has revealed that the same protein keeps ice crystals in their veins, apparently permanently, UPI reports. The crystals melted at a few degrees above freezing, but the fish may spend their whole lives without encountering temperatures that warm, according to research published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. The antifreeze proteins that prevented the blood from completely freezing also bound itself to the ice crystals, keeping them from getting smaller, the scientists say. Lead researcher Paul Cziko tells the Christian Science Monitor that it's not clear whether the fish have evolved some way of dealing with the ice crystals in their bodies, or if the ice ends up killing them in the long term. "This shows that with every good evolutionary innovation, some potentially bad or unintended consequences come along with that," he says. "Evolution is a stepwise process. You take two steps forward, and take one step back." (Researchers recently solved the decades-old mystery of a quacking noise detected in Antarctic waters.)

final_summary:
In the wake of the disappearance of Austin Stephanos and Perry Cohen, both 14, while fishing off the coast of Florida, a team of volunteers and experts has joined forces to aid in the search efforts. On Friday, the boys' families reported them missing after their 19-foot white single-engine boat was discovered abandoned approximately 67 miles off the shore of Daytona Beach, with no signs of the teenagers on board.

As the search continues, Mitch Roffer, founder and president of Roffer's Ocean Fishing Forecasting Service in West Melbourne, has utilized his expertise in oceanography to assist in the search. Roffer's company specializes in analyzing currents and pinpointing convergence zones along the Gulf Stream boundaries where fish tend to congregate. His analysis has been crucial in predicting the potential trajectory of the missing teens, should they still be floating at sea.

According to Roffer, even slight variations in current speed can significantly impact the search area, potentially leading to a 120-mile variance in estimates of the boys' location. This highlights the complexity of navigating the Gulf Stream, which can move at speeds of up to double its normal rate when entering its core. As Roffer explained, a quarter-of-a-mile-per-hour difference in current speed over 10 hours can result in a 2-to-3-mile separation, eventually growing to 15-to-20 miles, and ultimately 30 miles apart.

Roffer's analysis has led to the creation of color-coded maps, shared on Facebook and Twitter, which provide critical information for volunteer rescuers. These maps indicate areas where the boys may have drifted, given their initial position and the direction of the Gulf Stream. Volunteer pilots have flown missions from Melbourne and New Smyrna Beach using this data to aid in the search.

Video footage by Tim Shortt, published on July 27, 2015, documented Roffer's efforts to assist in the search. The video was recorded in West Melbourne, where Roffer's company is located. 

The families of the missing teens have also taken to social media to appeal for assistance, urging people to scour the coastline for any signs of debris or clues that might lead to their sons' safe return. In a statement, Nick Korniloff, Perry's stepfather, called upon residents from Palm Beach to Georgia to join the search efforts.

Meanwhile, local authorities continue to investigate the circumstances surrounding the boys' disappearance. As Ted Lund, a Florida Today outdoors writer and Cocoa Beach licensed boat captain, noted, the incident serves as a poignant reminder of the unforgiving nature of the ocean, which poses risks to individuals regardless of their experience or background.

reference: – The families of Perry Cohen and Austin Stephanos are living a nightmare—the 14-year-old boys are missing at sea and their capsized boat was found on Sunday—but they believe the teens have the skills to survive. As the search off the coast of Florida continues, family members say the boys may have crafted a flotation device using items including life jackets, an engine cover, and a cooler that were missing from their single-engine fishing boat, USA Today reports. Relatives and neighbors say the boys, like others in their Palm Beach County neighborhood, are experienced sailors and have been around boats their entire lives, reports the Palm Beach Post. A Coast Guard spokesman says the search is continuing "aggressively" and the water is warm enough that the teens, who were last seen on Friday, could survive for four or five days, the Post reports. Because of currents, search efforts are focusing on the area north of where the boat was found near Daytona Beach, though they could be hard to spot, the spokesman says. "When a person is in the water, you're basically looking for the chest up, so it's a relatively small object you're looking for," he says. "It's a challenging environment." The families have urged people from Palm Beach to the coast of Georgia to look for clues that might have washed up, especially the Yamaha engine cover and YETI cooler, NBC News reports.

final_summary:
On Sunday, a harrowing incident unfolded near Grande Prairie, Alta., where 10 horses broke through the ice covering a slough, prompting a daring rescue mission by local firefighters. According to Trevor Grant, regional fire chief in the County of Grande Prairie, the horses had wandered onto the ice, which had become increasingly fragile due to warmer temperatures, making it much thinner than usual for all winter. 

The emergency response was swift, with approximately 35 regional firefighters and members of a search and rescue team rushing to the scene, located about 65 kilometers west of Grande Prairie, shortly after receiving the distress call at around 4:15 pm. CTV News reported that the incident occurred on a property west of Hythe, Alberta. The rescue effort was further complicated by the presence of wild bronco horses, accustomed to rodeos, which posed a significant risk to both the rescuers and themselves.

Undeterred, the responders employed their specialized training to execute a meticulous rescue plan. Using chainsaws, they carefully cut pathways to the shore, while others utilized slings and boards to gently coax the horses across the treacherous terrain. By 6 pm, all 10 horses had been extracted from the icy waters, although tragically, three succumbed to their ordeal: two from exhaustion and one due to injuries inflicted by the panicked animals.

A veterinarian's assessment on Monday revealed that the surviving seven horses were in stable condition and expected to recover fully. Regional Fire Chief Trevor Grant expressed pride in his team's accomplishment, highlighting the significance of their large animal rescue training program, which has evolved over the last couple of years in response to growing demand. He emphasized the importance of awareness among landowners regarding the rapidly shifting ice conditions, particularly during this time of year, cautioning that what seemed safe in the morning could become perilous by the afternoon.

reference: – Dozens of firefighters and rescue crew risked their own safety to save 10 horses that fell through the ice Sunday in Canada—and it was all caught on video, CBC reports. After the horses fell through the thinning ice in northern Alberta, rescuers had to use chainsaws to cut a path back to the shore before using boards and slings to get the horses to dry land. Rescuers were at risk not only from the cold temperatures and thin ice, but from the frantic animals, which were "wild broncing" horses used in rodeos and not domesticated, according to CTV News. All 10 horses were removed from the water after two hours, though three later died. "To rescue seven was a great feat, especially when having such a large number in the water," a local fire chief tells CBC.

final_summary:
Wade Michael Page, a 40-year-old Army veteran, was identified as the suspect in the mass-shooting at the Sikh temple in Oak Creek, Wisconsin, on Sunday. Page, who was administratively discharged from the Army in 1998, had a history of extremist views and affiliations with white supremacist groups.

According to his military service record, Page joined the Army in April 1992 and underwent training at Ft. Sill, Oklahoma, before being stationed at Ft. Bliss, Texas, and Ft. Bragg, North Carolina. During his nearly six years of service, Page held various roles, including serving as a repairman for the Hawk surface-to-air missile system and training as a psychological operations specialist. However, he was demoted from sergeant to specialist and received a general discharge, rather than an honorable one.

Monday, neighbors in Cudahy, Wisconsin, reported that Page's behavior became increasingly erratic in the days leading up to the shooting. His neighbor, Jennifer Dunn, stated that he blasted loud music from his apartment and spent hours pacing outside his vehicle, staring into space. Dunn described Page as a "creepy quiet" individual who rarely interacted with others and avoided eye contact.

Page's stepmother, Laura Page, remembered him as a "precious child" who grew up in a loving family in Colorado. However, she acknowledged that her stepson's behavior began to change after his mother passed away due to lupus in North Carolina. Page's father remarried, and the family moved to Texas, but Page remained in Colorado, splitting time between his aunt and grandmother.

As an adult, Page struggled with employment and relationships. He worked as a truck driver for about five years but was dismissed from that job in 2010. He also lost a house in Lucas, North Carolina, due to foreclosure. In 2011, he moved to Wisconsin, where he lived in a South Milwaukee apartment with his girlfriend. Neighbors described him as a grumpy loner who occasionally played music with his band.

On Saturday, Page loaded his vehicle with bags and boxes, sparking concern among his neighbors. The next morning, he carried out a deadly attack on the Sikh temple in Oak Creek, killing six people and wounding three others. Authorities have deemed the incident a terrorist act, citing Page's tattoos and extremist ideology.

reference: – More is emerging on suspected Sikh temple gunman Wade Michael Page: The white supremacist and former Army soldier was demoted in rank and discharged in 1998, the LA Times reports. A military official didn't say why Page received a general discharge rather than an honorable one after 6 1/2 years of service. But a dishonorable discharge would have stopped him from buying the 9mm handgun he purchased July 28, picked up July 30, and used days later in the shooting, the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel reports. Among other emerging details: Lt. Brian Murphy, 51, was the first officer on the scene. He came upon a victim in the temple parking lot and was ambushed by Page, who shot him eight or nine times. When other officers arrived, Page fired on them and was killed by a policeman with a rifle. Murphy remains in critical condition. Authorities said this morning they were trying to identify a white male who was a "person of interest." But FBI officials said later he was not relevant to the case. Page was quiet and "not a friendly guy," said a neighbor. "You'd have more fun with a camel. He was very quiet. You'd say hi and he'd kind of 'uh.' It was like he didn't care if you were talking or not." Page's grandmother, Elaine Lenz, called Page a good grandson who gave her a dozen roses last month "just to tell me he loved me," she said. "I'm sorry he has caused so much trouble."

final_summary:
Minnesota Representative Michele Bachmann announced her decision to withdraw her Swiss citizenship, which she acquired through marriage to her husband, Marcus Bachmann, a dual American and Swiss citizen, in 1978. According to Bachmann, the citizenship was an "automatic" designation conferred upon her by Swiss law at the time of her marriage. However, this claim contradicts previous statements made by Bachmann and her spokesperson, Becky Rogness, who stated that the Bachmann family had actively sought the citizenship. 

In a statement released two days after a Swiss television news crew reported on the matter to the Swiss Consulate in Washington, Bachmann emphasized her commitment to the United States of America, stating that she was born in America and is a proud American citizen. Bachmann's decision comes amidst criticism from her opponent, Jim Graves, who described the news as a "distraction" yesterday. Some commentators questioned whether Bachmann's dual citizenship posed a risk to her security clearance, particularly given her position on the House Intelligence Committee. Others defended her right to hold dual citizenship, pointing out that many individuals benefit from such arrangements without compromising their loyalty to their primary country of residence. 

It appears that Bachmann's husband, Marcus, holds dual American and Swiss citizenship due to his Swiss heritage, and the couple's children had also explored the possibility of acquiring Swiss citizenship. Under Swiss law, individuals can acquire citizenship through marriage or birth, and the United States does not prohibit its citizens from holding dual citizenship. However, obtaining citizenship in multiple countries can be complex, and the State Department advises against holding dual citizenship for various reasons. Former President Obama had previously faced scrutiny over his own citizenship status, but ultimately proved to be a U.S. citizen.

reference: – If you were concerned that Michele Bachmann's Swiss citizenship meant she'd given up on America, worry no longer: She's getting rid of it. "Today I sent a letter to the Swiss Consulate requesting withdrawal of my dual Swiss citizenship, which was conferred upon me by operation of Swiss law when I married my husband in 1978," she said in a statement, according to Minnesota Public Radio. "I took this action because I want to make it perfectly clear: I was born in America and I am a proud American citizen." Bachmann hopes to put an end to the story that surfaced earlier this week. At the time, her spokeswoman described the situation this way, notes the LA Times: "Congresswoman Bachmann's husband is of Swiss descent so she has been eligible for dual citizenship since they got married in 1978. However, recently some of their children wanted to exercise their eligibility for dual citizenship so they went through the process as a family." Her opponent in November, Jim Graves of the Democratic-Farmer-Labor party, has called the Swiss story a "distraction." He has confirmed that he and his family are also "proud to be Americans."

final_summary:
On Friday, a senseless tragedy unfolded in the quiet town of Calera, Alabama, when 42-year-old Kathleen Dawn West, affectionately known as Kat or Kitty Kat West, was found dead outside her home on Saturday, January 13. The mother of one and wife to her husband, Jeff, was discovered partially nude and bleeding from the head, with a cell phone and a green liquor bottle nearby. The Calera Police Department has classified her death as a homicide, but the cause remains unknown, and no arrests have been made.

Kat West led a dual life, presenting herself as a devoted wife and mother on social media platforms like Facebook, where she had thousands of followers. However, behind the façade, she maintained a private website and social media profiles under the pseudonym Kitty Kat West, where she showcased risqué photos and offered exclusive content for a monthly fee of $15.99. Her online presence sparked intense curiosity among amateur sleuths, who flocked to Facebook groups dedicated to discussing her death, sharing theories, and speculating about potential motives.

These online forums, with over 3,000 members combined, have become a hotbed of unfounded speculation, with some users pointing fingers at her husband, while others suggest a deranged stalker might have been responsible. Despite the lack of concrete evidence, the discussion has taken a voyeuristic turn, with some users fixating on West's online activities as a possible factor in her murder. However, law enforcement officials have thus far refused to confirm any connection between her online presence and her death.

As the investigation continues, the community is reeling from the shock of West's sudden and tragic demise. Neighbors describe her as a friendly and outgoing person who kept to herself, often seen tending to her lawn and enjoying quality time with her family. Her husband, Jeff, a campus police officer at Birmingham Southern College, has remained tight-lipped about the circumstances surrounding her death.

Gizmodo reported on the case, highlighting the enigmatic nature of West's online persona and the subsequent speculation surrounding her death. Local residents, including Xavier Lee, who lives nearby, expressed concern and sadness, emphasizing the importance of prioritizing family safety in the wake of this tragedy. As the investigation unfolds, it remains unclear what drove the brutal murder of Kat West, but one thing is certain – her life and legacy will continue to captivate and haunt the online communities that once celebrated her.

Federal court records reveal that West and her husband filed for bankruptcy in 2007, but the case was resolved without incident. The couple moved to Calera in 2015, purchasing a home valued at $169,000. West's love for Marilyn Monroe was evident in her online personas, where she often incorporated elements of the iconic actress's style and charm.

A photograph taken on Thursday, January 18, 2018, shows the home of Kathleen Dawn West in Calera, Alabama. West identified herself as an exhibitionist on a website offering adult photos. She was found dead outside the house, and no arrests have been made.

As the search for answers continues, the community comes together to mourn the loss of a vibrant and complex individual, whose life was cut short in the most devastating manner.

reference: – Kathleen Dawn West described herself as a full-time wife and mom on Facebook but lived another life on other social media platforms, calling herself an exhibitionist and posting risque photos with a chance for subscribers to see sexier images for $15.99 a month. The 42-year-old's partially nude body was found dead outside her Alabama home, and authorities are now faced with a question: Did West's online activities play a role in her death? Police have classified West's death as a homicide, but they haven't said how she died. No charges had been filed by Friday, six days after she died. But the mysterious nature of West's death—she was found dead early Jan. 13 in the quiet bedroom community of Calera, a town of 14,000 people about 35 miles south of Birmingham—has people buzzing. At least two Facebook groups with more than 2,200 members total have been created to discuss the case (Gizmodo digs into their theories). West posted photos of her husband and daughter on her own Facebook account, but seemed like a different person on other online accounts, reports the AP. With a personal description that included "exhibitionist," West had a private Instagram account under the name Kitty Kat West, reports Al.com, with nearly 52,000 followers that linked to a paid site that featured adult content and "naughty fun." Some of the images were included on a site for "mature hotties." Initially, West just tweeted links to a site where she sold clothing items, including dresses, coats, shoes, and kid's clothing. The feed took a sharp turn Aug. 11, when she tweeted: "Happy Frisky Friday - Let's get this party started," with a link to the pay-per-view site. The posts continued until the morning before her death. The AP has more.

final_summary:
On March 23, 2016, the music world mourned the loss of Phife Dawg, whose real name was Malik Taylor, a renowned member of the critically acclaimed group A Tribe Called Quest. The rapper succumbed to complications related to diabetes, leaving behind a legacy of iconic hip-hop tracks and timeless lyrics. As news of his passing spread, numerous artists paid their respects through social media, including singer Jill Scott, who tweeted, "So incredibly sad to hear the Great Phife Dawg of Tribe Called Quest has passed away at only 45 yrs. GREAT MC. Sincere Love and Respect." Fellow hip-hop legend Chuck D also honored Phife Dawg, describing him as a "HipHop & Rap word Warrior" and praising his ability to breathe life into rhymes.

In response to Phife Dawg's untimely death, WSB-TV Atlanta traffic reporter Mark Arum found a unique way to pay tribute during his live broadcast today. Inspired by the rapper's music, which had been a significant part of his youth, Arum incorporated Phife Dawg's lyrics into his traffic updates. This was not an isolated incident; Arum revealed that he often incorporates hip-hop lyrics into his reports, typically using one or two per show. He decided to let them fly this morning, dedicating more time to celebrating Phife Dawg's legacy, seamlessly weaving the rapper's words into his commentary.

Some of the notable references included in Arum's tribute were lines such as "More hits than the Braves or the Yankees," "Are things ludicrously speedy or infectious with the slow-mo?", "Track record's longer than a DC-20 aircraft," "Do that, do that, do do that that that," and "Tell your mother, tell your father, send a telegram." Another memorable reference was "One for the treble, two for the bass, you know the style." These clever insertions showcased Arum's creativity and respect for Phife Dawg's artistry.

As news outlets reported on Phife Dawg's passing, online platforms like BuzzFeed shared stories about the impact of his music on fans worldwide. In addition to paying tribute to the late rapper, viewers can learn more about his life and career by watching the documentary "Beats, Rhymes, and Life," which features A Tribe Called Quest.

reference: – A Tribe Called Quest rapper Phife Dawg lived in Atlanta for more than a decade, and there are apparently at least a couple Quest fans at Atlanta's WSB-TV. After the rapper's death Wednesday, the station's traffic reporter, Mark Arum, worked an impressive number of Quest references into his traffic report. The video went viral and was shared by sites including the Daily Dot and BuzzFeed, who note some of the best call-outs: "This rush hour comin' on with more hits than the Braves or the Yankees." "Are things ludicrously speedy or infectious with the slow-mo?" "[Interstate-]85 is stacked and packed now, heading into midtown Atlanta with a crash south of 400—tell your mother, tell your father, send a telegram." Anchor Fred Blankenship even got in on the action, noting at one point, "Sometimes the definition of traffic comes sideways and straightways." "Phife and Tribe were the soundtrack of my youth," Arum tells BuzzFeed. "I was crushed when I heard the news this morning just before we went on the air. I wanted to pay tribute to him somehow." He says he's been inserting hip-hop lyrics into his traffic reports for years, but he really "let them fly" Wednesday—he typically only includes one or two a show. Arum and Blankenship also discussed their love for A Tribe Called Quest in a Facebook video Thursday, with Blankenship expressing his amazement that Arum "dropped deep Tribe Called Quest lyrics, and then didn't short-change the traffic."

final_summary:
Nearly six months after the passing of Robin Williams, the renowned actor and comedian, a contentious legal battle has erupted between his widow, Susan Schneider Williams, and his three children, Zak, Zelda, and Cody Williams, from his previous marriages. The dispute centers around the distribution of Robin Williams' vast estate, which includes a significant portion of his accumulated wealth and cherished personal belongings, acquired over some 40 years of a film, television, and stage career. 

According to exclusive details obtained by TMZ, the disagreement revolves around the interpretation of Robin Williams' will and trust, particularly regarding the division of his personal effects, including clothing, collectibles, and photographs. Both parties exhibit a strong interest in these items, which serve as tangible reminders of Robin Williams' unique comedic genius and creative spirit. These possessions range from his bicycles to fossil and toy collections, showcasing the breadth of his eclectic interests.

The tension between Susan and the Williams children stems from differing interpretations of the will and trust. Susan, who was married to Robin for 3 years, claims certain items, such as the tuxedo from their wedding and various knick-knacks, should be hers. However, the children argue that these items fall under the category of "personal effects," which, according to the trust, belong to them. They assert that Susan is attempting to redefine the term "jewelry" to exclude Robin's watch collection, which they believe should be included in their inheritance.

In a separate incident, the children claim that someone entered the house days after Robin's death and removed some of his belongings, leading to allegations of theft. Sources connected with the children say Susan initially refused to let them in the house after Robin's passing, fueling the dispute. The situation has escalated to the point where the parties involved are seeking resolution through the courts.

Those familiar with Robin Williams express concern that he would be disheartened by the public nature of the conflict, wishing that the matter had been resolved amicably within the family. The prolonged and acrimonious dispute highlights the complexities and challenges that often arise when loved ones disagree on the distribution of a deceased person's assets.

reference: – In a sad but all-too-common postscript to someone's life cut short, Robin Williams' children and wife are locked in a bitter dispute over his estate. In court papers filed in December and January, the comedian's three adult children say they are "heartbroken" that widow Susan Williams is "challenging the plans he so carefully made for his estate" by trying to keep personal effects she's not entitled to, reports the New York Times. The children—Zak, Zelda, and Cody—are from two previous marriages, and they note that Susan was married to their father for "less than three years." They accuse her of adding "insult to a terrible injury" and say she refused to allow them in his Tiburon, Calif., home in the days after his death. Williams, who committed suicide in August, specified his estate go to his three children, but the dispute centers around a provision that the home, its contents, and enough cash to cover "all costs related to the residence" go to Susan Williams. The children accuse her of wrongly interpreting this to mean she can keep belongings she was never meant to have and of "greedily" trying to claim extra funds. Sources tell TMZ that the widow, who refers to Williams' collections of things like action figures and movie posters as "knickknacks," also argues that "jewelry" doesn't include things like watches. Her lawyer tells the Times that his client doesn't have "sticky fingers" and "compared to what the Williams children were set to receive from their father, this is a bucket of water in a lake."

final_summary:
In a groundbreaking achievement, researchers from the University of Tokyo and Kyoto University announced on Friday that they have successfully created human ear cartilage from induced pluripotent stem cells. According to the breakthrough, the team utilized a novel approach by culturing human induced pluripotent stem cells into cartilage cells, which were then formed into numerous tiny balls. These balls were subsequently arranged within a plastic tube approximately 3 millimeters in diameter, allowing the researchers to shape a human ear.

The innovative technique involved implanting the ear-shaped tube onto the back of a rat, where it remained for about two months before dissolving, leaving behind a fully formed human ear measuring approximately 5 centimeters in length. This remarkable development holds promise for individuals born without ears or those suffering from ear deformities, offering a potential solution through regenerative medicine.

This achievement comes as part of ongoing efforts to push the boundaries of bioengineering, a field that applies engineering principles to biological systems to create new human tissue, organs, and other body parts. In the realm of kidney disease, the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) is pioneering the creation of an artificial kidney device utilizing human kidney cells, silicon nanofilters, and powered by blood pressure. Led by nephrologist William Fissell and bioengineering professor Shuvo Roy, the project aims to initiate clinical trials in 2017, addressing the pressing issue of chronic kidney disease affecting nearly one in ten American adults, as reported by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in the U.S.

Furthermore, advancements in bioengineering have enabled the growth of artificial intestines in laboratories to aid in diagnosing digestive disorders and assisting patients born without a portion of their intestine. Researchers at Cornell University collaborated with pediatric surgeon David Hackam to develop a small artificial intestine using collagen and stem cells, while a team at the Swiss EPFL laboratory created a miniature intestinal wall from cultured epithelial cells and electronics called NutriChip to identify food triggers for inflammation. In 2011, scientists at Harvard's Wyss Institute developed a "gut-on-a-chip" to mimic the human gut using intestinal cells in a tiny silicon polymer device.

In addition, scientists at Cornell University successfully implanted gel and collagen-seeded rat cells into rat spines to create artificial discs alleviating chronic back pain. Duke bioengineers have also developed a gel mixture capable of regenerating tissue when injected into the space between discs. 

A significant milestone was achieved in 2011 when a team of surgeons spent nine hours operating on a patient at Children's Hospital of Illinois in Peoria, creating a bioengineered windpipe made from the patient's own bone marrow cells. Although the patient later passed away due to complications from a subsequent surgery, her case highlights the potential of bioengineering in saving lives.

Bioengineers are also making strides in recreating other vital organs, including the liver, with teams at Wake Forest University Baptist Medical Center and Yokohama City University Graduate School of Medicine publishing studies on the growth of miniature livers using decellularized animal livers and reprogrammed human adult skin cells, respectively. This month, a team from the Yokohama City University Graduate School of Medicine published results of a study where they reprogrammed human adult skin cells, added other cell types, and got them to grow into early-stage liver "buds." Currently, the scientists can produce about 100 of them, but the study's lead author Takanori Takebe noted that even a partial liver would require tens of thousands.

The French company Carmat is set to test an artificial device containing cow heart tissue, while researchers at Massachusetts General Hospital are developing a bioartificial heart scaffold, and MIT scientists have printed functional heart tissue from rodent cells.

Moreover, the ability to generate blood vessels in the lab from a patient's own cells may offer improved treatments for cardiovascular disease, kidney disease, and diabetes. A study conducted by Cytograft Tissue Engineering demonstrated the successful implantation of bioengineered blood vessels in three end-stage kidney disease patients, resulting in sustained function after eight months.

Surgeon Anthony Atala, director of the Wake Forest Institute for Regenerative Medicine, has made significant contributions to the field, including the development of bioengineered bladders that have shown positive results in clinical trials. His team constructed bladders from patients' cells grown on a biodegradable scaffold, which were then implanted, providing relief for children with spina bifida who risked kidney failure.

Researchers at Princeton University employed 3D printing technology to create a functional ear from calf cells and electronic materials, demonstrating the potential for reproducing complex biological structures. Additionally, the Fraunhofer Institute for Interfacial Engineering and Biotechnology has developed a system capable of manufacturing large quantities of artificial skin for grafting and toxicity testing.

reference: – Cool—and/or creepy—news out of Japan, where researchers at the University of Tokyo and Kyoto University have created a human ear ... on the back of a rat. The researchers used stem cells that grew into ear cartilage, shaping the cells into an ear by putting them in "biological tubing," Discovery News reports. More specifically, they used "induced pluripotent stem cells," which the National Institutes of Health explains are "adult cells that have been genetically reprogrammed to an embryonic stem cell-like state." The ear-shaped cartilage was implanted under a rat's skin, where it was allowed to grow for two months; the tubing dissolved and a 2-inch-long ear was left behind. The researchers believe this technology could be used to treat people who are born with no ears or with smaller-than-normal ears, Japan Today reports. It could also be used to repair ears disfigured in accidents, Discovery adds. In the case of a child born with a missing or misshapen ear, the researchers note that the ear they created is "living," and thus would develop and grow along with the child. A clinical trial involving humans is expected to start in five years. In 2013, researchers in Boston used sheep cells, bovine collagen, and an ear-shaped titanium wire frame to grow an ear on the back of another rat, Discovery notes. (These creatures can grow the heads and brains of other species.)

final_summary:
A sanitation worker was captured on camera throwing a stepladder into his truck in North Andover on Wednesday, March 23, 2016. AP Photo/Elise Amendola. Just a few days later, on Friday, April 1, 2016, the U.S. government released the U.S. jobs report for March, providing insight into the nation's economic landscape.

According to the Labor Department, American employers added 215,000 jobs in March, marking another solid month of hiring. This surge was largely driven by substantial gains in the construction, retail, and healthcare sectors. Notably, construction firms contributed 37,000 new jobs, possibly due to favorable weather conditions. Conversely, the manufacturing and mining industries experienced job losses, with the latter being affected by low oil prices, which have resulted in a loss of 185,000 positions since September 2014.

Despite the positive job additions, the unemployment rate ticked up to 5 percent from 4.9 percent, primarily due to more Americans entering the workforce, although not all found employment. Nevertheless, this uptick included some encouraging signs, as it suggested that employers remained optimistic about their business prospects, even amidst slower overall growth since last winter. Analysts estimated that the economy expanded at a 1 percent annual rate or below during the first quarter.

The steady hiring trend has led to moderate wage growth, with salaries increasing by 2.3 percent to $25.43. Although this figure has improved since the early stages of the recovery, it still lags behind the typical 3.5 percent pace seen in a strong economy. The sluggish wage growth has been a persistent concern since the end of the Great Recession in 2009.

As the economy continues to navigate a complex landscape, other indicators have been mixed. Consumer spending, a crucial driver of growth, has faltered since last winter, with a mere 0.1 percent increase in February for the third month in a row. Economists have subsequently revised their growth forecasts downward. Furthermore, Americans seem to be saving more of their savings from lower gas prices, with the savings rate reaching 5.4 percent in February, the highest level in a year.

Additionally, the housing market has shown signs of slowing down, with sales of existing homes plummeting 7.1 percent in February due to a shortage of available properties, leading to rising prices. Amidst these mixed signals, the jobs report offered a glimmer of hope, suggesting that the labor market remains resilient despite the challenges facing the broader economy.

Low oil prices have cost that industry 185,000 jobs since September 2014. Meanwhile, the MoneyBeat team at the Wall Street Journal has deemed Friday "datageddon" for all of the additional info scheduled to be released, including manufacturing data from the US, China, and Europe and an update from the University of Michigan's Consumer Sentiment Index.

final_summary:
In the quiet town of Epping, N.H., a distressing incident unfolded on Aug. 11, involving sixty-one-year-old Leslie Kahn, a breast cancer survivor who found herself trapped in her own backyard pool. The unexpected turn of events began when Leslie attempted to exit the pool after a refreshing daily swim on a day much nicer than today, only to discover that the ladder had broken beneath her weight. With her cellphone safely inside the house, Leslie was left stranded in four feet of water, unable to muster the strength to pull herself out due to her reduced agility and upper body strength.

As panic began to set in, Leslie resorted to using a pool pole to drag a nearby chair containing her iPad onto the deck, allowing her to access the device and call for help through a community Facebook page called "Epping Squawks." In a desperate bid to attract immediate attention, Leslie labeled her post "911," which quickly garnered responses from concerned neighbors and residents interviewed by WMUR-TV. 

Within minutes, a kind-hearted woman from the neighborhood arrived at Leslie's residence, soon followed by local law enforcement and another neighbor. Despite their best efforts to repair the broken ladder and devise alternative solutions, none proved successful until a step ladder was brought to the scene, enabling Leslie to finally escape the predicament unscathed.

Leslie's harrowing experience served as a poignant reminder of the importance of community support and the power of social media in connecting people during times of need. As she reflected on the ordeal, Leslie expressed gratitude towards the people of Epping, stating that she was already laughing about the situation even as it unfolded, and that there was little else she could do but appreciate the kindness shown to her.

reference: – A New Hampshire woman who became stuck in her swimming pool after the ladder broke turned to the loving arms of Facebook to ask for help getting out. "I had one foot on (the ladder), and when I put my other foot on it, it went down. And I cracked my knee," Leslie Kahn tells WBZ-TV of the Aug. 11 incident. The 61-year-old says she didn't have the upper-body strength to hoist herself out, and "my tenants were out, nobody was home. My phone was inside." With few other options, Kahn used a pool pole to drag the chair her iPad was on toward her and posted in a community Facebook page, asking for help. She said she labeled the post "911" to get people's attention. A woman who lived nearby showed up, followed by police and a neighbor. "Every idea we thought of didn’t work," she says, until her rescuers thought to try a step ladder. "And I just climbed out. Happily ever after at that point." Kahn, whom the AP notes is a breast cancer survivor, is apparently no worse for the wear: "People keep saying, 'I bet you can laugh about it now.' I was laughing about it then. What else can you do!"

final_summary:
On Monday, Aug. 25, 2014, thousands of mourners gathered at Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church in St. Louis to bid farewell to Michael Brown, the 18-year-old who was shot and killed by a police officer, Darren Wilson, on Aug. 9 in Ferguson, Missouri. As the nation grappled with the aftermath of the incident, which sparked widespread protests and raised questions about racial tensions and police brutality, the funeral served as a poignant reminder of the young man's life and aspirations. According to his family and friends, Brown was a "gentle soul" with a deepening faith in Christianity and ambitions to become a heating and air-conditioning technician. 

As the Associated Press reported, the church's sanctuary, which seated 2,500 people, was filled to capacity, with an additional 2,000 attendees waiting in overflow rooms. Many others, unable to gain entry, lined up outside, braving the sweltering heat. The atmosphere was somber, with mourners clad in black, yet infused with moments of joy and celebration as they remembered Brown's life. 

During the service, speakers reflected on Brown's personality, describing him as a kind and gentle giant who loved rap music, computer games, and the TV show "Family Guy." His family and friends shared stories of his aspirations, including his desire to one day make a name for himself. Brown's mother, Lesley McSpadden, wrote a heartfelt letter to her son, expressing her love and gratitude for the gift of his life. His father, Michael Brown Sr., penned a similar message, vowing to keep his son's memory alive in his heart.

The Rev. Al Sharpton delivered a powerful eulogy, urging the congregation to channel their emotions into a call for change, rather than violence. He emphasized the need for reform in policing practices and encouraged the crowd to exercise their right to vote. Other notable figures in attendance included Spike Lee, the Rev. Jesse Jackson, and Martin Luther King III, who came to pay their respects to the fallen youth. The White House sent three representatives to the funeral, including Broderick Johnson, Heather Foster, and Marlon Marshall. 

As the service concluded, a motorcade escorted Brown's body to St. Peter's Cemetery in St. Louis, accompanied by scores of motorcycles ridden by mourners. The event marked a turning point in the community's response to the shooting, as the town began to heal and rebuild. Despite the challenges ahead, the outpouring of support for Brown's family served as a testament to the power of unity and collective grief.

On Monday, Aug. 25, 2014, thousands of mourners gathered at Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church in St. Louis to bid farewell to Michael Brown, the 18-year-old who was shot and killed by a police officer, Darren Wilson, on Aug. 9 in Ferguson, Missouri. As the nation grappled with the aftermath of the incident, which sparked widespread protests and raised questions about racial tensions and police brutality, the funeral served as a poignant reminder of the young man's life and aspirations. According to his family and friends, Brown was a "gentle soul" with a deepening faith in Christianity and ambitions to become a heating and air-conditioning technician. 

As the Associated Press reported, the church's sanctuary, which seated 2,500 people, was filled to capacity, with an additional 2,000 attendees waiting in overflow rooms. Many others, unable to gain entry, lined up outside, braving the sweltering heat. The atmosphere was somber, with mourners clad in black, yet infused with moments of joy and celebration as they remembered Brown's life. 

During the service, speakers reflected on Brown's personality, describing him as a kind and gentle giant who loved rap music, computer games, and the TV show "Family Guy." His family and friends shared stories of his aspirations, including his desire to one day make a name for himself. Brown's mother, Lesley McSpadden, wrote a heartfelt letter to her son, expressing her love and gratitude for the gift of his life. His father, Michael Brown Sr., penned a similar message, vowing to keep his son's memory alive in his heart.

The Rev. Al Sharpton delivered a powerful eulogy, urging the congregation to channel their emotions into a call for change, rather than violence. He emphasized the need for reform in policing practices and encouraged the crowd to exercise their right to vote. Other notable figures in attendance included Spike Lee, the Rev. Jesse Jackson, and Martin Luther King III, who came to pay their respects to the fallen youth. The White House sent three representatives to the funeral, including Broderick Johnson, Heather Foster, and Marlon Marshall. 

As the service concluded, a motorcade escorted Brown's body to St. Peter's Cemetery in St. Louis, accompanied by scores of motorcycles ridden by mourners. The event marked a turning point in the community's response to the shooting, as the town began to heal and rebuild. Despite the challenges ahead, the outpouring of support for Brown's family served as a testament to the power of unity and collective grief.

reference: – More than 2,500 mourners crowded Friendly Temple Missionary Baptist Church in St. Louis this morning to lay Michael Brown to rest, reports the AP, with another 2,000 in overflow seating and national television carrying the 18-year-old's funeral. Brown's casket was closed, with a St. Louis Cardinals cap atop it, notes the Post-Dispatch. His uncle, Bernard Ewing, recalled him as a "big guy, but a kind, gentle soul" in a service that the New York Times calls "deeply religious." The overarching message was one of change, with Brown's family members urging mourners to vote, and the Rev. Al Sharpton saying, "We are required to leave here today and change things. Michael Brown must be remembered for more than disturbances." Michael Brown Sr., wearing a tie bearing his son's image, had yesterday pleaded with protesters for "a day of silence so I can, so we can, lay our son to rest." Other highlights: Among those gathered: Jesse Jackson, Martin Luther King III, Spike Lee, Rep. Maxine Waters, Rep. William Lacy Clay; family members of Trayvon Martin and Sean Bell; three White House representatives. Gov. Jay Nixon declined to attend "out of respect for the family." Sharpton on protesters who turned to violence, as per CNN: "Can you imagine, they're heartbroken, their son taken, discarded, and marginalized, and they have to stop mourning to get you to control your anger, like you're more angry than they are, like you don't understand that Michael Brown does not want to be remembered for a riot." The Rev. Charles Ewing, Brown's uncle who eulogized him: "Michael Brown's blood is crying from the ground, crying for vengeance, crying for justice. There is a cry being made from the ground, not just for Michael Brown, but for the Trayvon Martins, for those children at Sandy Hook Elementary School, for the Columbine massacre, for the black-on-black crime."

final_summary:
On Wednesday, December 2, 2014, a tragic incident occurred at the Inca citadel of Machu Picchu in the Peruvian Andes, where a 51-year-old German tourist, identified as Oliver Park, lost his life while attempting to take a photo. According to eyewitness accounts, Park had bypassed a security cordon and ventured into an off-limits area, ignoring safety warnings and signs, before tumbling to his death from a height of approximately 130 feet. 

Guillermo Mestas, a Peruvian tourist who witnessed the incident, stated that Park had asked a fellow traveler to take a photo of him mid-leap, but in the process of handing over the camera, he lost his balance and fell. Mestas noted that the park rangers were nearby, roughly 20 feet away, but unfortunately, they could not intervene in time to prevent the tragedy.

This incident highlights the risks associated with taking selfies in precarious locations, a trend that has gained significant attention globally in recent years. In 2014 alone, over one million visitors flocked to Machu Picchu, making it Peru's top tourist destination, as captured by photographer Enrique Castro-Mendivil for Reuters. However, the site's breathtaking scenery also poses significant hazards, including steep cliffs and deep ravines.

Machu Picchu, a UNESCO World Heritage site, has witnessed several fatal incidents involving tourists over the years. In 1997, an American tourist, Ingrid Mason, fell to her death after slipping on a mountain path overlooking the citadel. In 2004, a Russian tourist died after being struck by lightning while climbing the same peak. These incidents underscore the importance of adhering to safety guidelines and respecting restricted areas.

In a separate incident, a South Korean tourist fell to his death while taking a selfie at the Gocta waterfall in the Amazonas region of Peru just a day before Park's fatal accident. This incident, along with others, raises concerns about the increasing danger posed by selfie-taking tourists worldwide. From bison-related injuries in Yellowstone National Park to drowning incidents in rivers and waterfalls, the risks associated with taking selfies in scenic locations cannot be overstated.

According to reports from El Comercio, Peruvian authorities have implemented measures to mitigate these risks, including limiting the daily number of tourists to 2,500 for safety reasons and to preserve the ruins. On Thursday, Park's body was removed from the mountainside and taken by train to a morgue in Cusco. Despite these efforts, the allure of capturing the perfect shot continues to tempt tourists, often with devastating consequences. As the world grapples with the phenomenon of selfie culture, the tragic loss of Oliver Park serves as a poignant reminder of the importance of prioritizing safety over the pursuit of the perfect photograph.

reference: – It wasn't quite death-by-selfie, but it wasn't far off. Peru This Week reports that a German tourist died Wednesday while posing for a photo at Peru's famed Machu Picchu site. Oliver Park, 51, went into a restricted area of the mountainous tourist locale in the Andes, despite warning signs and instructions from wardens, reports the BBC. The news agency says that while posing for a photo at the edge of a ravine, Park apparently decided to jump in the air to make it look like he was flying. Instead, he ended up falling off the cliff. A witness gives a slightly different take to TV station Canal N, as translated by the Washington Post: "He asked a man who was there to take a photo of him," recounts the Peruvian tourist. "The man came over to take the photo and in the moment he was handing him the camera, he lost his balance and fell.” Both accounts agree that Park had ventured into an area of Machu Picchu, home to an ancient Incan citadel, that's supposed to be off limits to tourists. His body was recovered Thursday. (In 2014, Machu Picchu was plagued by streakers.)

final_summary:
Jeff Herman, a lawyer from Boca Raton, Florida, has spent nearly three decades fighting for victims of childhood sexual abuse, taking on powerful institutions like the Catholic Church, the Boy Scouts of America, and now, Hollywood. Herman's law firm, Herman Law, has handled over 800 cases involving sexual abuse, with many resulting in significant payouts, including a $21.3 million settlement from the Archdiocese of Miami.

Herman's motivation stems from his own traumatic experience as a child, when he was allegedly held by his arms and legs and subjected to a potentially invasive examination. Although he cannot recall the specifics, the memory triggers a deep-seated discomfort. Additionally, Herman's older sister revealed to him five years ago that their grandfather had sexually abused her when she was a child, forcing Herman to confront the reality of his family's dark past.

On Monday, Herman announced that he is suing X-Men director Bryan Singer and producer Gary Goddard, whom he accuses of sexually assaulting a 15-year-old boy in 2006. Herman has filed multiple lawsuits against Singer and Goddard, as well as others, citing allegations of rape, coercion, and grooming. The defendants have vehemently denied the claims, labeling Herman a publicity-seeking lawyer with a history of questionable tactics.

In a recent press conference, Herman presented photographs and emails allegedly linking Singer and Goddard to the abuse, including a picture of the victim standing next to Singer at the 2006 premiere of Superman Returns in London. Herman also shared an email from Goddard, which read, "The closest thing I have to a 'naughty' shot of you," accompanied by a photo of the victim wearing only a towel around his waist.

Herman's methods have raised eyebrows, particularly among critics who view him as a self-promoter rather than a genuine advocate for victims. However, Herman maintains that his goal is to bring attention to the issue and encourage others to come forward, rather than solely seeking financial gain. He has faced criticism from Singer's lawyer, Marty Singer, who called Herman's suits "fabricated" and "reckless."

According to The Daily Beast, Herman's crusade began with a case involving Michael Egan, who claimed he was coerced into underage sex in California and Hawaii in 1998 and 1999. Herman has since filed multiple lawsuits against Singer and Goddard, as well as others, including a case involving Elmo puppeteer Kevin Clash, whom Herman represented in a series of lawsuits alleging underage sex abuse.

Despite the backlash, Herman remains resolute in his pursuit of justice for his clients. He has vowed to continue filing lawsuits against those he believes have committed abuse, including a potential case involving another alleged sex ring in Hollywood. Herman's determination is driven by his conviction that the entertainment industry has enabled and covered up abuse for far too long, leaving victims to suffer in silence.

As Herman navigates the complex web of allegations and denials, he remains focused on giving a voice to those who have been silenced. For him, the fight against childhood sexual abuse is a personal mission, fueled by his own experiences and a desire to protect others from suffering the same trauma.

reference: – Bryan Singer has already been hit with two lawsuits alleging sexual abuse of minors—and the lawyer who filed both of them, plus other suits against Hollywood bigwigs, says more are coming. "I’ve heard from victims about very recent claims of being sexually exploited in Hollywood," Jeff Herman said at a news conference yesterday, according to Variety. He said one coming lawsuit involves another Hollywood "sex ring." Herman also shared photos showing the second alleged victim (with his face redacted) and Singer at the London premiere of Singer's Superman Returns in 2006; John Doe, then 17, says Singer abused him after the premiere, the New York Daily News reports. As for the strenuous denials from all the Hollywood VIPs named in the various suits, Herman said, "The pushback I’m getting reminds me of the early days of the clergy sex abuse scandal." Meanwhile, in an interview with the Daily Beast, Herman says he himself has vague memories of being sexually abused as a child. He says he's worked on some 800 sex abuse cases in his career, notably involving the Catholic Church and the Boy Scouts.

final_summary:
In 2014, Sundar Pichai, then the newly appointed CEO of Google, described one of the company's latest Android Nexus smartphones. However, behind the scenes, Pichai was set to receive a significant equity award from parent company Alphabet Inc., valuing nearly $200 million. According to a securities filing, Alphabet granted Pichai 273,328 Class C Google stock units, which at the time was valued at $199 million but had declined to $187 million due to market fluctuations. 

The award, which vests in quarterly increments over the next four years as long as Pichai remains employed by Alphabet, significantly increased his equity stake in the company, pushing it above $600 million. Although this amount falls short of the stakes held by co-founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin, as well as Executive Chairman Eric Schmidt, it marked one of the largest awards given by a U.S. company. 

Pichai's compensation package drew comparisons to Apple's award to then-new CEO Tim Cook in 2011, which was valued at $376 million. Meanwhile, Google's founders, Page and Brin, boasted net worths of $34.6 billion and $33.9 billion respectively, while former CEO Eric Schmidt held shares worth $3 billion. 

Notably, Pichai's award was larger than the $187 million Google agreed to pay the UK government in back taxes since 2005, despite generating an estimated $34.6 billion in profits during that period. The effective tax rate for Google in the UK was reportedly between 2% and 3%, compared to the country's 20% corporate tax rate. 

Following the announcement, Pichai took to Twitter to celebrate, sharing a photo of himself watching the Super Bowl alongside NFL legend Ronnie Lott. Other high-ranking Google executives, including CFO Ruth Porat and Diane Greene, head of Google's cloud computing business, also received substantial share awards, totaling $38 million and $42.8 million respectively.

final_summary:
On Monday, Snoop Dogg, a renowned American rapper, singer, songwriter, and television personality, received a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, marking a significant milestone in his illustrious career spanning over 25 years. The ceremony took place outside the El Capitan theater on Hollywood Boulevard, where Snoop Dogg was joined by his wife, Shante Broadus, and their children, including daughters Cori and Julian, as well as his sons Corde and Cordell.

During the ceremony, Snoop Dogg paid tribute to various individuals who have played a crucial role in his life, including his parents, his wife, and fellow celebrities such as Dr. Dre, Jimmy Kimmel, and Quincy Jones. However, in a surprising twist, Snoop Dogg chose to express gratitude towards himself, stating, "I want to thank me for believing in me... I want to thank me for doing all this hard work... I want to thank me for never quitting... I want to thank me for always being a giver, and trying to give more than I receive."

This self-praise was met with laughter and applause from the audience, which included notable figures such as Martha Stewart, who has collaborated with Snoop Dogg on the VH1 variety series "Martha & Snoop's Potluck Dinner Party." Despite her absence from the ceremony, Stewart expressed her admiration for Snoop Dogg in a previous interview with People magazine, describing him as her "freedom" and a valuable collaborator.

As the 2,651st star to be placed on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, Snoop Dogg's achievement is a testament to his enduring impact on the entertainment industry. With a career spanning over 25 years, Snoop Dogg has released 18 studio albums, including his debut album "Doggystyle," which debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 chart and sold over 800,000 copies in its first week. He has also acted in numerous films, such as "Starsky & Hutch" and "Training Day," and has hosted several television shows, including "Martha & Snoop's Potluck Dinner Party" and "Joker's Wild" on TNT.

Throughout his career, Snoop Dogg has been recognized with numerous award nominations, including 17 Grammy nominations. He has also made headlines for his entrepreneurial ventures, including co-founding Merry Jane, a marijuana-focused media company, and establishing the Snoop Youth Football League to provide opportunities for children in sports. As Ana Martinez, producer of the Hollywood Walk of Fame, noted, "Snoop Dogg is one of those people who can do anything... He is a man of the ages and we welcome him to our Hollywood Walk of Fame."

final_summary:
In the aftermath of the execution of Jang Song-thaek, the uncle of North Korea's supreme leader, Kim Jong-un, a massive crackdown on his relatives has unfolded in North Korea. According to The Daily NK, a newspaper run by defectors from the regime, hundreds of Jang's family members, including distant relatives, have been arrested and taken into custody by the Ministry of State Security in Pyongyang. The arrests began on December 13, the day after Jang's execution, with armed men arriving in the Pyongchon area of Pyongyang to detain individuals linked to the former high-ranking official.

Jang, who was married to the sister of Kim Jong-il, the nation's former supreme leader and father of Kim Jong-un, was a leading figure in North Korea before his downfall. He was charged with numerous crimes, including "gnawing at the unity and cohesion of the party," "dreaming different dreams," and "creating illusion about himself." Additionally, he faced allegations of corruption, improper relationships with women, and squandering foreign currency at casinos.

The execution of Jang marked a significant turning point in the power dynamics within North Korea's elite, with many speculating about a possible power struggle between Kim Jong-un and his uncle. However, a more nuanced understanding has since emerged, suggesting that Jang's downfall was largely due to his dominance of lucrative business deals, particularly in the coal industry, which sparked friction among the country's ruling elite.

Meanwhile, in a separate development, former NBA player Dennis Rodman, who has become an unlikely ambassador for North Korea, visited the country for the third time to train local basketball players for an exhibition game scheduled for January 8, Kim Jong-un's birthday. Despite the controversy surrounding his trips, Rodman expressed enthusiasm for the experience, describing the North Korean players as "awesome." However, his visit was marred by the withdrawal of online betting company Paddy Power, which had initially sponsored the trip, citing concerns over the North Korean regime's human rights record.

As tensions continue to simmer within North Korea, the country's authoritarian leader, Kim Jong-un, appears determined to eliminate any perceived threats to his authority. The mass arrest of Jang's relatives serves as a stark reminder of the regime's willingness to use brutal measures to maintain control and suppress dissent. With the fate of those detained uncertain, the situation remains shrouded in secrecy, leaving many to wonder about the true extent of the regime's reach and the consequences of challenging its authority in Seoul, South Korea.
