reference: – Authorities have rounded up hundreds of relatives of Kim Jong Un's recently executed uncle and ferried them to prison camps, according to a report out of Seoul. The night after Jang Song Thaek was executed, "armed men from the Ministry of State Security arrived unannounced in the Pyongchon area of Pyongyang where a lot of his relatives lived," an insider tells the Daily NK, which is run by defectors, Fox News notes. "It was not just his close relatives who were taken away, but distant members of his family, too." Observers say family members could be executed or spend life in prison (though things appear OK for Kim's aunt). In other news from Pyongyang: The Telegraph lists some of the 24 charges against Jang, which apparently included "gnawing at the unity and cohesion of the party" and "dreaming different dreams." He was also blamed for "improper relations with several women" and "squandering foreign currency at casinos." But Pyongyang's claims that Jang was executed over his plans to seize power are likely inaccurate, says South Korea's head of intelligence: The real problem was Jang and his associates' push to take control of major business deals—for instance, coal sales to China, the official said in a closed meeting, as noted by the New York Times. After Jang's rivals complained, Kim ordered Jang's associates to drop certain deals, but they refused; Kim believed his authority was being questioned. In less consequential North Korea news, Dennis Rodman has exited the country, having wrapped up his third "fun" visit. He didn't get to see his pal Kim this time around (he was training basketball players), but tells CNN, "I'm not worried about it. I'll see him again. I will be coming back in another week."

final_summary:
In a heartwarming display of love and commitment, a 103-year-old man and a 91-year-old woman became the oldest couple to get married in the world, breaking a record previously held by a French couple. George Kirby and Doreen Luckie, who have been together for 27 years, exchanged vows at a hotel in Eastbourne, a seaside town in southern England, United Kingdom, on Saturday, surrounded by close friends and family. The couple's combined age of 194 years surpassed the previous record of 191 years held by Francois Fernandez and Madeleine Francineau.

The ceremony took place on a significant day for George, as it coincided with his 103rd birthday. Doreen, looking stunning in a white dress with blue flowers, beamed with joy alongside her groom, who was dressed smartly in a suit and tie despite being confined to a wheelchair due to a recent fall. The couple, who have seven children, 15 grandchildren, and seven great-grandchildren between them, have been living together since 1988, but decided to formalize their relationship after pressure from their elderly children.

George, a former boxer who won all 60 of his fights, met Doreen at the age of 76, after he had recently divorced, while Doreen had become a widow three years prior after 36 years of marriage. Doreen, a former shorthand typist for the Director of Public Prosecutions, was born in Brixton in September 1923, while George was born in London in June 1912. The couple's love story is a testament to the power of true devotion and companionship.

Meanwhile, thousands of miles away in Sydney, Australia, hundreds of people gathered for a marriage equality rally, calling for the Australian government to follow Ireland's lead and introduce same-sex marriage. Demonstrators carried flags and placards, including one bearing the image of Christine Forster and her partner Virginia Edwards, who addressed the crowds at Taylor Square. The rally was part of a broader movement pushing for marriage equality in Australia, with many participants holding signs urging the government to hold a free vote on the issue.

As George and Doreen celebrated their new union, they reflected on their long and fulfilling life together. "It's about time, really," George said, smiling. "Doreen keeps me young, and I can definitely say we're going to spend the rest of our lives together." Doreen added, "I wasn't a blushing bride, but I was a bit giddy. It was such a lovely day – and great to see everybody."

Their son, Neil Kirby, who runs the Langham Hotel in Eastbourne, where the ceremony took place, described the occasion as "a great day" and praised the couple's love for each other. "They are such a beautiful couple," he said. "Long ago we adopted Doreen as our mum, and we're all delighted that she's now a Kirby." As the newlyweds begin their new chapter together, they will undoubtedly continue to inspire others with their enduring love and commitment to each other.

reference: – A UK couple with nearly 200 years between them tied the knot on Saturday in Britain, in a move that was a long time coming. George Kirby, 103, and Doreen Luckie, 91, are the world’s oldest married couple, according to AFP. The newlyweds have been together (and living together) for 27 years, and have a slew of children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren between them. In regards to why they waited so long to marry, Luckie said they just "didn’t want to bother." That changed when Kirby popped the question on Valentine's Day—albeit in a nontraditional way. "I didn't get down on one knee, because I don't think I would have been able to get back up," he explained. The pair married at a hotel in Eastbourne, on the southern English coast, and a Guinness Book of World Records guest was in attendance, reports the Independent. The happy couple were a combined 194 years and 281 days old on their wedding day—three years older than the previous "world’s oldest couple," Francois Fernandez and Madeleine Francineau of France. The Brits met in 1988 through a group that organizes senior friendships, and hit it off right away. Kirby was a recently divorced 76-year-old, and Luckie had been widowed three years prior. As for whether any wild pre-wedding partying occurred, Kirby's 63-year-old son had this to say: "Dad missed his stag do lunch because he wasn't feeling up for it, but Doreen had a hen party luncheon which was a complete laugh with all her golden girls." (Want to stay married til you're 103? Avoid these 5 odd things that up your chances of divorce.)

final_summary:
On September 26, 2016, customs officers at the regional airport of Graz in Austria made an unexpected discovery - human entrails in the luggage of a Moroccan traveler. According to reports from the Kleine Zeitung newspaper and APA news agency, the unusual find was made during routine inspections of luggage belonging to a woman arriving from Morocco. The intestines were carefully wrapped and placed in two separate receptacles. 

The woman, whose identity remains anonymous, claimed that the entrails belonged to her deceased husband, a Moroccan man born in 1976. She alleged that she suspected her husband had been poisoned during a family meal and sought to have a toxicology analysis conducted on the tissue by European doctors. The woman had packed the four-inch piece of intestine in her checked baggage on a flight to Graz, where she and her husband had resided for eight years.

Her lawyer, Anton Karner, stated that the woman had acted on the advice of a doctor in Marrakesh, who also suspected foul play. The doctor allegedly extracted the intestine and helped package it in formaldehyde and thick plastic containers, which were deemed "very professional" by Gerald Höfler, the head of the pathology institute in Graz, where the intestine is currently being examined. Höfler noted that the packaging met European Union standards.

Customs officials discovered the unusual cargo during standard random checks upon the woman's arrival in Graz. The Ministry of Finance, which oversees customs operations, confirmed the incident through its spokesperson, Johannes Pasquali. Initially, the police were consulted, but ultimately determined that the woman had not broken any Austrian laws by bringing the sample into the country. The intestine was subsequently sent to Dr. Höfler's clinic for further examination, with results expected soon. This bizarre incident was first reported by the Kleine Zeitung, and later picked up by international outlets such as The Local and AFP, with a photo provided by Europol.

The woman's transport of the organ was not illegal, notes the paper. (The airport isn't the only unexpected place intestines have turned up.)

final_summary:
In a historic vote, France's lower house of parliament approved a contentious bill to legalize same-sex marriage on Tuesday, marking a significant milestone in the country's ongoing struggle for LGBTQ+ rights. The legislation, which aims to grant same-sex couples the same rights as their heterosexual counterparts, including the ability to marry and adopt children, was passed by a margin of 329 votes in favor to 229 against, with 10 deputies abstaining. The bill must now be ratified by the Senate, the upper house of parliament, before it can become law.

President François Hollande, who made same-sex marriage a key campaign promise during his presidential bid last year, hailed the vote as a major victory for equality and human rights. However, the passage of the bill has been met with fierce resistance from the Roman Catholic Church and other conservative groups, who argue that it undermines traditional values and poses a threat to the institution of marriage.

The controversy surrounding the bill has sparked massive protests across France, with tens of thousands of people taking to the streets in cities like Paris to express their opposition. Despite these demonstrations, supporters of the bill, including the French gay, lesbian, and transgender rights group Inter-LGBT, have welcomed the decision as a major step towards achieving full equality for LGBTQ+ individuals.

This marks one of the biggest steps forward for French gay rights advocates in more than a decade. France joins a growing list of countries that have recognized same-sex marriage, including Spain, South Africa, and the Netherlands. Just last week, UK lawmakers took a big step toward legalizing same-sex marriage when they approved the second reading of a bill in the House of Commons, although it still requires further approval before becoming law. Meanwhile, in the United States, nine states and the District of Columbia have legalized same-sex marriage, while others continue to grapple with the issue.

The vote comes as part of a broader trend of increasing acceptance of LGBTQ+ rights globally. According to a report by the International Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Trans and Intersex Association, same-sex relations are still criminalized in 76 countries, but attitudes are shifting in many parts of the world. In the United States, polls suggest that public opinion is increasingly supportive of same-sex marriage, with a majority of Americans now in favor of the practice.

Prime Minister David Cameron supports the UK legislation, but the move has prompted widespread rebellion within his Conservative Party. As the bill moves to the Senate, opponents of the legislation have vowed to continue their fight, with a large-scale protest scheduled for March 24 in Paris. However, with the National Assembly's approval, momentum appears to be building in favor of the bill, which could potentially make France one of the most progressive nations in the world on LGBTQ+ rights.

history.draft:
In a historic vote, France's lower house of parliament, the National Assembly, approved a contentious bill to legalize same-sex marriage on Tuesday, marking a significant milestone in the country's ongoing struggle for LGBTQ+ rights. The legislation, which aims to grant same-sex couples the same rights as their heterosexual counterparts, including the ability to marry and adopt children, was passed by a margin of 329 votes in favor to 229 against, with 10 deputies abstaining. The bill must now be ratified by the Senate, the upper house of parliament, before it can become law.

President François Hollande, who made same-sex marriage a key campaign promise during his presidential bid last year, hailed the vote as a major victory for equality and human rights. However, the passage of the bill has been met with fierce resistance from the Roman Catholic Church and other conservative groups, who argue that it undermines traditional values and poses a threat to the institution of marriage.

The controversy surrounding the bill has sparked massive protests across France, with tens of thousands of people taking to the streets in cities like Paris to express their opposition. Despite these demonstrations, supporters of the bill, including the French gay, lesbian, and transgender rights group Inter-LGBT, have welcomed the decision as a major step towards achieving full equality for LGBTQ+ individuals.

France joins a growing list of countries that have recognized same-sex marriage, including Spain, South Africa, and the Netherlands. In neighboring Britain, lawmakers in the House of Commons recently voted in favor of a similar bill, although it still requires further approval before becoming law. Meanwhile, in the United States, nine states and the District of Columbia have legalized same-sex marriage, while others continue to grapple with the issue.

The vote comes as part of a broader trend of increasing acceptance of LGBTQ+ rights globally. According to a report by the International Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Trans and Intersex Association, same-sex relations are still criminalized in 76 countries, but attitudes are shifting in many parts of the world. In the United States, polls suggest that public opinion is increasingly supportive of same-sex marriage, with a majority of Americans now in favor of the practice.

As the bill moves to the Senate, opponents of the legislation have vowed to continue their fight, with a large-scale protest scheduled for March 24 in Paris. However, with the National Assembly's approval, momentum appears to be building in favor of the bill, which could potentially make France one of the most progressive countries in Europe on LGBTQ+ rights.

final_summary:
Today, Rep. Pete Sessions (R-Texas) announced that he was ending his bid to become the next House majority leader, paving the way for Rep. Kevin McCarthy (R-Calif.) to assume the role. Sessions, the current House Rules Committee chairman and former head of the House GOP campaign committee, cited concerns about creating unnecessary division within the party as the reason for his withdrawal. In a statement, Sessions expressed his commitment to working with the Republican conference to advance the conservative agenda demanded by the American people.

The move comes after Rep. Eric Cantor (R-Va.) lost his primary election on Tuesday, sparking a leadership vacuum in the House. Sessions had begun seeking support for the majority leader position shortly after Cantor's defeat, but ultimately found himself unable to overcome McCarthy's established advantage as the party's chief vote counter and top fundraiser. 

McCarthy's prospects were bolstered by Speaker John Boehner's (Ohio) decision to schedule a secret ballot election for today, giving him a significant head start in securing support among House Republicans. Additionally, several prominent conservatives, including Reps. Paul Ryan (Wis.), Tom Price (Ga.), and Jim Jordan (Ohio), opted not to challenge McCarthy for the position.

While Rep. Raúl Labrador (R-Idaho) has been considering a potential challenge to McCarthy, it remains unclear whether he can mount a serious threat to the Californian congressman. Meanwhile, the competition for the position of majority whip has intensified, with Rep. Peter Roskam (Ill.) facing off against Rep. Steve Scalise (La.) and Rep. Marlin Stutzman (Ind.).

In Washington, Sessions' decision to drop out of the running leaves McCarthy as the sole remaining candidate for the majority leader position, setting him up to succeed Cantor, who will remain in office until July 31 to facilitate a smooth transition.

reference: – Unless a surprise challenger emerges at the last minute, the race to replace Eric Cantor as the House's second-ranking Republican is already over. House Rules Committee chief Pete Sessions says he has dropped his bid to become majority leader "after thoughtful consideration and discussion" with colleagues, leaving Majority Whip Kevin McCarthy as the only lawmaker known to be seeking the position, reports Reuters. Cantor—the first majority leader ever to lose a primary—is stepping down at the end of July following his shock defeat at the hands of little-known Tea Party challenger David Brat. "It became obvious to me that the measures necessary to run a successful campaign would have created unnecessary and painful division within our party," Sessions said. The leadership election will be held by secret ballot next week but the more competitive race is now the one to succeed McCarthy as whip, the Hill finds. McCarthy's chief deputy, Rep. Peter Roskam, is in the running, along with Rep. Steve Scalise, chairman of the conservative Republican Study Committee, and Rep. Marlin Stutzman, a hardline conservative who was punished last year for defying House leaders, the Indianapolis Star notes.

final_summary:
Newt Gingrich, a former Speaker of the House, has been wrapping himself in the mantle of Ronald Reagan throughout his presidential campaign, claiming to have worked with the president to change Washington and defeat communism. However, this portrayal is misleading, as Gingrich frequently criticized Reagan, his top aides, and his policies to defeat communism during his early years in Congress. Despite voting with the president regularly, Gingrich's rhetoric was consistently negative, and he was proven wrong in his predictions that Reagan's policies would fail.

During the Reagan administration's efforts to counter Soviet expansionism in the Third World, Gingrich was not a reliable ally. He criticized Reagan's policies and meetings with Soviet leaders, including a particularly scathing assessment of the president's meeting with Mikhail Gorbachev, comparing it to the infamous meeting between Hitler and Chamberlain in 1938. Meanwhile, other lawmakers, such as Tom DeLay, Henry Hyde, Dick Cheney, and Dan Burton, were vocal supporters of Reagan's efforts.

Fast-forward to the present, and Gingrich is now seeking the Republican nomination, positioning himself as a conservative alternative to Mitt Romney. However, several prominent conservatives, including Tom DeLay, Bob Dole, and Ann Coulter, have spoken out against Gingrich, citing his lack of leadership skills, erratic behavior, and questionable character. DeLay described Gingrich as "not really a conservative" who "will tell you what you want to hear" but lacks discipline and consistency.

Coulter, meanwhile, has accused Gingrich of being a "Clintonian fraud" and compared him unfavorably to Romney, who she believes is the most conservative candidate left in the field. Coulter notes that Romney has consistently held conservative positions, including opposing abortion, supporting entitlement reform, and rejecting amnesty for illegal immigrants. In contrast, Gingrich has flip-flopped on several key issues, including his stance on abortion, healthcare, and immigration.

Gingrich's critics also point to his lucrative consulting work with Freddie Mac, which earned him $1.6 million, and his support for a federal individual mandate to purchase health insurance, which he later reversed. Additionally, Gingrich has been accused of hypocrisy for advocating for a "guest worker" program that would import a slave labor force from Mexico, while Romney has proposed a more limited approach to immigration reform.

Rick Santorum, another contender for the Republican nomination, has also been mentioned alongside Gingrich and Romney as a conservative alternative. Santorum has been praised for his commitment to conservative values, although he has been criticized for his stance on immigration. Romney, meanwhile, has emerged as the most conservative candidate left in the field, with a track record of holding conservative positions and a reputation for being a self-made businessman.

Obama, the incumbent president, will likely be a major factor in the general election, and Gingrich's ability to appeal to independents and moderate voters will be crucial in determining his chances of success. Gingrich's lack of leadership skills, inconsistent record, and questionable character have raised concerns among many conservatives, making it increasingly difficult for him to secure the nomination.

reference: – The conservative media have apparently figured out that Newt Gingrich could actually win the Republican nomination, and they are responding with a breathtaking collective attack on the candidate. Politico rounds up the highlights, noting that "this is just a sampling of what's hitting Newt." In the National Review, Elliott Abrams writes that Gingrich often undermined Ronald Reagan, in what Politico calls "a devastating takedown." In another recent National Review piece being circulated by conservatives, the editors ramp up their opposition to Gingrich and urge conservatives not to support him. R. Emmett Tyrrell Jr. of the American Spectator compares Gingrich to Bill Clinton, calling them both "1960s generation narcissists" and noting that, for "girl hopping" Gingrich, "there are private encounters with the fair sex that doubtless will come out." On her website, Ann Coulter minces no words: "Re-elect Obama, Vote Newt!" Her column blasts Gingrich's "hotheaded arrogance." The Drudge Report linked to the Abrams piece with a banner reading, "Insider: Gingrich Repeatedly Insulted Reagan." The site, which has been viewed as pro-Romney in the past, also linked prominently to Tyrrell and Coulter. Tom DeLay told KTRH, "He’s not really a conservative. I mean, he’ll tell you what you want to hear. … When he was speaker, he was erratic, undisciplined." A top figure in the conservative media tells Politico that Republican leaders and media are beginning to realize, "It could happen, and it would be a disaster. All of us who were around and saw how he operated as speaker—there’s no one who’s not appalled by the prospect of what could happen." Politico notes that dozens of GOP lawmakers, governors, and other VIPs have said the same, privately, in recent weeks. Click for more on why tonight's debate is crucial for Gingrich.

final_summary:
Janice Sage, the retiring owner of the historic 210-year-old Center Lovell Inn and Restaurant in Maine, sparked controversy when she awarded the property to a US Virgin Islands couple, Prince and Rose Adams, through an essay contest. The couple, originally from New York City, traded the Caribbean breezes of St. John for the rigors of Maine's four seasons after winning the contest. The contest, which drew over 7,000 entries, required participants to submit a 200-word essay along with a $125 entry fee. According to Sage, the couple was chosen from the pool of applicants by a local panel of judges assembled by her, but critics claim the contest was rigged in favor of the Adamses due to their background as restaurant owners.

Kelley Prass Collins, founder of the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, alleged that Sage's advertising for the contest was deceptive and violated consumer rights regulations. Collins claimed that Sage misled contestants by stating that no advantage would be given to restaurant or inn owners, yet ultimately awarded the prize to a pair with extensive experience in the industry. 

Maine State Police launched an investigation into the matter, focusing on whether the contest violated state law regarding games of skill. Under this law, operators of such games are prohibited from partially or completely controlling the outcome. Entrants were led to believe that the winner would be selected by an independent panel of judges, but critics argue that the outcome was predetermined.

Sage, who won the inn herself through a similar contest 22 years ago, maintained that she received fewer than the desired 7,500 entries and used the funds generated from the contest to secure her retirement. The Adamses plan to close their Caribbean-style restaurant on St. John and relocate to Center Lovell, where they aim to reopen the inn by July 10, taking advantage of the area's changing seasons. The Center Lovell Inn offers spectacular views of the White Mountains.

As the investigation unfolds, Sergeant Michael Johnston of the Maine State Police's special investigations unit emphasized that the inquiry is limited to determining whether state law was broken, rather than exploring potential federal regulations or civil liabilities. Meanwhile, Collins advised contestants to seek private counsel to explore alternative avenues for redress.

reference: – Maine State Police are investigating whether an innkeeper violated state law in an essay contest with her 210-year-old country inn as the prize. Maine Department of Public Safety spokesman Steve McCausland said today an investigation was opened into whether the "Win an Inn" contest violated laws governing games of chance. The Boston Globe first reported that some contest losers felt the odds were stacked against them, contending that Center Lovell Inn owner Janice Sage marketed the contest to "dreamers" but instead awarded the prize to a couple with hospitality business experience in the Virgin Islands. "One of the many allegations against Janice Sage and the contest she sponsored is that the advertising of the contest ... was illegally deceptive and violated consumer-rights regulations, intentionally coercing thousands of people to enter a contest that they never had an actual chance of winning," says the founder of the Center Lovell Contest Fair Practices Commission, a group made up of dozens of unhappy contestants. The entry fee was $125. Sage told the Globe she received fewer than the 7,500 entries she sought, but it was still enough to fund her retirement.

final_summary:
Today, the United States and China, the world's two largest economies and emitters of greenhouse gases, have pledged to significantly reduce their carbon emissions in a bid to combat climate change. As the two countries account for about 40 percent of the world's emissions, their collective efforts are crucial in addressing this pressing issue. According to President Obama, even if the US were to eliminate all domestic greenhouse gas emissions, it would not be sufficient to offset the carbon pollution emanating from China and the rest of the world, highlighting the importance of a collaborative approach.

The joint announcement comes ahead of the UN's deadline for countries to submit their emissions reduction targets under the framework of the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change in 2015. The US and China aim to encourage other nations to follow suit and contribute to a robust global climate agreement, which is expected to be finalized in Paris next year. The commitment made by the two leaders sends a strong signal that a comprehensive deal is achievable and necessary.

This development marks a significant milestone in the US-China relationship, building upon the establishment of the US-China Climate Change Working Group in Beijing last year. The working group was formed to address the shared concerns of climate change and the responsibility of the world's largest economies to lead the way towards a low-carbon future.

The US has set a target to reduce its net greenhouse gas emissions by 26 to 28 percent below 2005 levels by 2025, doubling the pace of carbon reductions compared to the period from 2005 to 2020. This ambitious goal is grounded in a thorough analysis of emission-reducing opportunities across various sectors, with projected benefits for public health, clean air, and energy security. The US has already made notable strides in reducing emissions, with wind energy production tripling and solar energy increasing tenfold since President Obama took office.

China, meanwhile, has announced plans to cap its carbon emissions by around 2030, with a goal of reaching the peak earlier if possible. The country aims to increase the proportion of its energy consumption derived from zero-emission sources to about 20 percent by 2030, requiring the deployment of an additional 800-1000 gigawatts of nuclear, wind, solar, and other renewable energy capacity. This represents a substantial undertaking, equivalent to the current capacity of all coal-fired power plants in China or nearly as much as the total electricity generation capacity of the US.

As the world's two largest polluters, the US and China's joint pledge serves as a catalyst for other nations to commit to similar reductions, ultimately driving the global transition towards cleaner energy economies. With the Lima climate talks resuming in less than three weeks and the Paris conference scheduled for next year, this breakthrough marks a critical turning point in the fight against climate change.

history.draft:
Today, the United States and China, the world's two largest economies and emitters of greenhouse gases, have pledged to significantly reduce their carbon emissions in a bid to combat climate change. As the two countries account for about 40 percent of the world's emissions, their collective efforts are crucial in addressing this pressing issue. According to President Obama, even if the US were to eliminate all domestic greenhouse gas emissions, it would not be sufficient to offset the carbon pollution emanating from China and the rest of the world, highlighting the importance of a collaborative approach.

The joint announcement comes ahead of the UN's deadline for countries to submit their emissions reduction targets under the framework of the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change in 2015. The US and China aim to encourage other nations to follow suit and contribute to a robust global climate agreement, which is expected to be finalized in Paris next year. The commitment made by the two leaders sends a strong signal that a comprehensive deal is achievable and necessary.

This development marks a significant milestone in the US-China relationship, building upon the establishment of the US-China Climate Change Working Group in Beijing last year. The working group was formed to address the shared concerns of climate change and the responsibility of the world's largest economies to lead the way towards a low-carbon future.

The US has set a target to reduce its net greenhouse gas emissions by 26 to 28 percent below 2005 levels by 2025, doubling the pace of carbon reductions compared to the period from 2005 to 2020. This ambitious goal is grounded in a thorough analysis of emission-reducing opportunities across various sectors, with projected benefits for public health, clean air, and energy security. The US has already made notable strides in reducing emissions, with wind energy production tripling and solar energy increasing tenfold since President Obama took office.

China, meanwhile, has announced plans to cap its carbon emissions by around 2030, with a goal of reaching the peak earlier if possible. The country aims to increase the proportion of its energy consumption derived from zero-emission sources to about 20 percent by 2030, requiring the deployment of an additional 800-1000 gigawatts of nuclear, wind, solar, and other renewable energy capacity. This represents a substantial undertaking, equivalent to the current capacity of all coal-fired power plants in China or nearly as much as the total electricity generation capacity of the US.

As the world's two largest polluters, the US and China's joint pledge serves as a catalyst for other nations to commit to similar reductions, ultimately driving the global transition towards cleaner energy economies. With the Lima climate talks resuming in less than three weeks and the Paris conference scheduled for next year, this breakthrough marks a critical turning point in the fight against climate change.

reference: – The US and China rank as the world's two largest polluters, but a surprise deal is turning them into the world's two largest pollution fighters: The nations announced that they'll be working in tandem on long-term greenhouse-gas controls that would cut emissions by nearly a third over the next 15 years or so, CNN reports. "As the world's two largest economies, energy consumers, and emitters of greenhouse gases, we have a special responsibility to lead the global effort against climate change," President Obama said in a joint press conference with Chinese President Xi Jinping today at the end of the APEC summit. The move, which was months in the making, came as a surprise to experts expecting a much more limited scope, the Wall Street Journal reports. Calling it a "game changer," Mother Jones notes that it could heavily influence next year's climate talks in Paris. Key mandates include the US reducing emissions by 26% to 28% by the year 2025 (compared to the year 2005), as well as doubling the rate of emissions reduction each year between 2020 and 2025 to an average 2.3% to 2.8%, the AP reports. China is pledging to stop increasing emissions by 2030 (even earlier, if possible), as well as increase energy from zero-emission sources such as wind, solar, and nuclear to 20% by 2030. Although some worry about recent GOP gains in government as a possible roadblock, a senior administration official tells CNN that "we believe we can proceed with the authority we already have." Secretary of State John Kerry writes in a New York Times op-ed that "this is … a milestone in the United States-China relationship," a "fresh beginning," and "the first step toward a world that is more prosperous and more secure."

final_summary:
This year's 2015 Academy Award nominations have sparked widespread disappointment and frustration within the film industry, particularly with regards to the notable absence of Ava DuVernay, the acclaimed director of the critically acclaimed film Selma, from the list of nominees for Best Director. Despite receiving widespread acclaim and numerous awards nominations, including a Golden Globe nomination for Best Director, DuVernay failed to secure a spot among the five finalists for the prestigious honor. This decision has been met with criticism and anger from many in the industry, who argue that DuVernay's exclusion sends a damaging message about the Academy's commitment to diversity.

DuVernay's omission from the Best Director category is particularly striking given the film's significant impact and influence, as well as its timely release in a year marked by heightened awareness and activism surrounding issues of racial justice and equality. Selma, which tells the story of the pivotal events leading up to the 2014 film's depiction of the Selma to Montgomery marches, features outstanding performances from its cast, including David Oyelowo as Martin Luther King Jr., and has been widely praised for its historical accuracy and emotional resonance.

Furthermore, DuVernay's exclusion from the Best Director category raises questions about the Academy's priorities and values. With a membership comprised of 93% white individuals and 76% men, the Academy has long faced criticism for its lack of diversity and representation. This year's nominations seem to reinforce this trend, with a notable absence of minority-led films and filmmakers. As pointed out by the Academy's own president, Cheryl Boone Isaacs, this lack of diversity is particularly concerning given the growing importance of diverse voices and perspectives in the entertainment industry.

The snubbing of DuVernay and Selma has also sparked concerns about the potential impact on the film's legacy and DuVernay's future career prospects. As a black female director in a predominantly white and male-dominated industry, DuVernay faces unique challenges and biases that can limit her opportunities and hinder her ability to secure funding and support for her projects. The Academy's decision to overlook her work may perpetuate these systemic barriers and reinforce the notion that women and minorities are not valued or respected in the industry.

Other notable omissions from the Best Director category include the absence of Steve McQueen, who directed the critically acclaimed 12 Years a Slave, which won several Academy Awards in 2014. Additionally, the nominations for Best Actor overlooked David Oyelowo's powerful performance as Martin Luther King Jr. in Selma, instead favoring white male actors such as Steve Carell, Bradley Cooper, Benedict Cumberbatch, Michael Keaton, and Eddie Redmayne.

The Academy's decision to recognize American Sniper, a film produced by Paramount and directed by Clint Eastwood, with multiple nominations, including Best Actor and Best Editing, has also raised eyebrows. Meanwhile, Selma, which tackles complex and sensitive topics such as racism and police brutality, received only two nominations, for Best Original Song and Best Picture. The film's omission from the Best Director category is particularly striking considering the success of Jennifer Aniston in the film Cake, which garnered several nominations, including a Golden Globe nomination for Best Actress, but ultimately failed to receive an Oscar nomination.

As the film industry continues to grapple with issues of diversity and representation, the snubbing of Ava DuVernay and Selma serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing challenges and biases that persist within the Academy and the broader industry.

reference: – The Oscar nominations come once a year, and so too the avalanche of stories on the biggest snubs. But in terms of 2015, Richard Lawson at Vanity Fair puts it thusly: "Don’t the nominations this year feel so wrong?" Our roundup of this year's particularly vocal snub-coverage: Director Phil Lord is grabbing a ton of headlines for his "touché" response to the Lego Movie somehow not being nominated for Best Animated Feature: "It's okay. Made my own!" he tweeted along with an image of an Oscar made entirely from yellow Legos. He's nearing 12,000 retweets. How Jezebel sums it up: 2015 Oscar Nominations Snub Basically Everybody Who Isn't a White Dude. Mashable notes "this will be the whitest group since 1998." In that vein, Scott Mendelson at Forbes zeros in on one "egregious" omission: Selma director Ava DuVernay. His lengthy piece explores why her snub matters. Mashable notes that no black female director has ever gotten a nod; only three black male directors have (John Singleton, Boyz n the Hood; Lee Daniels, Precious; Steve McQueen, 12 Years a Slave). None won. Lawson looks beyond Selma's Best Director snub, expanding it: "That the movie’s wonderful acting, directing, writing, editing, and cinematography were shut out is a real oversight, sad in the story it tells and troubling in how grimly predictable it was." (Its only nods were Best Picture and Best Original Song.) For a meaty rundown, check out Huffington Post's list of today's 29 biggest snubs.

final_summary:
In the Siberian city of Irkutsk, Russia, a devastating outbreak of mass poisoning has left a trail of death and destruction in its wake. At least 58 people have lost their lives after consuming a highly toxic substance masquerading as a safe alcoholic drink, known as "Hawthorn", which was actually a bath oil containing methanol. The incident has been described as the worst such case in modern Russian history.

According to Irkutsk region health minister Oleg Yaroshenko, nearly half of those still being treated are not expected to survive, with 13 individuals in a very serious condition and their chances of survival virtually nil. The victims, ranging in age from 25 to 62, included a doctor and a kindergarten teacher, highlighting the indiscriminate nature of the tragedy. Many of the victims were discovered in their homes, having succumbed to the poison before they could seek medical help.

Survivor Alexei, 33, recounted his harrowing experience, revealing that he only consumed a small amount of the lotion but woke up blind the next morning. He attributed his quick thinking and medical knowledge as the reason he sought immediate medical attention, unlike many others who succumbed to the poison.

The investigation revealed that the black marketeers behind the poisonous alcohol were producing several brands of vodka in addition to the dodgy bath lotion. The labels on the bottles claimed to contain ethyl alcohol, but in reality, they were laced with methanol, a highly toxic substance found in antifreeze.

Methanol poisoning can cause severe symptoms, including nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, breathing difficulties, dizziness, blindness, and coma. Even a small amount, equivalent to just over a tablespoon, can be fatal. The National Institutes of Health (NIH) noted that antidotes exist to reverse the effects of methanol, but prompt medical treatment is crucial for recovery.

People aged between 35 and 50 were disproportionately affected by the poisoning. As part of the crackdown, police in Irkutsk seized approximately 500 liters of bath oil and other liquids from stores in the area, while also uncovering an illicit workshop where the lotion was being manufactured. The Siberian Times reported that the mass poisoning has sparked a state of emergency in the city, with authorities working tirelessly to identify those responsible for the tragedy.

Photographs taken by EPA and published in various outlets, including Vesti.Irkutsk and Channel 1, show the aftermath of the disaster, with images of the confiscated bath oil and vodka bottles serving as stark reminders of the devastating consequences of the black market trade. The incident has highlighted the alarming prevalence of surrogate alcohol consumption in Russia, with up to 12 million people reportedly resorting to household products as a cheap alternative to legitimate liquor.

The incident serves as a grim reminder of the dangers of unregulated markets and the importance of proper labeling and regulation to prevent such tragedies from occurring in the future.

reference: – "They came to doctors too late. … Only a miracle can save them." Those were the words to the Siberian Times from the Irkutsk region's health minister regarding those sickened from ingesting a deadly bath oil, with dozens already dead from the mass poisoning—said to be the "worst such case in modern Russian history." The BBC reports at least 58 have died, per local media, and notes the death toll is expected to rise, as many of the more than three dozen poisoned are in serious condition. The concoction that caused the deaths was a black-market mixture promoted as a safe, hawthorn-scented alcoholic beverage, with a label that said it contained ethyl alcohol, which is OK to consume. However, it actually was made with methanol, which Live Science notes is a harmful chemical used in antifreeze that (besides death) can also cause a slew of other medical problems, including blindness. A market worker tells Reuters she usually uses the oil to remove grease and remnants of old price tags still stuck on surfaces. The BBC—which notes millions of cash-strapped people in Russia often resort to drinking cheap "surrogate" booze, including perfume and window cleaner—says the owners of the shop where the bath oil was made have been arrested. (Beer wasn't officially considered alcohol in Russia until a few years ago.)

final_summary:
On Thursday, November 27, 2014, Mexican President Enrique Pena Nieto unveiled a comprehensive anti-crime plan aimed at tackling lawlessness and corruption in the country's law enforcement agencies. This move comes in response to the public outcry over the disappearance and suspected massacre of 43 college students who were detained by municipal police in the southern town of Iguala, Guerrero. The incident occurred two months earlier. President Pena Nieto addressed the nation from the National Palace in Mexico City, announcing a series of measures designed to address the entrenched problems plaguing Mexico's law enforcement.

At the heart of the proposal lies a plan to bring municipal police forces under the control of state governments, effectively dismantling the current system of autonomous local policing. This reform aims to curb corruption, particularly among municipal police, who are often coerced or bribed by powerful drug cartels. The president's initiative was influenced by the brutal treatment meted out to the missing students, whose fate remains shrouded in mystery. The incident has sparked widespread outrage, with relatives of the students leading mass protests across the country, demanding justice and accountability.

Pena Nieto's plan also seeks to simplify the complex division of powers between federal, state, and local authorities, allowing for a more cohesive approach to combating organized crime. Furthermore, the proposal includes the establishment of a single, nationwide emergency hotline and the issuance of national identification numbers or documents to citizens. The president emphasized that these reforms would be implemented in the country's most troubled regions, including Guerrero, Michoacan, Jalisco, and Tamaulipas, where federal police and military presence would be increased.

However, experts have expressed skepticism regarding the effectiveness of these measures, citing previous failed attempts to tackle similar issues. Security analyst Alejandro Hope questioned the feasibility of the plan, stating that it may follow the pattern of previous initiatives that were short-lived and ineffective. Law professor Pedro Torres echoed this sentiment, emphasizing that concrete actions rather than mere announcements are needed to restore trust in the government's commitment to addressing the crisis.

The president's announcement coincided with the discovery of 11 decapitated bodies, reportedly members of the Ardillos gang, found on a road near the Guerrero city of Chilapa. This gruesome find serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing violence and brutality that plagues Mexico. The mayor of Iguala, Jose Luis Abarca, has been arrested and accused of collaborating with a local drug gang, which allegedly orchestrated the disappearance of the students.

As the situation continues to unfold, President Pena Nieto faces mounting pressure to deliver tangible results. The proposed reforms, which will be formally presented to Congress next week, aim to revamp the country's law enforcement apparatus and restore faith in the government's ability to protect its citizens. Despite the challenges ahead, the president's determination to address the crisis is evident, as he declared, "Mexico must change."

The proposed reforms, which will be formally presented to Congress next week, aim to revamp the country's law enforcement apparatus and restore faith in the government's ability to protect its citizens. Despite the challenges ahead, the president's determination to address the crisis is evident, as he declared, "Mexico must change."

reference: – Mexican President Pena Nieto has promised sweeping reforms to the country's police amid nationwide outrage over the massacre of 43 students abducted by a local police force working with gang members. "Mexico must change," he said yesterday, unveiling plans to dissolve all 1,800 of the country's local police forces—many of which are notoriously corrupt and infiltrated by gang members—and replace them with state-wide forces, the BBC reports. The president's proposal would also allow the federal government to take over town governments corrupted by cartels. Most of Mexico's police officers belong to local forces and most of them make less than $500 a month, encouraging the corruption Pena Nieto aims to stamp out. But critics say the president is merely repacking initiatives already in progress and trying to repeat a few failed ones, and question is whether things will be any better if the police are put under unified control. "The root problem is the quality of the police, not who commands them," a public security expert tells the Wall Street Journal. "Many state forces are also a disaster." The president's plan will focus on four of the most violent states at first, including Guerrero, where the students were kidnapped and where 11 decapitated bodies were found dumped by a road yesterday, the AP reports.

final_summary:
A unique funeral service was held at Geisen Funeral Home in Crown Point on Saturday for Terry Wayne Ward, a man who couldn't give a damn about most material things. The service was a fitting tribute to Ward's unconventional personality, as described in his obituary, which was penned by his daughter, Jean Lahm. Lahm, who worked at Geisen Funeral Home in Lowell, chose to write a humorous obituary that captured her father's essence, much to the delight of his family and the community.

Ward's obituary, which went viral on social media, highlighted his quirky nature and sense of humor. It mentioned his 39-year tenure at AT&T, where he accumulated approximately 3,000 rolls of black electrical tape, using them for various purposes, including covering open wounds and labeling buttons. The obituary also revealed that Ward never owned a personal cell phone and had no knowledge of the Kardashians, showcasing his down-to-earth and old-fashioned demeanor.

The funeral service itself was a celebration of Ward's life, filled with laughter and stories about his adventures. Lahm shared several anecdotes, including one about a chance encounter with her father while he was working on a project at the Lake Village substation in Newton County. When she stopped to chat with him, he quipped, "It's just times like these that I wish I wasn't colorblind." This lighthearted exchange encapsulated Ward's playful and witty personality.

Lahm's decision to write a humorous obituary paid off, as it brought people together and created a sense of community around her father's passing. The service attracted numerous attendees, including a couple from Mundelein, Illinois, who had been inspired by Ward's obituary and wanted to pay their respects. They left a heartfelt note expressing their regret for not meeting Ward while he was alive.

In the aftermath of the funeral, Ward's obituary continued to resonate with people, inspiring a wave of tributes and condolences. Over 700 people signed the online guestbook accompanying the obituary, and a floral arrangement was sent from a stranger in Pennsylvania with a touching message, "There Are More of Us Out There." These gestures demonstrated the profound impact of Ward's obituary, which transcended his immediate family and friends to touch the lives of strangers.

As Jean Lahm reflected on the experience, she emphasized the importance of celebrating life and finding humor in difficult situations. Her father's obituary served as a testament to this approach, reminding everyone who read it that laughter and joy can be powerful tools for healing and connection. Terry Ward, a veteran of the Vietnam War, passed away on Jan. 23, and would have celebrated his 70th birthday on April 7.

reference: – "He lived to make other people laugh," says an Indiana woman of her father, who died last Tuesday after suffering a stroke. Terry Ward is now generating plenty of chuckles thanks to the obituary daughter Jean Lahn wrote for the Army veteran. Fox59 reports she's a little more familiar with obits than most, as she works at a funeral home where she comes across plenty of routine and formulaic remembrances. The 71-year-old doesn't get that from the get-go. His obit kicks off by saying Ward "escaped this mortal realm ... leaving behind 32 jars of Miracle Whip, 17 boxes of Hamburger Helper and multitudes of other random items that would prove helpful in the event of a zombie apocalypse." She charts his progress from high school ("only three of his teachers took an early retirement after having had him as a student") to marriage ("in the fall of 1969, perfectly between the Summer of Love and the Winter of Regret") to his 39 years of work with telephone companies, during which "he accumulated roughly 3,000 rolls of black electrical tape." Lahn wrote that he "despised 'uppity foods' like hummus, which his family lovingly called 'bean dip' for his benefit, which he loved consequently." Speaking of loves, his included "shooting guns, Bed Bath & Beyond, starlight mints, cold beer, free beer, The History Channel, CCR ... and above all, his family." As for those who loved him, they're invited to make donations in his name to "your favorite watering hole, where you are instructed to tie a few on and tell a few stories of the great Terry Ward." Read the obit in full here, or read some more tales about him, courtesy of the Post-Tribune.

final_summary:
On Monday, February 08, 2017, by Kevin Byrne, AccuWeather staff writer, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) unveiled the first high-resolution images captured by its newly-launched GOES-16 satellite, previously known as GOES-R. Launched on November 19 from Cape Canaveral, Florida, the $1.2 billion satellite marks a significant milestone in weather forecasting, boasting the capability to send back data faster and generate images in higher resolution, ultimately leading to more accurate predictions.

According to NOAA officials, the satellite's Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) instrument, built by Harris Corporation, produces images at four times the resolution of existing GOES spacecraft, allowing forecasters to pinpoint the location of severe weather with greater precision. The satellite can capture a full image of the Earth every 15 minutes and one of the continental U.S. every five minutes, scanning the planet at five times the speed of NOAA's current GOES imagers.

The first images, taken on January 15, 2017, showcase the satellite's capabilities, providing a clear view of the Western Hemisphere, including the U.S., Mexico, and parts of the Caribbean. The images also highlight the potential of the satellite in tracking severe weather events, such as storms and wildfires. According to Stephen Volz, Director of NOAA's Satellite and Information Service, the images demonstrate the satellite's capacity to provide "fantastically rich" data, enabling the development of life-saving forecasts.

In comparison to its predecessor, GOES-13, the new satellite boasts five times greater coverage, four times the spatial resolution, and three times the spectral channels, making it a game-changer in the field of weather forecasting. The satellite's enhanced capabilities will enable forecasters to issue more accurate warnings and predictions, saving lives and mitigating the impact of severe weather events.

As part of the GOES series, the GOES-16 satellite will be operational by November 2017, with the planned location to be announced in May. The satellite will be used to generate new or improved meteorological, solar, and space weather products using its six new instruments. The success of GOES-16 will pave the way for future satellites in the series, including GOES-S, which is currently undergoing environmental testing at Lockheed Martin's facility in Littleton, Colorado.
anchors: The National Oceanic And Atmospheric Administration

reference: – GOES-16, the fancy new satellite developed by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, is sending its first photos back to Earth since it lifted off from Cape Canaveral on Nov. 19, and the high-res results are causing astronomers and meteorologists to squeal with delight. One developer compared it to seeing a newborn baby's first pics—"it's that exciting for us," NOAA reports. Space.com calls the super high-res images "jaw-dropping," while NASA reports that the first images, from 22,300 miles up, catch details including dust blowing off the coast of Africa, shallow waters in the Caribbean, and mountain wave clouds in Argentina. (See a gallery here.) The hope is that viewing our own planet and the atmosphere around it in such detail ("high definition from the heavens," per NOAA) will provide data for more accurate weather forecasts that could save lives. AccuWeather reports that the "stunning" images boast resolution that is four times greater than the capability of the best current satellites. NOAA says the second (GOES-S) in a series of four sophisticated satellites is undergoing a year of testing at Lockheed Martin’s Corporation facility in Littleton, Colo., and NASA reports that it is expected to launch in 2018. GOES-16 captures a full image of Earth every 15 minutes and one of the continental US every five minutes, and it can scan five times faster than current GOES imagers. (Here's how NASA scouts asteroids.)

final_summary:
On a recent Friday afternoon, Darnell Barton, a 37-year-old driver for the Niagara Frontier Transportation Authority's (NFTA) Metro bus system, witnessed a distressing scene while driving the Elmwood 20 bus toward Buffalo State College in Buffalo, NY, where he has been driving for three years. As he approached the Scajaquada Expressway overpass, he noticed a young woman standing precariously on the narrow ledge outside the railing, overlooking the busy highway. Despite the chaos surrounding her, including passing traffic and pedestrians, Barton remained composed and quickly sprang into action.

He pulled up alongside the woman and opened the bus doors, calling out to her, "Ma'am, are you okay?" When she didn't respond, he cautiously exited the bus and approached her. The woman appeared distraught and disconnected, but Barton's gentle words and physical presence seemed to break through her emotional barrier. He wrapped his arm around her and encouraged her to step away from the railing, which she eventually agreed to do.

With the help of a corrections officer and a crisis counselor who arrived shortly thereafter, Barton guided the woman to safety on the sidewalk. As they sat together, he engaged her in conversation, trying to understand what led her to contemplate such a drastic act. Though she was initially unforthcoming, she eventually shared a small moment of connection with Barton, commenting on the pleasant scent of his cologne.

This incident occurred on October 18, and it marked a pivotal moment for Barton, who has been driving for the NFTA for three years. His selfless actions earned him praise from his colleagues, who affectionately refer to him as "Big Country," and admiration from the community. Even the McKinley High School students on his bus that day were deeply moved by his heroism, giving him a round of applause as he returned to continue his route.

In the aftermath of the event, Barton's humility and kindness shone through, as he credited the students for their exemplary behavior and expressed hope that his actions might help change the public perception of bus drivers, showcasing them as caring and compassionate individuals. The NFTA's executive director, Kimberley A. Minkel, echoed these sentiments, praising Barton's quick and calm response to the emergency.

reference: – A Buffalo bus driver is getting the hero's treatment after stopping his bus to get a suicidal young woman off a bridge overpass, reports WIVB-TV. Darnell Barton, who had just picked up about two dozen high school students, spotted the woman on the wrong side of the ledge, seemingly about to jump into traffic, reports the Buffalo News. He stopped his bus, asked her if she needed help, then slowly made his way toward her. "She was distraught, she was distant, she was really disconnected," he recalls. "I grabbed her arm and put my arm around her and said, 'Do you want to come on this side of the guardrail,' and that was actually the first time she spoke to me. She said yes." He then sat down with her on the sidewalk until authorities arrived. “Darnell won’t tell you this, but when he went back on his bus, the McKinley students gave him a round of applause,” says a spokesman for the local transit agency.

final_summary:
Business Email Compromise (BEC) scams, also known as CEO fraud, have become a growing concern worldwide, with the FBI estimating that these scams have cost organizations over $2.3 billion in losses over the past three years. In France, businesses have lost an estimated €465m since 2010, with 15,000 firms falling victim to the scam, including big names like Michelin, KPMG, and Nestle. The largest fraud was for €32m, and a further €830m could have been stolen if more phishing attacks had proven successful.

One notable case in France involved Carole Gratzmuller, the boss of Etna Industrie, a medium-sized company that employs 50 people and makes industrial equipment on the outskirts of Paris. Gratzmuller's accountant was targeted by scammers who posed as the company's president, sending an email with instructions to conduct a confidential transaction. The accountant was bombarded with multiple emails and phone calls, ultimately transferring €500,000 to foreign bank accounts before realizing the scam.

France has been particularly vulnerable to CEO fraud due to the prevalence of French-Israeli gangs operating in the country. Gilbert Chikli, a French-Israeli man, was convicted of defrauding over 30 banks and companies out of €7.9m. Despite being sentenced to seven years in prison in absentia, Chikli remains at large in Israel, where there is no extradition agreement with France.

Meanwhile, in India, a startup called StayUncle has emerged to provide a solution for unmarried couples seeking private accommodations. Founded by Sanchit Sethi, StayUncle allows couples to book hotel rooms for short durations, often as little as 8-10 hours, without facing judgment or harassment from hotel staff or local authorities. The company has partnered with 34 hotels in Delhi and Mumbai, including upscale brands like the Trident and Oberoi.

However, CEO fraud is not limited to France or India. The FBI has reported a 270% increase in identified victims and exposed losses since January 2015, with complaints coming from every U.S. state and at least 79 countries. The agency estimates that organizations victimized by CEO fraud attacks lose an average of between $25,000 and $75,000, although some incidents have resulted in losses of millions of dollars.

Security experts warn that CEO fraud scams are particularly insidious because they rely on tricking employees into ignoring basic security precautions. To combat these scams, the FBI recommends adopting two-step or two-factor authentication for email and establishing alternative communication channels to verify significant transactions. Additionally, businesses are advised to exercise caution when publishing information about employee activities online, as attackers often use this information to target executives while they are away from the office.

PhishMe, a security education and awareness firm, has highlighted the importance of employee training in preventing CEO fraud scams. By educating employees to recognize and resist these types of attacks, organizations can reduce their vulnerability to social engineering tactics. Periodic security tests can also help identify problematic users and implement additional security controls to prevent future attacks.

Thinkstock images illustrate the various forms of CEO fraud, including emails from fake CEOs, IT departments, and suppliers. Getty Images captures the impact of CEO fraud on businesses, including the loss of millions of dollars. The FBI's efforts to combat CEO fraud are ongoing, with the agency urging businesses to take proactive steps to protect themselves against these sophisticated scams.

One notable case in France involved Carole Gratzmuller, the boss of Etna Industrie, a medium-sized company that employs 50 people and makes industrial equipment on the outskirts of Paris. Gratzmuller's accountant was targeted by scammers who posed as the company's president, sending an email with instructions to conduct a confidential transaction. The accountant was bombarded with multiple emails and phone calls, ultimately transferring €500,000 to foreign bank accounts before realizing the scam.

France has been particularly vulnerable to CEO fraud due to the prevalence of French-Israeli gangs operating in the country. Gilbert Chikli, a French-Israeli man, was convicted of defrauding over 30 banks and companies out of €7.9m. Despite being sentenced to seven years in prison in absentia, Chikli remains at large in Israel, where there is no extradition agreement with France.

Meanwhile, in India, a startup called StayUncle has emerged to provide a solution for unmarried couples seeking private accommodations. Founded by Sanchit Sethi, StayUncle allows couples to book hotel rooms for short durations, often as little as 8-10 hours, without facing judgment or harassment from hotel staff or local authorities. The company has partnered with 34 hotels in Delhi and Mumbai, including upscale brands like the Trident and Oberoi.

However, CEO fraud is not limited to France or India. The FBI has reported a 270% increase in identified victims and exposed losses since January 2015, with complaints coming from every U.S. state and at least 79 countries. The agency estimates that organizations victimized by CEO fraud attacks lose an average of between $25,000 and $75,000, although some incidents have resulted in losses of millions of dollars.

Security experts warn that CEO fraud scams are particularly insidious because they rely on tricking employees into ignoring basic security precautions. To combat these scams, the FBI recommends adopting two-step or two-factor authentication for email and establishing alternative communication channels to verify significant transactions. Additionally, businesses are advised to exercise caution when publishing information about employee activities online, as attackers often use this information to target executives while they are away from the office.

PhishMe, a security education and awareness firm, has highlighted the importance of employee training in preventing CEO fraud scams. By educating employees to recognize and resist these types of attacks, organizations can reduce their vulnerability to social engineering tactics. Periodic security tests can also help identify problematic users and implement additional security controls to prevent future attacks.

Thinkstock images illustrate the various forms of CEO fraud, including emails from fake CEOs, IT departments, and suppliers. Getty Images captures the impact of CEO fraud on businesses, including the loss of millions of dollars. The FBI's efforts to combat CEO fraud are ongoing, with the agency urging businesses to take proactive steps to protect themselves against these sophisticated scams.

reference: – A relatively new email scam is raking in millions of dollars, and it's got nothing to do with long-lost relatives in Nigeria. Instead, as Quartz explains, the "CEO Email Scam" dupes employees into wiring money by using bogus messages from the boss. The scammers do their homework: They assume the identity of a company CEO, or sometimes simply send an email so close to the correct email that the recipient never notices the difference. The requests go to someone in the company authorized to deal with money, usually demanding quick action—and confidentiality—because of a pending business deal. It's been so successful that the FBI issued an alert last week, citing complaints in every state and in 79 countries. "On the surface, business email compromise scams may seem unsophisticated relative to moneymaking schemes that involve complex malicious software," writes Brian Krebs of Krebs on Security. But the CEO scam is especially effective because of one crucial factor: "In traditional phishing scams, the attackers interact with the victim’s bank directly, but in the CEO scam the crooks trick the victim into doing that for them." When it works, a typical haul is between $25,000 and $75,000. The known haul worldwide is $2.3 billion, says the FBI. One cybersecurity expert in France warns that it won't go away any time soon. "It will spread because it's too good to be ignored," he tells the BBC.

final_summary:
A disturbing incident unfolded at the Senate on Tuesday, as a letter sent to Sen. Roger Wicker (R-Miss.) tested positive for the toxic substance ricin at the Senate mail facility in Washington, D.C. The envelope, which bore a Memphis, Tenn., postmark and lacked a return address, was intercepted at an off-site facility where congressional mail is screened due to security concerns following the 2001 anthrax attacks that killed five people and infected 17 others in Miss.

According to Senate Sergeant-at-Arms Terrance Gainer, the letter was initially quarantined and hazardous material personnel from the U.S. Capitol Police responded to the scene. Preliminary tests indicated the presence of ricin, a highly toxic substance derived from castor beans, prompting the Capitol Police to partner with the FBI for an ongoing investigation.

As the situation unfolded, Senate Majority Leader Harry M. Reid revealed that the letter was addressed to Wicker's office, sparking concern for the senator's safety. In response, Wicker was assigned a protective detail, and the Senate mail facility was temporarily shut down for testing and further investigation.

Sen. Claire McCaskill (D-Mo.) disclosed that a suspect had been identified in connection with the incident, although no one was in custody as of Tuesday night. McCaskill also warned state offices in Miss. to be vigilant for potential follow-up letters containing the toxic substance.

Homeland Security Secretary Janet Napolitano and FBI Director Robert Mueller briefed senators on the matter, shifting the focus from cybersecurity to the ricin threat. The Senate subsequently went into recess, with lawmakers taking precautions to ensure their safety.

This incident serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing threats faced by public figures, particularly those in positions of power. The use of ricin, a potent toxin that can be produced easily and cheaply, raises concerns about its potential misuse by extremist groups or individuals. As the investigation unfolds, authorities will likely scrutinize the motives behind this sinister act and work to prevent future incidents.

reference: – Scary story breaking out of DC: A letter addressed to Republican Mississippi Sen. Roger Wicker tested positive for the poison ricin, reports Politico. The envelope never got to him, reports CNN, which says it was intercepted at an off-site facility that sorts mail for the Capitol. Three separate tests turned up positive for the highly toxic poison. Authorities have identified a suspect, said Sen. Claire McCaskill, adding only that it was someone who writes lots of letters to lawmakers. It's not clear why the apparent target was Wicker, described by the Washington Post as a "low-profile senator in his second term." (He replaced Trent Lott in 2008.) The envelope got detected at a facility set up in 2001 after some senators were sent letters tainted with anthrax. "The bottom line is, the process we have in place worked," said McCaskill. FBI chief Robert Mueller and Homeland Security chief Janet Napolitano briefed senators today, though it's not clear when the letter was received.

final_summary:
In a shocking incident that sparked widespread outrage and concern for animal welfare, a photo of a black Labrador Retriever, identified as Mya, being held upside down above a beer keg at an off-campus party in Brockport, New York, went viral on social media. The image, posted on the Twitter account @SUNYPartyStory, which shares photos from recent parties at State University of New York (SUNY) schools, showed the dog with its paws in the air and the beer keg's nozzle in its mouth.

The photo, captioned "Keg stands n Bits!", was shared on Sunday by the account, which has over 68,000 followers, and quickly gained attention from authorities and the public alike. According to Time Warner Cable News, the College at Brockport released a statement expressing alarm over the photo and promising to investigate the matter further through a joint investigation with the SUNY Brockport Police, the Brockport Police Department, and the Sweden Dog Warden.

Following the investigation, two individuals were identified as suspects in the case. Robert Yates, a resident of Brockport, was charged with taking the photo and posting it online, while Shane Oliver, a student at the College at Brockport, was accused of holding the dog during the incident. Both men were issued appearance tickets and are scheduled to appear in court on April 2. 

According to police, Mya, the black Labrador Retriever, was not owned by either of the suspects and had been stolen from her owner, who was not present at the time of the incident. The dog has been taken into protective custody and is reportedly in good physical condition.

The incident has sparked a heated debate on social media, with many expressing their disgust and disappointment at the treatment of the dog. Some students from the College at Brockport have come forward to defend the individuals involved, claiming that the photo was meant as a joke and that the dog was not harmed. However, others have condemned the actions of those responsible, calling for them to face severe consequences.

College officials have stated that they will investigate the incident and take appropriate action, citing the Student Code of Conduct, which allows for disciplinary action against students who engage in behavior that reflects poorly on the institution, even if it occurs off-campus. Spokesman John Follaco emphasized that the college takes allegations of animal abuse seriously and will work to ensure that those responsible are held accountable.

As the investigation continues, the community remains divided on the issue, with some calling for greater awareness about animal welfare and others defending the actions of those involved. The incident serves as a stark reminder of the importance of treating animals with respect and kindness, and the need for accountability when it comes to animal abuse.

final_summary:
In a recent evaluation conducted by U.S. News & World Report, 41 of the most popular diets were assessed to determine the best options for various health and fitness goals. This extensive review began in 1996 when Alexa Internet started donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive, which would later be added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. According to the report, the best diet for shedding excess weight is Weight Watchers, closely followed by Jenny Craig and the Raw Food Diet. However, the DASH diet emerged as the top-ranked diet overall, with the Mediterranean Diet taking the second spot. 

Researchers analyzed 20 diets, compiling profiles that were then reviewed by a panel of 22 experts specializing in diet and nutrition, diabetes, and heart disease. The rankings were based on scores in seven categories, including short-term weight loss, long-term weight loss, ease of adherence, nutritional completeness, ability to prevent or manage diabetes, and management of heart disease. Additionally, each diet was evaluated across five specific categories: best overall diet, best commercial diet plan, best weight-loss diet, best diabetes diet, and best heart-healthy diet. In the latter category, the Ornish Diet secured the top position, with the Therapeutic Lifestyle Changes (TLC) diet placing second. The DASH diet also took the top spot for the best diabetes diet, followed by the Mayo Clinic diet. 

Every year, millions of people try to lose weight, lower their blood pressure, or combat diabetes through dieting. A Reuters Life article published in New York highlights the importance of considering individual goals when selecting a diet, emphasizing that exercise plays a significant role in achieving success, although it falls outside the scope of a traditional diet. Approximately 34 percent of U.S. adults aged 20 and over are overweight, while an equal percentage are obese, according to 2007-2008 figures. Among the other notable diets included in the rankings were Atkins and Slim-Fast, which did not rank as highly as the top diets.
