reference: – The co-founder of the hugely popular hip-hop and skateboarding clothing brand LRG has been found dead in his Laguna Beach home. Jonas Bevacqua was 34. There was no obvious cause of death, and an autopsy will be conducted, AP reports. LRG, which the designer founded when he was barely out of his teens, branched out from clothing into music and other items, which supported up-and-coming recording artists. Bevacqua, who grew up in a multi-racial California family, said he founded LRG because no other company was creating clothing that reflected his and co-founder Robert Wright's interest in surfing, skateboarding, and hip-hop. "I grew up in a pretty unique environment and was exposed to a lot of different things," Bevacqua told the Orange County Register in 2009. "I didn't feel there was a clothing company to bridge the gap among all these different things that we were into—that spoke for that melting pot of what was going on. That's what LRG was all about."

final_summary:
Donald Trump has nominated Rick Perry, the former Texas governor, to lead the Department of Energy, an agency he once vowed to abolish and famously forgot the name of during a 2011 presidential debate at the White House. Perry, a climate skeptic and former 2016 presidential primary rival, is viewed by conservatives as having management experience and a willingness to question the agency's status quo. 

Perry's nomination comes at a time of heightened political pressure for the Department of Energy, which has a $29.6 billion budget and over 100,000 federal employees and contractors spread across the U.S. The agency plays a crucial role in the nation's nuclear security, energy research, and climate initiatives, including the Obama administration's nuclear deal with Iran, which was negotiated and defended by the Department of Energy. 

Conservatives have long criticized the Department of Energy's programs, including its fossil and efficiency offices, which they see as unnecessary and market-distorting schemes. Perry, who served as governor of Texas for 14 years, has a history of advocating for energy independence and reducing regulations on the oil industry. 

In 2012, during his presidential campaign, Perry called for eliminating the Department of Energy as part of his plan to shrink the federal government, but later clarified that the agency he meant to abolish was the Department of Energy. During his presidential campaigns, Perry repeatedly called for "energy independence" and a rollback of regulations from the Interior Department and the EPA. 

Perry's nomination has sparked alarm from environmental groups, who fear he will shift the department away from renewable energy and toward fossil fuels. As governor, Perry questioned the scientific consensus on climate change, comparing the minority of scientists who challenge this assumption to 17th-century astronomer Galileo, who was persecuted by the Catholic Church. 

Perry has also been a vocal supporter of the Dakota Access Pipeline, which has faced intense opposition from activists who claim it would disturb Native American burial grounds and pollute the drinking water of the nearby Indian reservation. Perry sits on the board of Energy Transfer Partners, the firm behind the pipeline, and has received nearly $237,000 in cash and shares from the company last year. 

If confirmed, Perry would oversee a department that has undergone significant changes since its creation in 1977 under President Jimmy Carter. The agency has shifted from focusing on weapons maintenance and regulations to prioritizing climate change mitigation and renewable energy. Perry's lack of experience in these areas has raised concerns among experts, who note that he would be walking into an agency where many career civil servants are wary of him due to his past pledge to abolish the department. 

The Department of Energy has also been at the forefront of the Obama administration's climate agenda, including the Iran nuclear deal and the promotion of renewable energy. Trump's transition team has requested the names of employees who worked on these initiatives, sparking fears of retaliation against career civil servants. 

Perry's nomination has been met with skepticism by lawmakers, including Rep. Frank Pallone, the top Democrat on the House Energy and Commerce Committee, who called it "defies all logic" given Perry's previous interest in dismantling the agency. The nomination has also raised questions about potential conflicts of interest, given Perry's ties to the fossil fuel industry. 

The Senate confirmation process for Perry's nomination is expected to be contentious, with many Democrats expressing concerns about his views on climate change and his potential impact on the Department of Energy.

reference: – News that Donald Trump has settled on Rick Perry to run the Energy Department prompted a slew of stories Tuesday with one common refrain, and the former Texas governor must be thrilled with it. Here's a sampling: NBC News: "President-elect Donald Trump has selected former Texas Gov. Rick Perry—who famously once forgot that he wanted to abolish the Energy Department—to be secretary of energy ..." New York Times: "Trump plans to name former Gov. Rick Perry of Texas to lead the Energy Department, elevating a man who once could not remember the name of the agency he wanted to eliminate to the cabinet post, secretary of energy, that will run it." Washington Post: "Trump has picked Rick Perry to head the Energy Department, said two people familiar with the decision, seeking to put the former Texas governor in control of an agency whose name he forgot during a presidential debate even as he vowed to abolish it." Politico: "On a presidential debate stage five years ago, Rick Perry blanked on the Energy Department's name when trying to include it in a list of agencies he promised to abolish—memorably concluding with 'oops.' Now Trump has chosen the former Texas governor ..." ABC News: "Trump is expected to choose a man to lead the Department of Energy who infamously forgot its name." BBC: "Trump is expected to pick ex-Texas Governor Rick Perry as his energy secretary, a department whose name he famously forgot in a TV debate." NPR: "It's a good thing former Texas Gov. Rick Perry once forgot he wanted to eliminate the Department of Energy, because President-elect Donald Trump is nominating him to lead the agency. That's according to reports by multiple news outlets." And so on, even here.

final_summary:
As the General Election looms, Prime Minister David Cameron finds himself in a high-stakes battle for his political survival, with just 18 weeks to convince voters that he is the right leader to steer Britain through uncertain times. In an exclusive interview with The Mail on Sunday, Cameron outlined his vision for the country's future, emphasizing the need for economic stability and warning that a Labour government would lead to "chaos." He vowed to introduce reforming legislation on education and welfare within the first 50 days of a new term, if re-elected, and promised to "hit the ground running and hard."

Cameron's personal life took center stage during the Christmas break, as he spent time at his Oxfordshire home, where he indulged in DIY activities, including unblocking drains and cooking meals for his family. Despite the demands of office, he managed to squeeze in quality time with his loved ones, including his wife Samantha, who has been a constant source of support throughout his tenure as Prime Minister. The couple celebrated their 20th wedding anniversary in the near future and have three children together.

However, the weight of responsibility cannot be ignored, and Cameron acknowledged that the upcoming election will be a defining moment for Britain's future. He warned that a Labour government would lead to economic disaster, citing the party's plans to increase borrowing and spending, which would result in £13.5 billion in extra interest payments. In contrast, Cameron boasted of his government's record on employment and responsible stewardship of the economy, which he claimed had brought about a period of unprecedented growth and stability.

Cameron's relationship with US President Barack Obama has been a highlight of his premiership, with the two leaders developing a close bond that extends beyond formal diplomatic channels. Obama has taken to calling Cameron "bro," a term that has raised eyebrows among some observers, although the Prime Minister sees it as a sign of their strong friendship. The two leaders have found common ground on several issues, including their shared commitment to tackling terrorism and promoting global trade.

In the run-up to the election, Cameron has been crisscrossing the country, visiting key battlegrounds such as Cornwall and Yorkshire, where he has been touting his party's message of economic competence and stability. His Chancellor, George Osborne, has been campaigning in Twickenham, where he faced off against Liberal Democrat leader Vince Cable. Meanwhile, Labour leader Ed Miliband has launched his party's campaign, focusing on the NHS and accusing the Tories of being reckless with the country's finances.

As the election heats up, Cameron's leadership style has come under scrutiny, with some critics questioning his ability to connect with working-class voters. However, the Prime Minister remains confident in his abilities, pointing to his record on issues such as education and welfare reform. He has also been praised for his willingness to listen to opposing views, including those of his closest allies, such as Boris Johnson, whom he has authorized to return to the Cabinet after the election.

Despite the challenges ahead, Cameron remains resolute, insisting that he is committed to serving a full five-year term as Prime Minister. His wife, Samantha, has been a rock of support throughout his career, offering sage advice on matters ranging from hostage-rescue operations to domestic policy. Together, the couple has navigated the complexities of high office, and their partnership has become a hallmark of the Prime Minister's tenure.

As the election approaches, Cameron's relationship with Europe will come under intense scrutiny, particularly in light of his pledge to renegotiate the UK's membership terms if he fails to secure concessions from EU leaders. German Chancellor Angela Merkel has signaled her opposition to any attempts to restrict freedom of movement, a key demand of the Prime Minister. The outcome of these negotiations will have significant implications for Britain's future, and Cameron's determination to secure a better deal has sparked debate among his own party members.

Throughout the campaign, Cameron has been keen to emphasize his commitment to social justice, including his support for gay marriage, which he sees as a fundamental human right. He has also spoken out against the idea of a wealth tax, which he believes would be counterproductive and unfair to hardworking individuals. As the election enters its final stretch, Cameron's message of economic competence and stability will face stiff competition from Labour's promises of greater equality and social justice. Only time will tell which path the British people will choose.

reference: – The "special relationship" between the US and the UK is still back-slappingly good, according to David Cameron. President Obama has said "the special relationship is stronger than it has ever been privately and in public and I agree," the British prime minister tells the Daily Mail, revealing that Obama sometimes calls him "bro" when they talk on the phone. The term is more commonly associated with frat boys than world leaders, but the Independent has decided it's still "a step forward" from George W. Bush's "condescending" greeting for Tony Blair: "Yo, Blair!" In the Mail interview, Cameron, who faces a tough election battle in May, also discusses gay marriage—he's proud of introducing the legislation and says he will probably attend a same-sex wedding soon—and his own marriage. He says Samantha Cameron is the first person he turns to for advice on difficult decisions like sending in special forces to rescue hostages. "If she'd organized Napoleon’s march on Moscow, they would have reached Vladivostok in good time," he says.

final_summary:
Susan Schneider Williams, BFA from Susan Schneider Fine Art in Marin County, California, shares a poignant account of her husband's struggle with Lewy Body Disease (LBD), a lesser-known yet debilitating neurodegenerative disorder affecting approximately 1.5 million Americans. In a heartfelt letter, she recounts the harrowing experience of watching her beloved partner succumb to the disease, which ravaged his brain, causing a cascade of symptoms that ultimately led to his untimely death in 2014.

Correspondence to S. Schneider Williams can be sent to susan{susan at}susanschneiderfineart.com. 

This is a personal story, sadly tragic and heartbreaking, but by sharing this information, Susan hopes to help researchers and caregivers understand the complexities of LBD and its impact on patients and their loved ones. As she writes, "My hope is that it will help you understand your patients along with their spouses and caregivers a little more."

Robin Williams had been under his doctors' care, struggling with symptoms that seemed unrelated: constipation, urinary difficulty, heartburn, sleeplessness, and a poor sense of smell—and lots of stress. He also had a slight tremor in his left hand that would come and go, attributed to a previous shoulder injury. In late October 2013, he started having gut discomfort, which marked the beginning of a downward spiral.

For 7 years, Susan and Robin had shared a deep connection, built on trust, love, and mutual support. They would regularly review their days, discussing joys and fears, insecurities, and concerns. This foundation of friendship and love served as their armor against the challenges posed by LBD. However, as the disease progressed, Robin's behavior became increasingly erratic, and he struggled to cope with his symptoms.

About 40% of dopamine neurons were lost, and almost no neurons were free of Lewy bodies throughout his entire brain and brainstem. Susan recalls, "Not until the coroner's report, 3 months after his death, would I learn that it was diffuse LBD that took him." Four doctors who reviewed his records agreed that his was one of the worst pathologies they had seen.

Throughout the ordeal, Susan and Robin tried various treatments, including psychotherapy, medication, and alternative therapies like self-hypnosis and yoga. Despite their best efforts, Robin's condition worsened, with symptoms such as paranoia, delusions, insomnia, and memory loss becoming increasingly pronounced. His medication regimen was adjusted multiple times, but the effects were short-lived, and he continued to deteriorate.

A year after Robin's passing, an autopsy revealed that he suffered from diffuse Lewy Body Disease, a pathology characterized by the widespread presence of Lewy bodies – abnormal protein clumps within brain neurons. This diagnosis shed light on the complexity of the disease, which is often misdiagnosed or mistaken for other conditions. Susan Schneider Williams' tireless efforts to understand LBD led her to join the American Brain Foundation, where she currently serves on the Board of Directors.

Through her experiences, Susan implores researchers and scientists to continue their pursuit of a cure for LBD and other brain diseases, emphasizing that every step forward brings hope for those affected by these conditions. Her story serves as a testament to the importance of awareness, education, and support for individuals and families navigating the complexities of neurodegenerative disorders.

history.draft:
Susan Schneider Williams, artist and wife of the late Robin Williams, shares a poignant account of her husband's struggle with Lewy Body Disease (LBD), a lesser-known yet debilitating neurodegenerative disorder affecting approximately 1.5 million Americans. In a heartfelt letter, she recounts the harrowing experience of watching her beloved partner succumb to the disease, which ravaged his brain, causing a cascade of symptoms that ultimately led to his untimely death in 2014.

In late October 2013, Robin began experiencing uncharacteristic symptoms, including constipation, urinary difficulties, heartburn, sleeplessness, and a poor sense of smell. Initially, these issues were dismissed as unrelated, but as the months progressed, his anxiety and fear spiked, culminating in a panic attack during a film shoot in Vancouver. Despite undergoing various tests and treatments, including psychotherapy and medication, Robin's condition worsened, with symptoms such as paranoia, delusions, insomnia, and memory loss becoming increasingly pronounced.

As the disease progressed, Robin's behavior became erratic, and he struggled to remember lines for his scenes, a stark contrast to his impressive performance in the Broadway production "Bengal Tiger at the Baghdad Zoo" three years prior. His medication regimen was adjusted multiple times, but the effects were short-lived, and he continued to deteriorate. The couple's sleep deficit grew, exacerbating the situation, and they were advised to sleep apart to mitigate the impact.

In the summer of 2014, Robin's condition accelerated, with his left-hand tremor becoming continuous, and he began to exhibit a parkinsonian mask, a slow gait, and difficulty finding words. His cognitive functions declined, and he became trapped in a cycle of fear and anxiety, which he desperately sought to escape. In a poignant moment, he expressed his desire to "reboot his brain," highlighting the desperation and frustration he felt as the disease ravaged his mind.

A year after Robin's passing, an autopsy revealed that he suffered from diffuse Lewy Body Disease, a pathology characterized by the widespread presence of Lewy bodies – abnormal protein clumps within brain neurons. This diagnosis shed light on the complexity of the disease, which is often misdiagnosed or mistaken for other conditions. Susan Schneider Williams' tireless efforts to understand LBD led her to join the American Brain Foundation, where she currently serves on the Board of Directors.

Through her experiences, Susan implores researchers and scientists to continue their pursuit of a cure for LBD and other brain diseases, emphasizing that every step forward brings hope for those affected by these conditions. Her story serves as a testament to the importance of awareness, education, and support for individuals and families navigating the complexities of neurodegenerative disorders.

reference: – In what Discover calls a "unique and touching paper" published in the journal Neurology Thursday, Robin Williams' widow shares the heartbreaking story of her husband's final 10 months and asks neurologists not to give up in their quest toward brain disease cures. Susan Schneider Williams refers to Lewy Body disease, the neurodegenerative disorder that ultimately led to Williams' suicide in August 2014, as "the terrorist inside my husband's brain." She describes how, toward the end of his life, "Robin was losing his mind and he was aware of it." He was struggling with paranoia, anxiety, delusions, insomnia, and other symptoms both physical and mental, and for months, he and his wife could get no answers about what was happening to him. He was ultimately diagnosed with Parkinson's disease, but as his symptoms continued to worsen, he grew "weary" and it felt like they were both "drowning." Then, ultimately, he took his own life. It was only after his death that an autopsy revealed Williams actually had Lewy Body disease—his clinical symptoms mirrored Parkinson's, but his brain pathology showed that almost all of the neurons throughout his brain and brainstem had been besieged by Lewy bodies, Schneider Williams writes. When she found out, she wasn't surprised: "The mere fact that something had invaded nearly every region of my husband's brain made perfect sense to me," she writes. She has spent the time since Williams' death learning all she can about Lewy Body disease, for which there is no cure, and she now serves on the Board of Directors for the American Brain Foundation. Her full piece is worth a read.

final_summary:
Ty, a 6-year-old brindle and white Bull Terrier, has gone missing after disappearing during a flight from Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) to Tampa on Friday night. According to his owner, Frank Romano, Ty was traveling in a specially designed kennel purchased from PetCo, which was placed in the cargo area of the Delta Airlines flight. However, when Romano boarded the plane with his family, a Delta employee informed him that Ty had escaped from the kennel approximately an hour prior, claiming the dog had chewed through the metal door and hard plastic walls.

Romano expressed skepticism about the explanation, citing the durability of the kennel and questioning why the employee did not notify him immediately about the incident. Furthermore, he was assured that the empty kennel would be available for collection at baggage claim, but it never materialized. Despite searching the airport and contacting Delta multiple times, Romano has yet to receive any concrete answers or updates on Ty's whereabouts.

The next day, Romano's family flew to Tampa as planned, leaving him behind to continue searching for Ty. He has called Delta several times, but the situation has only become more confusing, with the airline providing conflicting information about Ty's location. Romano was given a phone number to contact the cargo team at Tampa airport, but they claimed to have no knowledge of Ty.

This incident is not an isolated case; Delta Airlines has a history of mishandling pets in their care. A Change.org petition with over 2,000 signatures demands accountability from the airline due to numerous instances of lost or injured animals. One such case involved a New York man who attempted to transport a puppy from Alabama to New York via Delta in 2011, only to have the dog temporarily misplaced in Atlanta. Another instance in 2011 saw a German Shepherd, destined for Germany, escape in Atlanta and ultimately die after being struck by a vehicle.

In 2010, Delta experienced a series of blunders, including misrouting two children between Boston and Cleveland, losing a dog en route from Mexico to Canada, and failing to provide adequate care for a puppy that arrived in California instead of Maine. The airline's handling of these incidents has been met with widespread criticism, with some owners receiving inadequate compensation or assistance.

A spokesperson for LAist spoke with a representative from Delta, who stated that the airline would look into the matter and get back to them the next day. Meanwhile, Romano remains hopeful that he will be reunited with his beloved pet. The lack of transparency and accountability from Delta Airlines raises serious concerns about the safety and well-being of animals in their care.

In 2010, a German Shepherd mix named Nala was lost during a flight from LAX to Germany, with the airline claiming she had tried to escape from her crate. Her body was later discovered on a highway, sparking outrage among animal welfare advocates. Additionally, a family's two dogs were misrouted during a flight from Germany to Washington state, resulting in the death of one dog and the prolonged separation of the other.

The cumulative effect of these incidents highlights the need for Delta to conduct a thorough internal investigation and revamp their policies and procedures regarding animal transport.

reference: – A man is trying to find his best friend after Delta Airlines lost track of the 6-year-old bull terrier Friday night. "I keep praying and wishing for him to come back," Frank Romano tells ABC News. Delta has told him they're looking for the dog, Ty, who seems to have gone missing at LAX when Romano and his family were headed to Tampa. After Romano boarded the plane, Delta staff told him Ty was missing, having bitten through the door of his kennel. Romano finds that confusing "when it had a metal door and was made of hard plastic," he tells the LAist. He searched the airport without finding the dog. The next day, he and his family headed to Tampa, where he is now. Meanwhile, he says he's received mixed reports from Delta, who at first told him they'd found the dog; then, "a few minutes later," said they hadn't. "They give us the run-around," he says. "Even gave us a number to the cargo place at Tampa airport saying he was there. We called and (they) said they had no dogs." Delta has lost enough dogs in the past to prompt a Change.org petition, LAist notes, pointing to three different incidents. "If I could I would go back to LA in a heartbeat," Romano says. "I bet I could find Ty faster than Delta can."

final_summary:
In a candid interview with Access Hollywood's Kit Hoover, Demi Lovato, a 21-year-old singer and "X Factor" coach, has spoken publicly about her struggles with substance abuse and addiction. At 19 years old, Lovato reached rock bottom after checking into a rehab facility in Illinois for emotional and physical issues in 2010. However, despite receiving treatment and support, she continued to struggle with addiction, revealing that she would often sneak cocaine onto airplanes and use it in the lavatory.

Lovato confessed that she was addicted to cocaine and would experience withdrawal symptoms within 30 minutes to an hour without it. She would go to great lengths to obtain the substance, including smuggling it onto planes while traveling with a sober companion who was tasked with monitoring her 24/7. This level of manipulation and deception was a hallmark of her addiction, which she described as a "disease" that made her a skilled liar and manipulator.

In addition to her struggles with cocaine, Lovato also spoke about her history of alcohol abuse, recalling a particularly disturbing incident where she filled a Sprite bottle with vodka and drank it on the way to a flight, only to vomit in the car. This behavior, she acknowledged, was indicative of a deeper problem - she was exhibiting classic signs of alcoholic behavior, and it was no longer just about rebellion or fun.

Lovato's mother, Dianna, also shared her own experiences with addiction and mental health issues, revealing that she had struggled with an eating disorder for many, many years and had been in denial about her daughter's struggles with addiction. Dianna recalled feeling torn between believing her daughter's denials and recognizing the warning signs of substance abuse. Ultimately, both Demi and her mother sought help and support, and their relationship grew stronger as a result.

She credited an intervention by loved ones with prompting her to seek treatment, and she eventually became closer to her daughter as they both worked through their respective struggles. Demi expressed her gratitude and admiration for her mother's strength and resilience, stating that she loves and is proud of her. The interview took place on Monday from the set of "The X Factor," where Lovato was coaching contestants. As a former child star who rose to fame on Disney, Lovato has used her platform to speak out about her struggles and raise awareness about mental health issues.

final_summary:
A manhunt is underway in Santa Rosa County for William Boyette, a suspect accused of embarking on a deadly killing spree that has claimed the lives of three women and left one critically injured. Boyette, described as armed and extremely dangerous, is believed to be traveling with his accomplice, Mary Rice, 38, who has been named as a suspect in the capital murder of one of the victims.

The investigation began when two bodies were discovered at the Emerald Sands Inn in Milton, Florida. The victims, Alicia Greer, 30, and Jacqueline Moore, 49, were found deceased, with authorities believing Boyette to be responsible for their deaths. On February 3rd, Boyette allegedly committed another murder in Lillian, Alabama, where 52-year-old Peggy Broz lost her life.

On the next day, Boyette's killing spree continued when he allegedly shot 28-year-old Kayla Crocker in a home invasion in Beulah, Florida. Crocker, who was critically injured, managed to escape and was rushed to the hospital, while Boyette stole her vehicle, a white 2006 Chevrolet Cobalt with a distinctive skull and crossbones sticker on the rear passenger door and Florida license plate 9613BJ.

As the investigation unfolded, authorities revealed that Boyette and Rice had been evading capture for several days, hiding out in the woods of northwest Florida. They were spotted at a gas station in Pensacola, just hours after the home invasion, and were seen walking in the woods, with Boyette appearing to be armed with a handgun.

Escambia County Sheriff David Morgan stated that Rice had multiple opportunities to flee or seek help, yet chose to continue accompanying Boyette on his rampage. Rice's involvement in the killings has led to a capital murder warrant being issued for her arrest. The duo's whereabouts remained unknown, with authorities urging the public to remain vigilant and report any sightings to the authorities.

Boyette's history of violence dates back to 2002, with a string of arrests in Escambia and Santa Rosa counties for various charges, including battery, assault, kidnapping, and weapons offenses. Despite these incidents, he has consistently avoided serving significant prison time, often having charges dropped or being released on bail. This lack of accountability has raised concerns among law enforcement officials, who warn that Boyette's threats to not be taken alive should be taken seriously.

Deputies from Escambia County have increased their presence on the streets, doubling the number of deputies patrolling the area in an effort to apprehend Boyette and Rice. The search efforts have focused on a wooded area near Nine Mile Road, where footage showed the suspects in the vicinity. Dogs have been deployed to search for possible encampments, and authorities are urging residents to remain cautious and report any information that may lead to the capture of the fugitives.

Sheriff Morgan emphasized the gravity of the situation, stating, "We're dealing with a killer who has made it clear he will not be taken alive." As the manhunt continues, authorities stress that anyone recognizing Boyette or Rice should not approach them, but rather contact the authorities immediately.

reference: – Florida is facing a "running nightmare" with two people on a killing spree that has so far left three women dead and another in critical condition. William "Billy" Boyette and Mary Craig Rice are wanted in the Jan. 31 murders of 30-year-old Alicia Greer—who was in a relationship with Boyette—and 39-year-old Jacqueline Jeanette Moore at a hotel in Milton. Police believe the suspects then drove to Lillian, Ala., killed 52-year-old Peggy Broz in her front yard, and fled with her vehicle on Friday. In the latest incident, the pair are accused of shooting 28-year-old Kayla Crocker and stealing her vehicle in Pensacola on Monday. Crocker was taken to a hospital in critical condition but is expected to survive, reports the Pensacola News Journal. Authorities say Boyette, 44, and Rice, 38, have managed to elude capture by staying "very mobile." They're believed to have camped in wooded areas; two photos circulated by police show the pair walking in woods with supplies and an apparent handgun, reports WKRG. "What we are experiencing is a running nightmare, quite honestly," a police rep says, noting all victims apart from Greer appear to have been selected at random. Another adds Boyette—accused of stabbing and strangling a girlfriend in 2015—has told "many, many people that he will not be taken alive." Authorities "take those threats and those admonitions very seriously," the rep says. Police say the couple are armed and dangerous and should not be approached.

final_summary:
In the United States, nearly a third of antibiotics prescribed in outpatient settings, including doctors' offices, emergency rooms, and hospital-based clinics, are deemed unnecessary, according to a comprehensive analysis conducted by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and the Pew Charitable Trust. The study, published in the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA), reveals that approximately 47 million unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions are dispensed annually across the country, primarily for conditions that do not respond to antibiotics, such as colds, sore throats, bronchitis, flu, and other viral illnesses. This finding is based on an analysis of data from the 2010-2011 National Ambulatory Medical Care Survey and National Hospital Ambulatory Medical Care Survey, which estimated that 12.6% of outpatient visits in the U.S. result in an antibiotic prescription, translating to around 184,032 visits annually.

Furthermore, more than 40% of these prescriptions are written to treat patients with acute respiratory conditions, such as sinus infections, middle ear infections, sore throats, colds, bronchitis, asthma, allergies, flu, and pneumonia, with half of these prescriptions being unnecessary due to the viral nature of the illnesses. According to the CDC, the overuse of antibiotics has contributed significantly to the alarming rise of drug-resistant superbugs in recent years, posing a substantial threat to hospitals and nursing homes nationwide. The agency has warned that "nightmare bacteria" are becoming increasingly resistant to even the strongest antibiotics, resulting in an estimated 2 million illnesses and 23,000 deaths each year in the U.S. alone.

To address this pressing issue, the White House has set a target of reducing inappropriate antibiotic use in outpatient settings by 50% by 2020. However, based on the new report, achieving this goal would require a 15% reduction in overall outpatient antibiotic use, resulting in approximately 23 million fewer antibiotics prescribed annually by 2020. The CDC emphasizes that antibiotics are life-saving drugs, and continued inappropriate use will undermine their effectiveness in fighting life-threatening infections, cancer treatment, organ transplants, and burn and trauma care. The agency stresses that better communication between healthcare providers and patients about the dangers of antibiotic overuse can lead to more judicious prescribing practices.

A separate report by Pew notes that the majority of antibiotic prescriptions are written by individual healthcare providers, who are often influenced by patient demands for antibiotics. However, the reports suggest that relatively simple measures, such as displaying posters in patient waiting rooms, can help curb antibiotic prescribing. The CDC and Pew's findings highlight the need for concerted efforts to promote responsible antibiotic use, particularly in outpatient settings, where the majority of antibiotic prescriptions are dispensed. By working together, healthcare providers, patients, and policymakers can help preserve the effectiveness of antibiotics and mitigate the risks associated with antibiotic resistance.

reference: – Scientists have been concerned for some time about "Phantom Menace"-type superbugs that aren't fazed by meds. The latest CDC data finds that 2 million people a year in the US are infected with bacteria resistant to antibiotics, and at least 23,000 die directly from those infections. And a new report from the CDC and the Pew Charitable Trusts—the first research to quantify this issue, per the Washington Post—isn't encouraging. A study published Tuesday in JAMA finds nearly one in three antibiotic prescriptions (around 47 million per year) are given needlessly to people with viruses such as bronchitis and the common cold, as well as for ear and sinus infections and sore throats not caused by bacterial infections—meaning the Rx not only won't vanquish their illness, but can potentially build up antibiotic resistance, the St. Louis Post-Dispatch reports. Besides the drug resistance, overuse can up the risk for drug allergies and even an unpleasant-sounding diarrhea that can be fatal. The researchers analyzed 184,032 outpatient visits to ERs and medical clinics in 2010 and 2011 and found that 12.6% walked out with an antibiotic script, per the Pharmacy Times. Out of an estimated annual antibiotic-prescription rate of 506 per every 1,000 people, only about 353 of those who received prescriptions should likely have received them. The Times notes President Obama's initiative to cut down on unnecessary outpatient antibiotic use by 50% by 2020; researchers say that, based on this study, we'll need to slash overall antibiotic use by 15% to meet that. "If we continue down the road of inappropriate use we'll lose the most powerful tool we have to fight life-threatening infections," CDC Director Tom Frieden says. "Losing these antibiotics would undermine our ability to treat patients with deadly infections [and] cancer, provide organ transplants, and save victims of burns and trauma." (Here's hoping teixobactin can help us out with superbugs.)

final_summary:
In the small Pilbara town of Newman, a bizarre incident unfolded on a typical Friday afternoon, leaving residents stunned and authorities scrambling to address the potential dangers posed by a reckless individual. According to eyewitness accounts, a 37-year-old man, who owns a Beechcraft two-seater prop-driven plane, allegedly taxied the aircraft without wings and a steering wheel down the main street of Newman, ultimately parking it outside the local pub, known as the Purple Pub or Newman Hotel. 

Beau Woolcock, a resident of Newman, witnessed the unusual sight with his five-year-old daughter, describing the scene as surreal. "On the way back through [town], here's a plane parked outside the Purple Pub," Woolcock stated. The plane, which had its propeller running, was later towed away by a local tow truck driver, while the fire brigade and emergency services personnel were also present at the scene. People gathered near the Caltex station took photos with their phones, capturing the unusual moment, something that doesn't happen every day.

Newman Sergeant Mark Garner emphasized the gravity of the situation, citing the presence of children walking home from school at the time and the potential risks associated with the plane's design. "The danger obviously taxiing a prop plane down Newman Drive, bearing in mind that kids have just come out of school," Garner explained. "It's a busy Friday afternoon. I know it's Newman, but we do get a fair bit of traffic."

An investigation into the incident revealed that the plane's fuel line was attached to a jerry can inside the cabin, allowing it to continue running even after the engine was turned off. Furthermore, the plane's owner, who does not possess a pilot's license, had recently acquired the aircraft from another individual in town. 

A few days after the incident, on Monday, the 37-year-old man was charged with endangering life, health, or safety. He is scheduled to appear in court on November 18, later this month. The case serves as a stark reminder of the importance of adhering to safety protocols and regulations, particularly in situations involving potentially hazardous equipment and vulnerable populations.

reference: – Buying a plane must be thirsty work: Police in Newman, Western Australia, say a man who had just purchased a two-seater Beechcraft decided to drive it home and stop for a beer at a pub on the main street on the way. A resident says he was astounded to see the plane, which had no wings or steering wheel, parked outside the Purple Pub. Even more special was "seeing one of the local coppers poking his head in the door of the plane looking like he was asking for a license," he tells ABC Radio. "Even though no one was in the plane at the time, I was like, what the hell?" After some initial puzzlement over what, if anything, the driver should be charged with, police settled on a charge of "endangering life, health, or safety," reports WAToday. A police spokesman says the incident is being taken very seriously because there were children walking home from school at the time that the man—who doesn't have a pilot's license—was taxiing through the town, using the plane's foot pedals to steer. He passed a breath test at the scene, but police decided to impound the plane.

final_summary: A West Virginia Department of Transportation worker, Thomas Keith Henderson of Elkhorn, W.Va., faces charges of driving a state snow plow while intoxicated. Henderson was arrested late Wednesday during a traffic stop on Burke Mountain near Keystone by Deputy R.L. Jones of the McDowell County Sheriff's Department. The deputy stopped the snow plow because the driver's side headlight was out. Upon approaching the vehicle, Jones detected the presence of alcohol on Henderson's breath and administered a field sobriety test, which Henderson failed. Following the failed test, Henderson was taken into custody and transported to the Welch Police Department, where he underwent a breathalyzer examination. The results revealed a blood-alcohol content of 0.09, exceeding the permissible limit of 0.04 for commercial driver's license holders. final_summary:
The Los Angeles County Coroner's office has completed the autopsy on actress Brittany Murphy, 32, whose sudden cardiac arrest led to her death on Sunday. According to sources close to the investigation, the autopsy revealed no signs of foul play or trauma to the body, which appeared "normal" upon examination. However, the cause of death remains pending further testing, including toxicology reports and histopathology studies of her organs and tissues, as well as neuropathology analysis of her brain. TMZ reported that the Coroner's office will not determine the cause of death until these additional reports are submitted, which is expected to take 4 to 6 weeks. One area of particular interest for the Coroner is the presence of prescription medications in Murphy's system, which may have potentially contributed to her death. 

LAPD officials have concluded their initial investigation into Murphy's death, ruling out any possibility of a crime having occurred. Sources indicate that Murphy had been taking prescription medication for various medical conditions prior to her passing. The Los Angeles County Coroner's office, led by spokesperson Ed Winter, stated that they will make a final determination regarding the cause of death once the toxicology results are completed, which is anticipated within the next two months. In related news, the LAPD has also confirmed that there were no signs of foul play or trauma to Murphy's body, contradicting earlier concerns raised by some photos taken several weeks ago that suggested she may have been underweight. The investigation continues to focus on determining the exact circumstances surrounding Murphy's death, with the Coroner's office working closely with the LAPD to gather all necessary information. As reported by Andrew Blankstein today, the Los Angeles County Coroner's office will make a final determination of what caused the death of actress Brittany Murphy after toxicology results are completed in the next two months, officials said.

final_summary:
Last week, in Manitowoc County, Steven Avery, a man previously convicted of a sexual assault, was accused of murdering 25-year-old photographer Teresa Halbach, who vanished after visiting his auto salvage yard. His release from prison, which was met with apologies and a warm welcome from the community, had been just weeks prior. The case gained national attention and led to calls for law enforcement reforms, prompting the creation of a task force that aimed to improve the state's investigative procedures.

Avery became a prominent advocate for wrongful conviction cases, testifying before the State Legislature and appearing on panels discussing the topic. He was also involved in a $36 million civil lawsuit against the former prosecutor and sheriff in his original case. However, his reputation took a drastic turn when he was accused of murdering Halbach.

Investigations revealed that Halbach's car was found at the salvage yard, along with her remains, including bones and teeth. Blood samples from both Avery and Halbach were discovered in the vehicle, and her car key was found in Avery's trailer. DNA analysis confirmed that Avery's DNA was on the key. The new charges against Avery stunned his supporters, including his lawyer, who stated that the case had dealt a devastating blow to their efforts to reform the state's justice system.

Maddy deLone, executive director of the Innocence Project in New York, expressed concern that Avery's case would not impact the organization's mission to exonerate the wrongly accused. Since 1989, DNA testing has led to the exoneration of 163 individuals, including Avery, with only one of them committing a serious crime after being released. Keith Findley, co-founder of the Wisconsin Innocence Project, emphasized that the organization's goal is to uncover the truth and ensure proper punishment for those responsible, rather than solely focusing on releasing the innocent.

However, the new allegations against Avery have sparked a backlash among some who believe that his past behavior, including convictions for burglary and animal cruelty, indicates a propensity for violence. Critics argue that without the efforts of the Wisconsin Innocence Project, Halbach might still be alive. Avery's family maintains his innocence, with his father, Al Avery, claiming that the evidence was planted and that his son was wrongly accused.

The case has also raised questions about the reliability of eyewitness testimony, as Avery was initially convicted based on the victim's identification. New DNA testing later revealed that the pubic hair found on the victim belonged to another individual, who was already incarcerated for a separate sexual assault. The case has become a contentious issue, with some residents of Manitowoc expressing skepticism about Avery's guilt, while others firmly believe he was guilty of the original crime.

The controversy surrounding Avery's case has also affected the legislative efforts to pass the Criminal Justice Reforms package, which aimed to reform law enforcement practices in Wisconsin. The future of Avery's civil lawsuit, which sought $36 million in damages, remains uncertain, with depositions postponed and the possibility of a tainted jury pool looming.

reference: – Ten years before Making a Murderer fans started debating Steven Avery's case at just about every water cooler in the country, Monica Davey was just another reporter covering Avery's arrest on suspicion of the 2005 murder of Teresa Halbach in Manitowoc, Wis. The news came just as Avery was pursuing a $36 million civil lawsuit against a former prosecutor and sheriff, having recently been acquitted of a sexual assault for which he spent 18 years in prison. "There are 36 million reasons why they should be doing this to him," Davey quoted Avery's brother as saying in a 2005 New York Times article. It caught the eye of two graduate student filmmakers, who would spend the next decade creating Making a Murderer. The series has forced Manitowoc to relive its darkest history and has left the county transformed, Davey writes at the Times following a return visit. While the county tourism office used to field calls about jogging paths and beaches, callers now question why the office would want to draw visitors to such a town. The local police and sheriff's departments, city hall, and even the Manitowoc County Historical Society are forced to listen to "yelling, cussing, and swearing," says an official. As for Manitowoc residents, "the talkative, curious people I had come upon a decade earlier … avoided any talk about Making a Murderer, or simply spotted my notebook and walked away," writes Davey. One, however, said she was fearful of a rally in support of Avery, scheduled for Friday, per WBAY. Even Avery's mother, who ardently defends her son, says the calls for interviews are overwhelming. "I'm too old for this," she says. "It's too much." Read Davey's full piece here.

final_summary:
PERTH, Australia — The search for Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 resumed in earnest on Saturday, with surveillance planes returning to the skies above a newly defined search area in the southern Indian Ocean. This new area, located approximately 1,100 miles west of Perth, was identified after a re-analysis of radar data revealed that the plane was moving faster than initially estimated, causing it to burn fuel more quickly and potentially fall into the ocean farther north than previously believed.

Associated Press reported that two aircraft spotted floating debris in the new search area on Saturday, marking the second day of sightings. However, officials cautioned that these findings were inconclusive due to the presence of ordinary oceanic debris. 

A flotilla of vessels, including ships from China and Australia, arrived in the search zone to investigate the potential wreckage. While several objects were retrieved from the water, investigators determined that they were not from the missing plane.

The new search area, spanning approximately 123,000 square miles, poses both advantages and challenges. On one hand, it offers more favorable weather conditions and is closer to Perth, making it easier for search planes to operate. On the other hand, it is a vast expanse of ocean, requiring a concerted effort to scour the area effectively.

Australian Prime Minister Tony Abbott acknowledged the difficulties of the search, stating, "We are trying to find small bits of wreckage in a vast ocean." Despite these challenges, the Australian and Malaysian authorities remain committed to finding the wreckage and determining the cause of the plane's disappearance.

In related developments, a Chinese military plane spotted several objects floating in the sea, including two bearing colors similar to those of the missing jet. However, officials emphasized that these findings were preliminary and required further verification.

Malaysia Airlines' commercial director, Hugh Dunleavy, announced that around 40 to 45 Chinese relatives of passengers on the missing plane would travel to Beijing on Sunday to meet with Malaysian government officials. The relatives have expressed dissatisfaction with the responses provided by Malaysian authorities and seek a meeting with the prime minister and transportation minister.

If the plane is found to have crashed in the newly targeted search zone, recovering its flight data and cockpit voice recorders – commonly referred to as black boxes – could be complicated due to the depth of the ocean. The black boxes can survive submersion in saltwater for up to 20,000 feet, but the deepest waters in the search zone are approximately 13,000 feet.

Experts believe that the black boxes can preserve critical data for up to two years or longer, even if submerged in the corrosive saltwater of the Indian Ocean. If recovered, the recorders can be cleaned and dried using desalination techniques, allowing technicians to extract valuable information about the flight's final moments.

The search for Flight 370 has been ongoing for three weeks, with the initial focus shifting from the Gulf of Thailand to the Andaman Sea and eventually to the southern Indian Ocean. The Australian Maritime Safety Authority is leading the search efforts, working closely with international partners, including Boeing and the US Navy.

final_summary:
Daivon Reeder, a 22-year-old from Detroit, is set to make history as the first member of his family to graduate from Eastern Michigan University (EMU) on graduation day. Despite facing numerous challenges throughout his academic journey, including financial struggles, family problems, and racial disparities as a black male student at a predominantly white university, Reeder persevered and is now on the cusp of achieving his dream.

Reeder's remarkable story began when his stepfather doubted his ability to succeed, telling him that attending orientation was pointless and that he wouldn't graduate. However, Reeder took those words as motivation and proved his stepfather wrong. He tweeted about the encounter, saying, "My stepdad told me it was pointless to go to orientation I wasn't going to graduate anyway. 4 years later he is in jail and I am well...". The tweet has since gone viral, garnering thousands of retweets and shares, with many congratulating Reeder on his achievement.

Throughout his time at EMU, Reeder worked tirelessly to overcome obstacles, including losing a scholarship and having to attend basic training for the Army before returning to school. He also took on two jobs to support his family, demonstrating his resilience and determination. In addition to his academic pursuits, Reeder co-founded an organization with nine other students to mentor young boys and teach them valuable life skills, which he learned through his own experiences.

As Reeder prepares to receive his diploma, he reflects on the lessons he's learned, saying, "Just because somebody says you can't do something seems hard or it's not the norm, or it's something your family is not used to doing, you can be the change. You can be the difference." With his cap and gown in hand, Reeder will join his fellow graduates at EMU, marking a significant milestone in his life.

The Internet Archive, a non-profit organization dedicated to preserving online content, has been working to preserve web pages since 1996, including those related to government websites and data. Their efforts aim to ensure that important information remains accessible even after it is removed or altered. In 2016, the organization launched the End of Term project, which focused on documenting the federal government's web presence. This initiative has continued to evolve, with the current project allowing for the ongoing archiving of publicly nominated websites, including those related to government agencies and social media presence.

history.draft:
Daivon Reeder, a 22-year-old from Detroit, is set to make history as the first member of his family to graduate from Eastern Michigan University (EMU) on graduation day. Despite facing numerous challenges throughout his academic journey, including financial struggles, family problems, and racial disparities as a black male student at a predominantly white university, Reeder persevered and is now on the cusp of achieving his dream.

Reeder's remarkable story began when his stepfather doubted his ability to succeed, telling him that attending orientation was pointless and that he wouldn't graduate. However, Reeder took those words as motivation and proved his stepfather wrong. He tweeted about the encounter, saying, "My stepdad told me it was pointless to go to orientation I wasn't going to graduate anyway. 4 years later he is in jail and I am well...". The tweet has since gone viral, garnering thousands of retweets and shares, with many congratulating Reeder on his achievement.

Throughout his time at EMU, Reeder worked tirelessly to overcome obstacles, including losing a scholarship and having to attend basic training for the Army before returning to school. He also took on two jobs to support his family, demonstrating his resilience and determination. In addition to his academic pursuits, Reeder co-founded an organization with nine other students to mentor young boys and teach them valuable life skills, which he learned through his own experiences.

As Reeder prepares to receive his diploma, he reflects on the lessons he's learned, saying, "Just because somebody says you can't do something seems hard or it's not the norm, or it's something your family is not used to doing, you can be the change. You can be the difference." With his cap and gown in hand, Reeder will join his fellow graduates at EMU, marking a significant milestone in his life.

The Internet Archive, a non-profit organization dedicated to preserving online content, has been working to preserve web pages since 1996, including those related to government websites and data. Their efforts aim to ensure that important information remains accessible even after it is removed or altered. In 2016, the organization launched the End of Term project, which focused on documenting the federal government's web presence. This initiative has continued to evolve, with the current project allowing for the ongoing archiving of publicly nominated websites, including those related to government agencies and social media presence.

Hours later, the tweet began trending in Detroit, catching the attention of Twitter as well as the media. Local Detroit station WDIV 4 featured a segment on Reeder, who graduated Saturday with a degree in criminal justice and a minor in military science. He told the station that after losing his academic scholarship, those words pushed him even harder to succeed. "[I have] the average, typical, inner-city Detroit boy story," Reeder, 22, says. "I grew up constantly moving houses." The eldest of four, Reeder said his mother struggled to make ends meet for his family, and his stepfather was not always in the picture. After his stepdad made the comment about graduating, "I kind of heard that and I was like OK, I'll show you," Reeder tells Fox 8. final_summary:
On Tuesday, Amazon, Berkshire Hathaway, and JPMorgan Chase announced a groundbreaking partnership aimed at cutting healthcare costs and improving services for their U.S. employees in the United States. The joint venture, which brings together two of the world's most influential companies and a prominent banking institution, seeks to revolutionize the U.S. healthcare system by leveraging their collective resources and expertise. The new company, established to operate independently without profit-driven motives, will initially focus on developing technology solutions to simplify healthcare access, enhance quality, and increase transparency for millions of employees and their families.

Warren Buffett, chairman and CEO of Berkshire Hathaway, described the ballooning healthcare costs as a "hungry tapeworm on the American economy," emphasizing that the trio does not claim to have all the answers but is committed to tackling the issue head-on. Jeff Bezos, Amazon's founder and CEO, acknowledged the complexity of the challenge, stating that success will require collaboration, innovative thinking, and a long-term perspective. Jamie Dimon, JPMorgan Chase's chairman and CEO, expressed his team's desire for transparency, knowledge, and control in managing healthcare, highlighting the potential for the partnership to benefit not only their employees but also the broader American public.

The partnership marks a significant development in Amazon's growing involvement in the healthcare sector, which has been eagerly anticipated by investors and industry observers. As a company known for disrupting various markets, Amazon's entry into healthcare has sparked excitement and concern within the industry. The tech giant's influence has already been felt in sectors such as retail, groceries, and finance, and its foray into healthcare promises to shake things up further.

Berkshire Hathaway, led by the seasoned investor Warren Buffett, brings its vast financial expertise and experience in navigating complex business ventures to the table. JPMorgan Chase, under the leadership of Jamie Dimon, contributes its extensive knowledge of the financial sector and its ability to navigate the intricacies of large-scale operations. Together, the three partners aim to leverage their combined strengths to drive meaningful change in the U.S. healthcare landscape.

As news of the partnership broke, shares of major healthcare companies experienced a sharp decline, indicating the potential impact of the new entity on the existing healthcare system. The partnership's goals are ambitious, and its success will depend on its ability to develop effective solutions that address the pressing issues plaguing the U.S. healthcare system, including rising costs, lack of transparency, and limited access to quality care. Despite the challenges ahead, the partnership represents a significant step towards creating a more efficient, patient-centered, and cost-effective healthcare system for millions of Americans.

reference: – "The ballooning costs of health care act as a hungry tapeworm on the American economy," says Berkshire Hathaway CEO Warren Buffett, and he has teamed up with some equally huge names in an attempt to go after said tapeworm. Amazon.com, Berkshire Hathaway, and JPMorgan Chase on Tuesday announced that they'd be creating a company tasked with coming up with technological solutions that can cut health-care costs for their hundreds of thousands of US employees. "Our group does not come to this problem with answers," Buffett notes—nor, reports the Wall Street Journal, with details on how much it plans to spend, where the company's HQ will be, or how many of its own employees would be impacted—"but we also do not accept it as inevitable." What the companies' leaders did reveal is that the new company will be "free from profit-making incentives and constraints." "The healthcare system is complex, and we enter into this challenge open-eyed about the degree of difficulty," says Jeff Bezos of Amazon, per CNBC, which notes the three companies employ a total 1.1 million workers, though that figure is not exclusive to the US. The AP reports it's unclear whether the trio would welcome other companies into the effort, but it does flag this line from JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon: "Our goal is to create solutions that benefit our US employees, their families and, potentially, all Americans." But they're not exactly benefiting Wall Street at the moment: Pre-market trading was unkind to health care companies, with the AP noting they represented eight of the 10 down companies on the S&P 500.

final_summary:
Miamian Arturo Carvajal, a doctor with a family practice in Hollywood, was mystified by the artichoke when he dined at Houston's in North Miami Beach in May 2009. His lawyer, Marc R. Ginsberg, notes that it takes a sophisticated diner to be familiar with the artichoke, and Carvajal, being from Cuba, may have been accustomed to consuming all parts of a meal. However, this cultural background did not exempt him from understanding basic dining etiquette.

Carvajal had never seen or heard of the artichoke before and ordered the grilled-artichoke special, which was not listed on the menu. His server failed to inform him that the outside portion of the artichoke leaves is indigestible and should not be consumed. Carvajal, being unfamiliar with the proper method of eating an artichoke, proceeded to chew on all the leaves, including the tough and sharp outer portions. This decision led to severe abdominal pain and discomfort, prompting him to seek medical attention at a local hospital. An exploratory laparotomy revealed that artichoke leaves had become lodged in his small bowel.

This October 27, Carvajal filed a lawsuit against Houston's Restaurant, owned by Hillstone Restaurant Group, alleging negligence and seeking unspecified damages for bodily injury, pain and suffering, disability, disfigurement, aggravation of pre-existing conditions, and medical expenses. The lawsuit claimed that the restaurant and its manager were responsible for failing to train their staff to advise customers on the proper method of consuming an artichoke.

Riptide called Hillstone Restaurant Group for comment, but received no response. Business Insurance reported that a spokesperson for the group dismissed the lawsuit as a "silly notion," suggesting that posting warnings against eating entire artichokes would be unnecessary, comparing it to warning customers not to eat the bones in barbecue ribs. The company plans to vigorously defend itself against the lawsuit.

Despite having never seen or heard of the vegetable before, Carvajal ordered the grilled-artichoke special, which was not listed on the menu. His server failed to inform him that the outside portion of the artichoke leaves is indigestible and should not be consumed.

Carvajal, being unfamiliar with the proper method of eating an artichoke, proceeded to chew on all the leaves, including the tough and sharp outer portions. This decision led to severe abdominal pain and discomfort, prompting him to seek medical attention at a local hospital. An exploratory laparotomy revealed that artichoke leaves had become lodged in his small bowel.

According to Carvajal's lawyer, Marc R. Ginsberg, it requires a certain level of sophistication to be familiar with artichokes, and Carvajal, being from Cuba, may have been accustomed to consuming all parts of a meal. However, this cultural background did not exempt him from understanding basic dining etiquette in the United States.

On October 27, Carvajal filed a lawsuit against Houston's Restaurant, owned by Hillstone Restaurant Group, alleging negligence and seeking unspecified damages for bodily injury, pain and suffering, disability, disfigurement, aggravation of pre-existing conditions, and medical expenses. Hillstone Restaurant Group's vice president and general counsel, Glenn Viers, dismissed the lawsuit as a "silly notion," suggesting that posting warnings against eating entire artichokes would be unnecessary, comparing it to warning customers not to eat the bones in barbecue ribs. The company plans to vigorously defend itself against the lawsuit.

reference: – Just because Arturo Carvajal is a doctor, that doesn't mean he knows how to eat an artichoke—and he's suing a Miami restaurant for not instructing him on the proper method. Carvajal ordered the grilled artichoke (a vegetable he had "never seen nor heard of previously," according to court filings) and proceeded to eat the entire thing, rather than scraping just the meat off of the leaves. He later found himself experiencing abdominal pain, and doctors found artichoke leaves lodged in his bowel. He filed a suit seeking unspecified damages last month, the Miami New Times reports. In it, he complains that the server failed "to explain the proper method of consuming an artichoke." His lawyer explains that Carvajal thought "it was like a food he might have eaten in his native Cuba, where you eat everything on the plate." The restaurant's lawyer begs to differ: "What's next?" he asks Business Insurance. "Are we going to have to post warnings on our menu they shouldn't eat the bones in our barbecue ribs?" Click here for more wacky lawsuits.

final_summary:
In a significant development, a group of 3,000 individuals in Sweden have successfully secured official recognition for their organisation, known as the Missionary Church of Kopimism, as a legitimate religion by the Swedish authorities. This milestone marks a culmination of efforts initiated in 2010, when the group first sought to have their unique set of beliefs acknowledged. The Church's name, derived from the term "Kopimi," meaning "copy me" in Swedish, reflects its core tenets centered around the sanctity of communication and data sharing. 

The Church's ideology emphasizes the importance of respecting the act of sharing and opposes monitoring and eavesdropping on individuals. Despite previous denials, including one in July 2011, the Church persevered in its pursuit of official recognition. Just before Christmas, the Church received the long-awaited news that its application had been approved by the National Judicial Board for Public Lands & Funds, after formally establishing its way of praying or meditation. According to Isak Gerson, the founder and a philosophy student, the Church's primary ritual revolves around the act of copying and connecting with others through information sharing. Gerson expressed optimism that the newfound recognition would bolster the Church's identity and potentially influence future legislative decisions regarding copyright laws. Notably, the official status does not grant immunity from copyright infringement laws, but rather serves as a symbolic acknowledgment of the Church's existence.

Gustav Nipe, the chairman of the Church, joined forces with Gerson in their quest for legitimacy. As the Church continues to grow, having tripled its membership from 1,000 to 3,000 over the past half-year, its leader remains committed to spreading the message of Kopimism. Isak Gerson encouraged all individuals with internet access to continue sharing information, stating that the Church's mission is not limited to its official members. With this development, the Church of Kopimism joins the ranks of recognized religions in Sweden, marking a significant milestone in the evolution of this unconventional faith.

reference: – Sweden may not condone file-sharing—it's still illegal—but the country is officially OK with belief in the practice. Some 3,000 passionate file-sharers have gotten their beliefs recognized as an official religion. The Missionary Church of Kopimism—as in, "copy-me-ism"—has sought official status in Sweden since 2010, and it's finally won the battle, TorrentFreak reports. The decision follows a pair of rejections; authorities wanted Kopimism to define its prayer practices. "Our main ritual is the act of copying and connecting with each other by sharing information," the church's founder tells Wired. Now, Isak Gerson says, "more people will have the courage to step out as Kopimists. Maybe not in the public, but at least to their close ones." He hopes that the recognition will have an effect on future legislation surrounding file-sharing. Meanwhile, he has some advice for fellow believers: "Keep copying. Maintain hardline Kopimi."

final_summary:
In a landmark conviction, Nechemya Weberman, a 54-year-old respected religious counselor in New York's ultra-orthodox Jewish community in Williamsburg, Brooklyn, was found guilty of 59 counts of sexual abuse against a young girl who had sought his counsel due to questioning her faith and dressing immodestly according to the sect's customs. The Brooklyn courtroom fell silent as the foreman announced the verdict on Monday, which carries a maximum combined sentence of 117 years, with the top charge alone carrying a 25-year prison term. Weberman faces sentencing on January 9.

The abuse, which spanned three years, involved the counselor taking advantage of his position to engage in various forms of sexual misconduct, including oral sex, groping, and forcing the girl to reenact scenes from pornographic videos during therapy sessions intended to guide her towards greater religiosity. The victim, now 18 and married, recounted the traumatic experience in graphic detail during a four-day testimony, stating that she felt broken and suicidal throughout the ordeal.

Defense attorneys, led by George Farkas, maintained that the jury failed to grasp the complexities of the case and vowed to appeal the decision. However, prosecutors argued that Weberman exploited his position as a trusted advisor to satisfy his own desires, intimidating the girl into silence through fear and manipulation.

The trial shed light on the insular nature of the Satmar Hasidic sect, which governs the community's interactions with the outside world and enforces strict rules regarding attire, social behavior, and contact with outsiders. Members of the community, including prominent figures, rallied in support of Weberman, while others, like Judy Genut, expressed relief and hope that the conviction might pave the way for others to come forward with similar claims.

Brooklyn District Attorney Charles Hynes acknowledged the challenges posed by the community's culture of intimidation and secrecy, which often deters victims from reporting abuse. Despite these obstacles, Hynes expressed optimism that the case could mark a turning point in addressing the issue within the ultra-orthodox community, citing the bravery of the victim and the potential for further investigations into alleged abuses.

On the day of the sentencing, scheduled for January 9, the victim delivered a poignant statement, expressing her relief and resilience in the face of trauma. Meanwhile, Weberman's lawyer reiterated his client's innocence, paving the way for an anticipated appeal. As the community grapples with the aftermath of this high-profile case, many are left wondering whether the conviction will serve as a catalyst for change within the Satmar community, particularly in regards to its handling of allegations of sexual abuse.

As the community grapples with the aftermath of this high-profile case, many are left wondering whether the conviction will serve as a catalyst for change within the Satmar community, particularly in regards to its handling of allegations of sexual abuse.

reference: – Nechemya Weberman, an unlicensed therapist and powerful member of the Hasidic Jewish community of Brooklyn, will likely spend the rest of his life behind bars after being convicted of 59 counts of sexual abuse yesterday, reports the New York Times. After days of testimony from a victim who says she was repeatedly abused by Weberman starting when she was 12, a judge sentenced him to 103 years in prison, just short of the 117-year maximum. During her testimony, the victim, now 18, told Weberman that he "played around with and destroyed lives as if they were your toys, without the slightest bit of mercy.” Weberman's crimes included various sexual acts with minors, sometimes during therapy sessions under the guise of helping girls become more religious. The case is the first in two decades Brooklyn DA Charles Hynes has brought against a member of the Satmar ultra-Orthodox community. While many criticize Hynes for not being aggressive enough toward Hasidic abusers, he says the self-silencing culture of the Satmar community is to blame. The case has divided the community, even leading to violence against a victim advocate.

final_summary:
In a major crackdown on child pornography and sexual abuse, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), through its Homeland Security Investigations division, launched Operation Sunflower, a five-week operation spanning 46 states and six countries, including the United States, resulting in the arrest of 245 suspects and identification of 123 victims of child exploitation. The operation, which concluded in November and December, saw law enforcement officials remove 44 children from their alleged abusers, while 79 others were identified as having been exploited by individuals outside their homes or were adults who were victimized as children.

The youngest victims identified were as young as 3 years old, with five under the age of 5 and nine between 4 and 6 years old. A total of 110 victims lived in the United States, spread across 19 different states, with the remaining victims located in six other countries whose identities were not disclosed due to ongoing international cooperation. ICE officials emphasized the importance of collaboration with international law enforcement partners in combating global child exploitation.

ICE Director John Morton highlighted the significance of forensic analysis technology in tracking down perpetrators and identifying victims, stating that such tools enable the agency to follow digital trails of abuse that transcend borders. Morton noted that the agency works closely with the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children to analyze images and track down both victims and perpetrators.

One notable case involved Michael Lindsay, who was arrested at San Francisco International Airport on suspicion of engaging in child sex tourism while attempting to board a flight to Manila, Philippines. Lindsay had previously returned from a trip to the Philippines, where he faced allegations of having sexual contact with a 13-year-old girl. Further investigation revealed that Lindsay may have had contact with additional children, prompting agents to seek out potential victims.

Operation Sunflower drew inspiration from a 2011 case in which Danish law enforcement officials collaborated with ICE to rescue an 11-year-old girl from rape. By analyzing a video featuring a sunflower-shaped road sign, agents were able to pinpoint the location of the suspect and victim in Kansas, marking the first success of ICE's Victim Identification Program.

As part of the operation, ICE agents in the Bay Area secured five indictments and arrested four individuals, ranging in age from their early 20s to early 50s, with one suspect remaining at large. In the Fresno area, 14 residents were arrested, and three children were rescued, including a 7-year-old girl with disabilities. Morton appealed to the public for assistance in identifying three individuals linked to unsolved Operation Sunflower cases, including a woman with distinctive tattoos and two white male suspects accused of abusing prepubescent toddlers. Those with information or tips can contact ICE at 1-866-DHS-2-ICE or visit www.ICE.gov/tips.
