reference: – A massive child-porn bust has led to 245 arrests and the identification of 123 exploited children, some as young as two, reports the Contra Costa Times. The crackdown, dubbed Operation Sunflower, began Nov. 1 and ran for five weeks, and extended to 46 states and six countries, officials revealed yesterday. Of the 123 children, 44 were living with their alleged abusers. Five of the children were three or younger, and 41 were between the ages of four and 12. California led the way with 37 arrests, and all but 23 of those arrested were in the United States, reports CNN. "Whenever our investigations reveal the production and distribution of new child pornography online, we will do everything we can to rescue the victim and prosecute the abuser, even if takes us years or (going) around the world to do it," said ICE's director. Investigators say the are continuing to make arrests and attempting to identify further victims. The operation's name is a nod to the impressive 2011 rescue of an 11-year-old in Kansas; click for the fascinating story of how ICE agents tracked her down.

final_summary:
On Monday, a devastating explosion ripped through the Boston Marathon finish line, claiming the lives of several innocent bystanders, including an 8-year-old boy named Martin Richard from Dorchester. Martin's mother, Denise, and 7-year-old sister suffered serious injuries in the blast. The family's father, Bill Richard, released a heartfelt statement expressing his gratitude for the support of their community and asking for continued prayers as they mourned the loss of their beloved son. Neighbors and strangers alike gathered outside the Richard family home in the Ashmont section of Dorchester, leaving flowers and candles in memory of Martin, who was remembered as a kind and caring young boy who had a passion for learning.

Martin was at the marathon to watch his father cross the finish line, which he had watched his father do for years. He was a former student at Pope John Paul II Catholic Academy, where he was described as a bright and enthusiastic learner. The school community came together to offer their condolences to the Richard family, praising Martin's warm personality and spirit.

Tragedy struck multiple families in the aftermath of the bombings. Krystle Campbell, a restaurant manager from Medford, was identified as the second victim of the attacks. Her parents, William and Patty Campbell, received devastating news when they were initially told that their daughter had survived the blast, only to discover later that it was actually their friend, Karen Rand, who had perished. Krystle's mother, Patty, broke down in tears as she remembered her daughter as a caring and loving person who worked tirelessly in the food service industry.

As the city of Boston struggled to come to terms with the senseless violence, authorities vowed to bring those responsible to justice. FBI officials assured the public that there were no known additional threats, and Mayor Thomas Menino pledged that the city would not be intimidated by terrorism. The mayor emphasized that Boston was a resilient city that would pull together in the face of adversity.

Tuesday evening, the third victim was identified as a Boston University graduate student from China who was watching the race with two friends near the finish line. The Chinese Consulate in New York confirmed the student's death, and the university did not release the student's name or gender, pending family approval.

Meanwhile, the city began to show signs of returning to normalcy, with planes, trains, and buses resuming their regular schedules. However, the impact of the bombings was still palpable, particularly in the neighborhood surrounding the finish line, where a makeshift memorial grew as people left flowers, balloons, and messages of condolence for the victims and their families.

In the midst of the chaos, the community rallied around each other, providing support and comfort to those affected by the tragedy. The Salvation Army deployed mobile kitchens and volunteers to provide food and emotional aid, while the First Corps of Cadets Armory served as a hub for runners searching for their belongings and making travel arrangements. As the city began to heal, officials expressed their commitment to finding justice and rebuilding the community.

reference: – Another fatality in the Boston bombings has been identified: The family of 29-year-old Krystle Campbell says she was killed as she and a friend were cheering on another friend who was racing, reports WCVB. "This is just a waste," says her mother. “Everyone that knew her loved her. She was always smiling. You couldn’t ask for a better daughter. She was the best.” Campbell was a restaurant manager, reports the Boston Herald, which notes an especially wrenching detail: Her father was initially told she was alive, but when he got to the ICU, he realized it was his daughter's friend who had survived. Campbell is the second of the three people killed to be identified. The first was 8-year-old Martin Richard, and his father, Bill, released a statement today thanking supporters and asking for privacy, reports WBZ. "My dear son Martin has died from injuries sustained in the attack on Boston. My wife and daughter are both recovering from serious injuries. We thank our family and friends, those we know and those we have never met, for their thoughts and prayers. I ask that you continue to pray for my family as we remember Martin. We also ask for your patience and for privacy as we work to simultaneously grieve and recover. Thank you." The third victim was a grad student at Boston University, but the person's identity is being withheld until the family says it's OK to release, reports USA Today.

final_summary:
Tiger Woods, the world-renowned professional golfer, is embroiled in yet another scandal surrounding allegations of infidelity. According to exclusive reports by RadarOnline.com, Devon James, a former adult film actress and escort, claims that Woods is the father of her secret love child. This shocking revelation comes amidst ongoing rumors of Woods' numerous extramarital affairs, which have been linked to at least 14 other women.

James alleges that she had a two-and-a-half year affair with Woods, during which time they engaged in unprotected sex, resulting in her becoming pregnant in early 2001. At the time, Woods was not yet married to his current wife, Elin Nordegren. It is reported that James and Woods reconnected in 2006, at which point she informed him that she believed their son was his biological child. She even claimed to have told him this in person, and a source close to the situation revealed that James remains adamant about her claim.

RadarOnline.com has obtained photographs of the alleged love child, who bears a striking resemblance to Woods. Although Woods has not undergone a paternity test, a source close to the situation revealed that James remains insistent about her claim. Furthermore, RadarOnline.com has discovered that James discussed her alleged relationship with Woods on the set of a forthcoming adult film in Los Angeles.

It is reported that James claimed to have had a two-girl party with Woods, where she and another woman were paid $2,000 each. This incident allegedly led to her pregnancy. 

This latest scandal follows previous reports of Woods' alleged infidelities, including a rumored affair with Theresa Rogers, who gave birth to a daughter in 2003 and sought $2 million in hush money. Another woman, also linked to Woods, attempted to pass off her child as his own, although sources close to her denied the claim.

As Woods prepares to compete in the US Open, his wife Elin and their two children are currently visiting her brother in Shanghai, China. Meanwhile, a journalist involved in a Channel 4 documentary, "Tiger Woods: The Rise and Fall," has come forward stating that there is substantial evidence, including DNA proof, supporting the existence of a secret love child fathered by Woods.

reference: – Guess who’s back in the news: Everyone’s favorite golfer…and his love children. Yes, another mistress has come forward claiming to have given birth to Tiger's child in 2001. This time it’s porn star/escort Devon James—whose son, Radar notes, looks an awful lot like the golfer. As if that’s not bad enough, a new documentary airing Thursday in the UK will delve into the mystery of Tiger Woods’ first purported love child, the Sun reports. Tiger Woods: The Rise and Fall includes interviews with journalist Neal Boulton, who says he knows someone who has DNA evidence that backs up the secret-baby claim and adds, “Tiger will eventually admit to fathering a child.” If this all sounds vaguely familiar, it’s because alleged mistress Theresa Rogers—who reportedly gave birth to Tiger’s daughter in 2003 and is the subject of the documentary’s claims—has been crying love child for months.

final_summary:
In a bid to revolutionize the aviation industry, Airbus has unveiled its latest innovation, the A350 XWB jet, designed to tackle some of the most pressing issues faced by flyers, including jet lag, cramped seating, and inefficient fuel consumption. According to Alain De Zotti, chief engineer of the A350 XWB program at Airbus, the design process focused on optimization, pushing boundaries, and exploring novel solutions to address these concerns. The result is a game-changing aircraft that boasts cutting-edge features aimed at enhancing passenger comfort and reducing the environmental impact of long-haul flights. 

The A350 XWB made its debut in early 2015, marking a significant milestone in the company's efforts to push the boundaries of aircraft design. One of the standout features of the A350 XWB is its innovative use of materials, with over 50% of the structure composed of carbon fiber-reinforced plastic, making it significantly lighter and more durable than traditional aluminum planes. This reduction in weight translates to improved fuel efficiency, with Airbus claiming a 25% better fuel economy compared to similar-sized planes from competitors, such as the Boeing 787 Dreamliner and 777 long-distance planes. The aircraft's larger and wider wings, combined with advanced flap systems that follow the flight path direction, contribute to this remarkable improvement.

To combat the debilitating effects of jet lag, the A350 XWB incorporates a unique lighting system that mimics the natural sunlight and adjusts its color temperature to synchronize with the human body's circadian rhythms. Additionally, the plane's air filtration system refreshes the cabin air every two to three minutes, maintaining a comfortable pressure level equivalent to 6,000 feet above sea level. These features aim to minimize the physical toll and stress associated with crossing multiple time zones.

Passengers can also expect enhanced comfort in the cabin, with slightly wider and more spacious economy seats, allowing for easier movement down the aisles. The interior design is modular, enabling airlines to customize the layout and configuration to suit their specific needs, whether it's accommodating more business-class passengers or optimizing space utilization. As Alain De Zotti notes, the A350 XWB's open architecture allows for seamless integration of new technologies and software updates, ensuring the aircraft remains relevant for the next 30 years. 

Qatar Airways was the first airline to introduce the A350 XWB to its fleet, with the inaugural flight landing in the United States last week. Despite a minor hiccup during the maiden voyage, where the plane aborted takeoff due to a short runway, the aircraft has undergone rigorous testing, accumulating 2,600 hours of flight time. Backed by a substantial investment of $15 billion and set to serve for the next 30 years, the A350 XWB is poised to redefine the standards of commercial air travel, offering a more efficient, comfortable, and enjoyable experience for passengers worldwide.

To combat the debilitating effects of jet lag, the A350 XWB incorporates a unique lighting system that mimics the natural sunlight and adjusts its color temperature to synchronize with the human body's circadian rhythms. Additionally, the plane's air filtration system refreshes the cabin air every two to three minutes, maintaining a comfortable pressure level equivalent to 6,000 feet above sea level. These features aim to minimize the physical toll and stress associated with crossing multiple time zones.

Passengers can also expect enhanced comfort in the cabin, with slightly wider and more spacious economy seats, allowing for easier movement down the aisles. The interior design is modular, enabling airlines to customize the layout and configuration to suit their specific needs, whether it's accommodating more business-class passengers or optimizing space utilization. As Alain De Zotti notes, the A350 XWB's open architecture allows for seamless integration of new technologies and software updates, ensuring the aircraft remains relevant for the next 30 years. 

Qatar Airways was the first airline to introduce the A350 XWB to its fleet, with the inaugural flight landing in the United States last week. Despite a minor hiccup during the maiden voyage, where the plane aborted takeoff due to a short runway, the aircraft has undergone rigorous testing, accumulating 2,600 hours of flight time. Backed by a substantial investment of $15 billion and set to serve for the next 30 years, the A350 XWB is poised to redefine the standards of commercial air travel, offering a more efficient, comfortable, and enjoyable experience for passengers worldwide.

reference: – If it works, it could be an air-traveling perk to trump all others: no more jet lag. Airbus is making the bold claim that it can at least reduce the malady for those aboard its new A350 XWB line of jets. The main tool is a set of LED lights within the cabin that mimics natural shifts in sunlight and theoretically keeps people's natural circadian rhythms in sync, reports Fast Company. Another factor: Because of the planes' relatively light frames, they can be pressurized to 6,000 feet, which the airline says allows for more humidity and makes the air pressure inside the aircraft closer to the pressure on the ground than is the case with most jets, per Quartz. A third factor is the jet's filtration system, which replaces the air in the cabin in its entirety every two to three minutes. The idea there is that, if nothing else, the improved air quality should help people sleep better on long flights. And the big question: Has it worked? It's a little too early to tell. The first such jet landed in the US only earlier this month via Qatar Airways, though other airlines plan to add the jet to their fleets, notes a post at Science Alert. (This protein might provide another way to fight jet lag.)

final_summary:
According to recent research conducted by Stéphanie Mazza and her team at the University of Lyon, published in the journal Psychological Science, sleep plays a crucial role in the learning process, extending far beyond mere memory recall. The study revealed that sleep not only enhances relearning, but also enables individuals to relearn information twice as quickly and up to three times more effectively compared to staying awake.

The research involved 40 French-speaking adults who were tasked with learning a list of 16 Swahili-French word pairs. Participants were divided into two groups - one that learned in the morning and relearned in the evening of a single day, and another that learned in the evening, slept overnight, and then relearned in the morning of the next day. The results showed that the group that slept between learning sessions performed significantly better in terms of relearning, recalling an average of 10 word pairs on their first attempt, compared to around seven attempts made by the group that stayed awake.

Moreover, the sleep group required fewer cycles to complete the relearning session, with the lowest-performing members still outperforming the highest-performing members of the wake group. When tested a week later, the sleep group demonstrated superior recall performance, with an average of 15 correctly recalled word pairs compared to 11 in the wake group. Six months later, the sleep group's recall performance remained three times better than that of the wake group, with an average of nine correctly recalled word pairs versus just three.

In a follow-up study, a control group of 20 participants underwent a similar procedure, but instead of relearning, they only briefly practiced the list of word pairs once. As a result, their subsequent recall performance was no better than the wake group, highlighting the importance of relearning in conjunction with sleep for long-term memory benefits.

Mazza and her team interpret their findings as evidence that sleep "transforms" memories, making the effects of initial learning stronger and facilitating subsequent relearning. They propose that sleep not only prevents memories from being forgotten but also makes it easier to restore memories during relearning. Furthermore, the study found a positive correlation between total sleep time and recall and relearning performance, suggesting that sleep duration may play a significant role in enhancing learning and memory.

However, the study's design, which involved training at different times of day, raises questions about the generalizability of the findings. Tim Rickard notes that varied time experimental designs often yield better performance in sleep vs. wake groups, regardless of a sleep-specific learning benefit. He suggests that this may be due to individual differences in fatigue levels, alertness, and other characteristics that vary according to the time of day and natural sleep-wake cycle.

Despite these limitations, the study provides compelling evidence that incorporating sleep into learning strategies can be a powerful tool for improving long-term retention of information. The researchers recommend scheduling study sessions shortly before bedtime and repeating material across multiple study sessions to maximize the benefits of sleep on learning and memory. In a world where getting a full night's sleep is becoming increasingly rare, embracing the idea of resting after a day of learning may hold the key to unlocking our full cognitive potential.

reference: – It's well established through previous research that sleep after learning is best for many memory-related tasks, including word lists, mazes, auditory tones, and so on. Sleep seems so vital to recall that some speculate it is directly responsible for, not just supportive of, learning, reports Scientific American. So researchers out of the University of Lyon chose to investigate another aspect of learning—not recall but relearning, where something previously learned has been forgotten and must be re-acquired. Reporting in the journal Psychological Science in August, they wroite that, coupled with practice, "Sleep makes perfect," and that "sleeping after learning is definitely a good strategy, but sleeping between two learning sessions is a better strategy." The team tested 40 French-speaking adults tasked with learning 16 Swahili words. Some learned them at night, slept, and relearned them in the morning, while others learned the words in the morning, didn't sleep, and relearned them at night. The group that slept between sessions performed so much better that even those who forgot the most words relearned them faster during their morning session than the least forgetful members of the group relearning the words at night. The results don't clarify which is at play—sleep boosting learning, which helps improve later relearning, or sleep simply allowing for uninterrupted learning processes to speed up relearning—but either way, sleeping between studying seems worth a shot. (This woman only sleeps three hours a night.)

final_summary:
In 1996, Alexa Internet began donating its daily crawl data to the Internet Archive, which would eventually be added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. Meanwhile, a trend emerged in America's wealthiest communities, as revealed by IRS data analyzed by Forbes. According to the findings, affluent individuals tend to favor warm climates and low-tax environments when deciding where to relocate.

The top destination for the wealthy in 2008, based on IRS data, was Collier County, Fla., which encompasses the city of Naples. A total of 15,150 tax returns accounted for individuals moving to Collier County from other parts of the country, boasting an average reported income of $76,161 per person, equivalent to $304,644 for a family of four. Notably, although slightly more taxpayers left Collier County than arrived, the departing residents' average income was significantly lower at $26,128 per person.

Households migrating to Collier County primarily originated from within Fla., with nearby counties such as Lee, Broward, Palm Beach, and Orange leading the list. Northern cities like Suffolk County, N.Y., Cook County, and Oakland County also contributed substantial numbers of movers to Collier County.

Greene County, Ga., ranked second on the list, with a population of approximately 15,743 residents, according to the last estimate. In 2008, 788 people moved to the county, roughly 75 miles east of Atlanta.

Florida and Texas dominated the top 20 counties, with eight and four locations respectively, due to their lack of income tax. Robert Shrum, manager of state affairs at the Tax Foundation in Washington, D.C., attributed this phenomenon to the significant tax benefits offered by both states. After accounting for property taxes, his analysis revealed that Texas boasts the fourth-lowest personal tax burden nationwide, while Florida ranks eighth.

Pitkin County, Colo., famous for its expensive Aspen ski community, witnessed a notable exodus of affluent individuals in 2008 as the economy contracted. The 962 tax filers and dependents who departed had an average income of $71,473 per capita, whereas those moving to the county averaged $30,000 lower. Many of those leaving Pitkin County relocated to neighboring Garfield County, where homes were significantly cheaper, listing for 75% less than those in Pitkin County.

Forbes employed IRS data on household moves, categorized by county and income, to identify areas favored by the wealthy. They considered counties where incoming households earned higher incomes than those that did not move and outgoing households earned lower incomes. Conversely, to determine destinations the rich were fleeing, they reversed these criteria, focusing on counties where departing households were wealthier and incoming households were poorer. By applying these methods, Forbes identified emerging hotspots where the wealthy were relocating, often to areas with favorable tax conditions.

A new Forbes analysis of IRS data from 2008 shows Collier County, Fla., at the head of the pack, picking up 15,150 new residents with an average income of $76,161—compared to $26,128 for the people moving away. A nifty feature on the Forbes website illustrates the migration and breaks it down by average income, county-to-county. For instance, click on New Orleans (Orleans Parish, La.) and you see graphics showing not just migration in and out but the number of people moving and their average income: to Seattle (King County, Wash.), 56 people, $36,200; from Seattle, 41 people, $25,000. To get the details on migration in and out of the county of your choice—fair warning: it's addictive—click here. To see a slideshow breaking down the most popular destinations, click here.

final_summary:
A groundbreaking discovery has been made by Dr. Paul Booth, an Honorary Senior Research Fellow in History at Keele University, shedding new light on the origins of the word "fuck". While studying the Chester county court plea rolls, dated December 8, 1310, specifically the county court held on Tuesday after the feast of St Nicholas, 4 Edw. II, before Payn Tibotot, justiciar of Chester, Dr. Booth stumbled upon a name that has left historians stunned - Roger Fuckebythenavele. This name appears three times in the records, with the final mention on September 28, 1311, where Roger is officially outlawed.

The significance of this finding lies in the fact that it marks the earliest known use of the word "fuck" with a clear sexual connotation, predating previously discovered instances in medieval England. Prior to this discovery, the earliest recorded use of the word was in the poem Flen flyys, written around 1475, which contained a line that read "fvccant vvivys of heli", a Latin-English mix translating to "...they fuck the wives of Ely".

Dr. Booth suggests that the name Roger Fuckebythenavele may be a nickname, possibly referring to an incident involving Roger's alleged clumsiness during a romantic encounter, or alternatively, it could be a derogatory term implying that Roger thought he knew how to perform the act incorrectly. Another theory proposes that the name might be a medieval form of insult, similar to calling someone a "halfwit".

This remarkable find has sparked interest among linguists and historians, with some speculating that the name could be a genuine surname, while others believe it might be a clerical error or a joke inserted by the court clerk. Regardless of its origin, the appearance of the word "fuck" in the year 1310 has significant implications for the etymology of the language.

Interestingly, a similar name, John le Fucker, appeared in 1278, but it is believed to be a misinterpretation of the word "fulcher", meaning soldier. Dr. Booth's discovery has also led him to explore other unusual surnames from the same period, including Hunfridus de aureo testiculo (Humphrey of the golden testicle) and Rogerus deus salvat feminas (Roger God-save-women).

reference: – What's in a name? Just a crass term that's endured for centuries, if this holds any water. An English history researcher says he's spotted the earliest-known "f-word" on record in court documents from 1310, the Telegraph reports. Paul Booth came upon a reference to "Roger F***ebythenavele"—likely the guy's nickname, Booth says—while analyzing court records during the stormy rule of Edward II (1307-27). The name is "written clearly, and three times, and I think that shows it's not a joke," says Booth. Looks like F***ebythenavele was summoned twice to court in Cheshire, in December of 1310 and May of 1311, and finally outlawed on September 28, 1311. A court clerk might have altered the guy's name for fun, Booth admits, but short of that he sees two explanations. "First, that it applies to an actual event—a clumsy attempt at sexual intercourse by an 'Inexperienced Copulator' (my name for Roger), revealed to the world by a revengeful former girlfriend," he tells Vice.com. "Fourteenth-century revenge porn perhaps?" Or it might be an "elaborate way of describing someone regarded as a 'halfwit'—ie, that is the way that he would think of performing the sexual act." Before this, the earliest f-word on record was the phrase (translated from a Latin/English mix) "…they f**k the wives of Ely" from the poem "Flen flyys," circa 1475, Medievalists.net reports. The word has Germanic roots and is related to words that have "sexual meanings as well as meaning[s] such as 'to strike' or 'to move back and forth,'" writes Jesse Sheidlower in his book The F Word.

final_summary:
On Tuesday, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit delivered a significant blow to the concept of net neutrality, striking down the Federal Communications Commission's (FCC) 2010 Open Internet rules that ensured equal treatment of all internet traffic. The ruling effectively allows internet service providers (ISPs) like AT&T, Comcast, and Verizon to discriminate against content they dislike, favoring their business partners instead. This shift in power dynamics could have far-reaching consequences for consumers, small businesses, and the overall structure of the internet.

Under the previous rules, ISPs were required to treat all internet traffic equally, preventing them from speeding up or slowing down specific websites or services. However, with the court's decision, ISPs can now charge content providers extra for faster content delivery, forcing smaller websites and startups to pay for priority access. This could lead to a situation where larger corporations, like Comcast-owned NBC News, receive preferential treatment over smaller competitors like Fox or CNN.

The loss of net neutrality is particularly concerning for small businesses, as they will struggle to compete with larger corporations that can afford to pay for faster content delivery. Independent music sites, web publishers, and other online services may find themselves hindered or blocked by ISPs, further exacerbating the digital divide. In extreme cases, ISPs may even block content entirely, reminiscent of the past when AT&T controlled the telephone networks in America.

The court's decision also raises concerns about the impact on popular services like Netflix, which relies heavily on fast and reliable internet connections to deliver its content. ISPs could slow or block peer-to-peer file sharing and online video streaming, affecting millions of users worldwide. Furthermore, the ruling may lead to a situation where ISPs prioritize their own content over that of competitors, stifling innovation and creativity online.

In addition to these concerns, the court's decision may pave the way for the emergence of "sponsored data" plans, where content providers pay for their users' data consumption. This could fundamentally alter the way people access the internet, making it more expensive for users to access certain services unless they are subsidized by the content providers themselves. Companies like Facebook have already begun exploring this model, offering free access to basic services and apps in exchange for advertising revenue.

The FCC, now led by Chairman Tom Wheeler, a former industry lobbyist, faces a daunting task in responding to the court's decision. Options include appealing the ruling to the U.S. Supreme Court or reclassifying broadband providers as common carriers, subject to stricter regulations. However, both paths are fraught with challenges, and many experts believe the agency will ultimately cave to industry pressure.

As the dust settles, consumers, small businesses, and internet enthusiasts are left wondering what the future holds for the open and accessible internet they once knew. With the FCC's authority to regulate ISPs severely limited, the stage is set for a new era of internet governance, one that may prioritize corporate interests over consumer needs and innovation.

Circuit dealt a significant blow to the concept of net neutrality, striking down the Federal Communications Commission's (FCC) 2010 Open Internet rules that ensured equal treatment of all internet traffic. The ruling effectively allows internet service providers (ISPs) like AT&T, Comcast, and Verizon to discriminate against content they dislike, favoring their business partners instead. This shift in power dynamics could have far-reaching consequences for consumers, small businesses, and the overall structure of the internet.

Under the previous rules, ISPs were required to treat all internet traffic equally, preventing them from speeding up or slowing down specific websites or services. However, with the court's decision, ISPs can now charge content providers extra for faster content delivery, forcing smaller websites and startups to pay for priority access. This could lead to a situation where larger corporations, like Comcast-owned NBC News, receive preferential treatment over smaller competitors like Fox or CNN.

The loss of net neutrality is particularly concerning for small businesses, as they will struggle to compete with larger corporations that can afford to pay for faster content delivery. Independent music sites, web publishers, and other online services may find themselves hindered or blocked by ISPs, further exacerbating the digital divide. In extreme cases, ISPs may even block content entirely, reminiscent of the pre-internet era when AT&T controlled the telephone networks.

The court's decision also raises concerns about the impact on popular services like Netflix, which relies heavily on fast and reliable internet connections to deliver its content. ISPs could slow or block peer-to-peer file sharing and online video streaming, affecting millions of users worldwide. Furthermore, the ruling may lead to a situation where ISPs prioritize their own content over that of competitors, stifling innovation and creativity online.

In addition to these concerns, the court's decision may pave the way for the emergence of "sponsored data" plans, where content providers pay for their users' data consumption. This could fundamentally alter the way people access the internet, making it more expensive for users to access certain services unless they are subsidized by the content providers themselves. Companies like Facebook have already begun exploring this model, offering free access to basic services and apps in exchange for advertising revenue.

The FCC, now led by Chairman Tom Wheeler, a former industry lobbyist, faces a daunting task in responding to the court's decision. Options include appealing the ruling to the U.S. Supreme Court or reclassifying broadband providers as common carriers, subject to stricter regulations. However, both paths are fraught with challenges, and many experts believe the agency will ultimately cave to industry pressure.

As the dust settles, consumers, small businesses, and internet enthusiasts are left wondering what the future holds for the open and accessible internet they once knew. With the FCC's authority to regulate ISPs severely limited, the stage is set for a new era of internet governance, one that may prioritize corporate interests over consumer needs and innovation.

reference: – Yesterday was the day net neutrality died, with a court ruling that Internet service providers aren't bound by FCC non-discrimination rules—so they can prioritize some traffic over others. The decision may mean major changes for the everyday user, and today, media sites are offering the details. Among their warnings: The Wall Street Journal reports that the decision could have a huge impact on sites like Netflix and YouTube, whose video services hog a lot of bandwidth (they account for 32% and 19% of peak web traffic, respectively). If ISPs start making Netflix, for instance, pay a usage fee to maintain speedy content delivery, that cost could make a serious dent in its profits—or trickle down to consumers. ISPs could charge sites big fees in exchange for faster content delivery, potentially giving giants who can pay, like Amazon, a big leg up over independent retailers—not to mention other small businesses, the Huffington Post notes. Providers might even go so far as to prioritize traffic to partner sites—Time Warner might push CNN, for example. Another anti-competitive fear, via BuzzFeed: Big companies might start paying your data fees for the use of their sites. That might sound good—free data?—but it would mean that companies with the ability to pay would have a major advantage over start-ups in winning users. As BuzzFeed points out, "If, in this future, you're choosing between two streaming music services, and one of them pays for your data, there's a very good chance you’re going to pick that one." Beyond concerns over fees, there are worries that providers could block certain websites completely: Comcast users could theoretically end up with no access to, say, Netflix or Vonage, writes Troy Wolverton in the San Jose Mercury News. But net neutrality, in some form, may live to see another day, the Journal notes, reporting that the ruling "left open the door for the FCC to craft rules in a different form that might accomplish its earlier intentions."

final_summary:
On November 9, 1861, just seven months into the American Civil War, the sidewheel steamer Keystone State met its demise in Lake Huron, resulting in the loss of 33 crew members. However, it wouldn't be until more than a week later, when wreckage was spotted off Port Austin and the ship's wheelhouse washed ashore, that the public became aware of the tragedy. For over a century and a half, the fate of the Keystone State remained shrouded in mystery, with speculation surrounding a possible clandestine cargo load of gold and war materials.

This year, renowned shipwreck hunter David Trotter, founder of Undersea Research Associates, shed new light on the enigmatic incident. On Monday, December 9, Trotter announced the discovery of the Keystone State in Lake Huron, approximately 50 miles north of Michigan's thumb, at a depth of less than 200 feet. Trotter, an experienced shipwreck sleuth with over 100 discoveries under his belt, revealed that the ship was not located where it was expected to be, sparking excitement among historians and enthusiasts alike.

Trotter's team utilized side-scan sonar to locate the wreck in July and conducted multiple dives to document the site and investigate the ship's cargo. The Keystone State, measuring 288 feet in length, was a luxurious vessel for its time, launched in 1849 as one of the largest side-wheel steamers on the Great Lakes. Initially, the ship was bound for Milwaukee, carrying "iron implements" – or farm machinery – according to the cargo manifest. Rumors persist that the Keystone State was actually transporting military supplies intended for the battlefield, given the unusual timing and destination.

David Trotter expressed skepticism regarding the cargo manifest, noting that farm equipment is typically not used during this time of year. Furthermore, the ship departed Detroit hastily, without lifeboats, suggesting that the crew might have jettisoned cargo to lighten the load and potentially save the vessel. Historical records indicate that the Keystone State was last spotted near Port Austin, struggling against rough waters. Captain Wilkes Travers may have hesitated to steer the ship towards land, fearing it could capsize in a sea trough, making control challenging due to the ship's design.

Trotter's team continues to unravel the mystery, exploring the wreck and assessing the extent of damage caused by the storm, the impact upon hitting the lakebed, and the effects of 152 years of sedimentation. Although no gold has been found, the surrounding area contains a substantial debris field waiting to be explored. Due to the water depth, divers can only spend around 15 to 20 minutes examining the wreck before needing to decompress for over an hour on the ascent.

As a seasoned shipwreck hunter, David Trotter remains driven by the thrill of uncovering historical secrets. With each new find, he emphasizes the significance of touching tangible pieces of history that captivate people worldwide. As he aptly puts it, "The most exciting shipwreck I've ever found is the next one."

reference: – The Keystone State, which set sail in 1849 as one of the largest and most luxurious steamer ships of its time, sank during a November storm in 1861 with 33 people aboard. No one knew its fate for more than a week, until debris was spotted, and it's been lost since then—until now. A shipwreck hunter found it at the bottom of Lake Huron, 40 to 50 miles from where it was last seen and far from where David Trotter expected to find it. Some historians say the ship may have been secretly hauling Civil War supplies when it sank, the Detroit Free Press reports—and there are even rumors it may have been carrying gold, according to Michigan Live. By 1857, after an economic downturn and the rise of the railroad, many ships like the Keystone State were considered too pricey to operate. By 1861, however, thanks to the Civil War, the Keystone State had been taken out of storage and refurbished. It picked up its cargo in Detroit—officially, farm implements, hardware, and grain, but "it was an emergency shipment," a historian explains; he adds that typically a ship wouldn't have made a trip like that so late in the year. Also suspect: The ship wasn't carrying any lifeboats (everyone aboard died in the wreck), which could indicate it left in a hurry. Trotter's team found her using side-scan sonar in July, and has since made 30 dives at the site—which turned up no gold or cargo. Some speculate that the crew dumped the cargo in a bid to save the ship. (Another ship was found this year in Lake Superior.)

final_summary:
A devastating attack shook the southern region of Afghanistan on Saturday, January 29, 2011, specifically in the strategic province of Kandahar. According to the Associated Press, a suicide bomber, riding a motorcycle packed with explosives, deliberately targeted a vehicle carrying Deputy Governor Abdul Latif Ashna, resulting in his tragic demise and injuring three of his bodyguards. This brazen act of violence occurred as Ashna was en route to work in the provincial capital of Kandahar city.

The Taliban swiftly claimed responsibility for the attack through a text message sent to reporters, stating that the suicide bomber successfully killed the deputy governor along with three of his bodyguards and his driver. The U.S. Ambassador to Afghanistan, Karl Eikenberry, and Afghan President Hamid Karzai strongly condemned the assassination, labeling it a setback to efforts to stabilize Kandahar.

This high-profile attack serves as a stark reminder of the ongoing conflict in Afghanistan, particularly in the Taliban's traditional stronghold of Kandahar. The province has witnessed numerous violent incidents in recent times, including a bicycle bomb attack that wounded at least 10 individuals, and a suicide car bombing last month that resulted in the deaths of three people and injuries to 26 others, mostly police personnel.

In a separate incident, a Taliban suicide bomber detonated explosives at a Kabul supermarket frequented by affluent Afghans and foreigners on Friday, claiming the lives of six members of a prominent Afghan family, including Dr. Massoud Yama, a young doctor, his wife Hamida Barmaki, a political science professor, and their four children. The Taliban initially claimed that their target was an official with the U.S.-based security contractor, Xe, formerly known as Blackwater; however, a representative for USTC Holdings, the new owner of Xe, denied any involvement.

Afghan authorities reported that one man, one boy, and six females – all Afghan nationals – perished in the blast, while 15 others sustained injuries, including 10 Afghans and five foreigners. A senior international intelligence official revealed that the Taliban's Haqqani network was responsible for the attack, although there was no evidence suggesting that the security contractor was the intended target.

As the situation in Afghanistan continues to unfold, protests erupted in various cities, including Kabul, Mazar-i-Sharif, and Herat, condemning the execution of Afghans in Iran and calling for the release of Afghan political prisoners. Demonstrators brandished signs reading "Death to Iranian President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad" and displayed photographs of Afghans detained in Iran.

Meanwhile, in a ceremony at the Afghan Ministry of Defense, U.S.-led coalition deputy chief of staff for operations, Major General John W. Nicholson, was promoted to the rank of major general, marking a significant milestone in the transfer of security responsibilities from international forces to Afghan ones by the end of 2014. The promotion was attended by General David Petraeus, the top NATO commander in Afghanistan, and Lieutenant General Sher Mohammad Karimi, the Afghan Army's Chief of Operations.

The attack on Deputy Governor Ashna highlights the ongoing struggle for stability and security in Kandahar, a region critical to the overall war effort in Afghanistan. As the U.S. and its allies continue to grapple with the Taliban insurgency, the recent surge in violence underscores the complexities and challenges facing the region.

final_summary: Sean "Diddy" Combs broke his silence Sunday, addressing the death of ex Kim Porter. Taking to social media, the rapper posted a touching tribute to the mother of three of his children, who died Thursday at the age of 47. For the last three days I've been trying to wake up out of this nightmare. But I haven't. I don't know what I'm going to do without you baby. I miss you so much," Diddy, 49, wrote. Today I'm going to pay tribute to you, I'm going to try and find the words to explain our unexplainable relationship. We were more than best friends, we were more than soulmates. WE WERE SOME OTHER S–T!! And I miss you so much. Super Black Love. A rep confirmed to Page Six on Thursday that Porter had passed away. Law enforcement insiders previously told TMZ that authorities were called to Porter's LA home, with a 911 call reporting "a patient in cardiac arrest." A source also revealed Porter had been experiencing flu-like symptoms for a number of weeks. Diddy and Porter dated on and off for 13 years and share three children together, son Christian, and 11-year-old twin daughters, Jessie James and D'Lila. Diddy also adopted Porter's son Quincy from her relationship with music producer Al B. Sure!.

history.draft:
Sean "Diddy" Combs, a renowned rapper and entrepreneur, finally broke his silence on Sunday regarding the passing of his long-time partner, Kim Porter. Porter, a 46-year-old model and actress, was found deceased at her residence in Toluca Lake, California, on Thursday. In a heartfelt tribute shared on social media, Diddy expressed his grief and struggle to come to terms with the loss, stating that he had been trying to wake up from a nightmare for the past three days. He confessed that he didn't know how to cope with life without Porter, whom he affectionately referred to as "baby." 

The two had been in a relationship on-and-off for 13 years, during which time they welcomed three children together - 20-year-old son Christian and 11-year-old twin daughters Jessie James and D'Lila. Additionally, Diddy adopted Porter's son Quincy from her previous relationship with music producer Al B. Sure! Despite their breakup in 2007, the couple maintained a close bond until her untimely demise. According to reports from law enforcement insiders, Porter had been experiencing flu-like symptoms for several weeks prior to her passing, which led to a 911 call reporting a patient in cardiac arrest at her residence. Although the exact cause of her death remains unknown, Diddy's emotional tribute serves as a poignant reminder of the profound impact Porter had on his life.

Porter, 46, was found dead Thursday in her Toluca Lake, Calif., home. No cause of death has been released.

final_summary:
On December 5, 2016, Dr. Muffazal Lakdawala, a renowned bariatric surgeon based in Mumbai, took to social media to appeal for help in securing a medical visa for Eman Ahmed Abd El Aty, a 36-year-old Egyptian woman weighing approximately 500 kilograms, or 1,102 pounds. Abd El Aty, who has not left her home in Egypt for over 25 years, was in dire need of potentially lifesaving surgery to address her extreme weight, which made her the heaviest woman alive by roughly 450 pounds, surpassing the previous record held by Carol Yager, who weighed 1,189 pounds at her heaviest. 

Abd El Aty's sister, Chaymaa', reached out to Lakdawala after Egyptian doctors deemed further treatment futile, and the surgeon was moved by her plight, stating, "If I can somehow use whatever God-gifted talent I have to save her, I must try." Lakdawala, who has performed numerous high-profile surgeries, including those on Indian ministers Nitin Gadkari and Venkaiah Naidu, expressed concern about the risks involved in operating on Abd El Aty, citing that transporting her would require demolishing a wall in her room due to her massive size.

Initially, the Indian Embassy in Cairo refused Abd El Aty's visa application due to the requirement for in-person fingerprinting, prompting Lakdawala to tweet External Affairs Minister Sushma Swaraj, who responded promptly despite being hospitalized for kidney failure. Swaraj ensured that the medical visa was processed within 24 hours, allowing Abd El Aty to proceed with her journey to Mumbai for surgery.

Lakdawala, who has performed the most single-incision laparoscopic surgeries worldwide, planned to operate on Abd El Aty using a minimally invasive procedure. However, he emphasized that the surgery would be "very high-risk" and that Abd El Aty would require extensive care and recovery time, potentially lasting two to three months in Mumbai. The surgeon estimated that it may take two to three years for Abd El Aty to achieve a healthy weight of 100 kilograms.

Abd El Aty's family claimed that she weighed 5 kilograms at birth and was diagnosed with elephantiasis, a parasitic infection causing severe swelling. However, Lakdawala disputed this diagnosis, attributing her condition to obesity-related lymphoedema instead. He stated that Abd El Aty's sister had contacted him in October, and he began fundraising efforts to cover the costs of a private chartered flight from Cairo to Mumbai.

After overcoming the hurdle of obtaining a visa, Abd El Aty's family and Lakdawala faced another challenge – arranging for a suitable aircraft to transport her safely. Lakdawala anticipated that Abd El Aty would fly to Mumbai within a short period, marking the beginning of her long and arduous journey towards a healthier life.

reference: – Eman Ahmed Abd El Aty's family says the 36-year-old Egyptian woman hasn't left her home in 25 years, the BBC reports. Her extreme weight forced her to crawl instead of walk by age 11, and an ensuing stroke left her bedridden. Her family says she weighs more than 1,100 pounds, which would make her the heaviest woman alive—the current Guinness record-holder is only 643 pounds—and close to the heaviest woman ever, according to the Washington Post. While Abd El Aty's weight hasn't been confirmed, Dr. Muffazal Lakdawala in India estimates she is at least 990 pounds based on photos of her. "My initial reaction was '‘How is she even alive?'" he says. His next reaction was to try to save her. Abd El Aty is expected to arrive in Mumbai for surgery in the next 10 days, but it won't be an easy journey. "In every sense, she's a challenge," Lakdawala says. The bariatric surgeon has been raising money to get her to Mumbai and will be operating pro bono, the Times of India reports. But it's not just money that's an issue. Because she can't leave her room, Abd El Aty was unable to make it to the Indian embassy to be fingerprinted for a visa; only a personal plea from Lakdawala secured her an exemption. And while transporting Abd El Aty will be challenging in its own right, Lakdawala says the surgery itself is "very high risk." He says he's "hopeful" but not "confident" he can help Abd El Aty lose up to 300 pounds. (For the obese, objects are closer than they appear.)

final_summary:
On Thursday, October 9, 2014, a significant event unfolded in Woburn, Massachusetts, as the Bostonian Society, a non-profit organization responsible for managing the historic Old State House in Boston, finally uncovered a long-lost time capsule that had been hidden within the lion statue atop the landmark for over a century. The time capsule, measuring approximately 8-by-12 inches in length and weighing between five and ten pounds, was discovered after the lion statue was removed from its perch for refurbishment. According to reports, the statue was taken down last month, and the time capsule was located inside the lion's head, secured by copper bands and a crown.

The discovery was made possible thanks to the efforts of Heidi Rogers Grundhauser, the great-great-granddaughter of Samuel Rogers, a craftsman who had worked on the Old State House renovations in 1901 and was believed to have placed the time capsule in the lion's head. Grundhauser, a genealogy enthusiast from Chubbuck, Idaho, had been researching her family history and stumbled upon references to the time capsule in her ancestor's letters. She contacted the Bostonian Society in 2011, confirming the existence of the capsule and sparking renewed interest in uncovering its contents.

As the time capsule was carefully removed from the lion's head by Bob Shure, the president of Skylight Studios in Woburn, where the statue was being refurbished, excitement grew among onlookers. Inside the copper box, historians were surprised to find a mysterious red book, which was not mentioned in the 1901 Boston Daily Globe article detailing the contents of the capsule. The article listed items such as photographs of prominent politicians, letters, and newspapers from the time period, but the red book remained a mystery.

Elizabeth Roscio, the library and archives manager at the Bostonian Society, expressed her delight at the remarkably good condition of the contents, with no signs of mold or yellowing of paper. However, due to the fragile nature of the items, the decision was made not to remove them from the box, instead opting to sort and study them in a controlled environment. The contents of the box will eventually be put on public display.

The Bostonian Society is now seeking suggestions from the public on what to include in a new time capsule, which will replace the extracted box. Some proposed items include an iPhone 6, a photo of Boston Mayor Marty Walsh, and a Mike's Pastry cannoli. Brian LeMay, the president of the Bostonian Society, stated that the organization aims to create a new time capsule that will be easier to access than the current one, ensuring that future generations can appreciate its contents without difficulty.

In related news, the Bostonian Society is planning to include a Boston Marathon medal and images and documents from the present day in the new time capsule. The event marked a significant milestone in the history of the Old State House, which has played a pivotal role in Boston's past, serving as the seat of English rule in the northern colonies, the site of the Boston Massacre, and the location where the Declaration of Independence was first read to Bostonians in 1776.

reference: – New York City opened an old time capsule today that turned out to be ... kind of boring. Boston, on the other hand, has itself a mysterious red book, reports WBUR. Historians today removed a copper box that was placed inside the lion atop the Old State House back in 1901 and were surprised to find the hard-cover book on top, apparently in great condition. The curious will have to wait a little longer, however, because officials didn't open the book or anything beneath it inside the box—they'll do that later in a controlled environment to make sure nothing gets ruined. Expect old photos, letters, newspapers, and similar memorabilia. But as for the book, no mention of it was made in a newspaper article of the time about the time capsule. An official with the Bostonian Society guesses that it might be a family history of a carpenter named Samuel Rogers, reports the Boston Globe. Rogers worked on the building and helped prepare the time capsule, notes a separate Globe story. And while he did reportedly pop a family history inside, there's no account of it being in a red book. Eventually, all the items will go on public display, and a new time capsule will go back inside the lion. One thing for sure that will be included: a medal from the 2013 Boston Marathon, the one hit by bombs, reports AP. (Click for more on a time capsule that was opened 40 years late.)

final_summary:
On September 20, 2015, the Emmy Awards ceremony took place, but it turned into a nightmare for thousands of viewers when organizers created a montage of spoilers for several hit television shows. The montage revealed the endings to popular series such as Nurse Jackie, Boardwalk Empire, Sons Of Anarchy, and Mad Men, which had all ended earlier this year, leaving many fans stunned and disappointed. Host Andy Samberg acknowledged the frustration, saying, "Welcome back to the Spoiler Awards. I guess everyone on every show died."

As expected, the reaction on Twitter was overwhelmingly negative, with users expressing their outrage and disappointment. Dalton Ross tweeted, "The #Emmys just set the land-speed record for most spoilers per minute." Another user, Ashley Renee, wrote, "That whole #Emmys montage celebrating shows that have ended was just a series of spoilers for anyone who hasn't finished any of those shows." However, not everyone sympathized with the complainants, with Kelly Sharon tweeting, "To everyone complaining about spoilers: most of those shows ended in the spring. If you haven't watched you're not going to."

Jon Hamm, the star of Mad Men, won the lead actor prize at the Microsoft Theatre after an eight-year wait, but the excitement was overshadowed by the controversy surrounding the spoilers. Fans took to Twitter to express their discontent, with some users accusing the Emmys of being insensitive. One user, Derick Valadao, tweeted, "Thanks for the series finale spoilers in that montage #Emmys2015, Brutal." Others echoed similar sentiments, with Donna Dickens writing, "Hey #Emmys, we aren't all @AndySamberg! Not everyone can lock themselves in a bunker and watch ALL the shows."

Despite the backlash, some defended the decision to air the spoilers, citing that the shows had already ended earlier this year and had been widely discussed online. However, for many fans, the surprise reveal ruined their experience of watching these shows, and they felt robbed of the opportunity to discover the conclusions without prior knowledge. As one user, Joshua Topolsky, exclaimed, "I mean seriously how f------ stupid are the people who put together that montage of spoilers?"

final_summary:
A voluntary recall of cold-smoked salmon products has been expanded by Multiexport Foods, Inc, based in Miami, Florida, due to possible contamination with Listeria monocytogenes. The initial recall was launched on Saturday, December 22nd, 2012, but has since been broadened to include additional lot-sublot-batch codes. The affected products, sold under the "Paramount Reserve" brand, come in 12 oz Twin Packs and 1.25 lb packs, both of which contain UPC codes 6 88264 86705 0 and 6 88264 86664 0 respectively.

The products were distributed to various retail outlets across multiple states, including Alabama, Arkansas, Alaska, Arizona, California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Florida, Georgia, and many other states, from November 12th to December 21st, 2012. The expansion of the recall affects consumers who purchased these products in 42 states, including California and Puerto Rico, where they were sold through Wal-Mart's Sam's Club stores.

According to Wal-Mart, the company pulled the product with an abundance of caution after discovering the presence of Listeria monocytogenes during a routine laboratory test on a shipment of salmon that had not yet been distributed to stores. Although no illnesses have been reported, the bacteria can cause severe infections in vulnerable populations such as the elderly, young children, and those with weakened immune systems, leading to symptoms like fever, nausea, and diarrhea.

Consumers who possess the affected products can return them to retailers for a full refund. The specific lot-sublot-batch codes can be found on stickers attached to the back of the packaging. The recall is a collaborative effort between Wal-Mart, Multiexport Foods, Inc, and the Food and Drug Administration.

final_summary:
A growing body of research suggests that social media, particularly Facebook, plays a significant role in the aftermath of a breakup. According to a study published in the journal Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking in October 2012, individuals who continue to monitor their ex-partners on Facebook may experience prolonged distress and hindered emotional recovery. Led by researcher Tara C. Marshall, the study analyzed responses from 464 participants who had experienced a breakup and found that those who frequently checked their ex-partners' Facebook pages exhibited greater current distress, negative feelings, and longing for the ex-partner.

The researchers identified three primary factors contributing to Facebook harassment: Covert Provocation, Public Harassment, and Venting. These behaviors were linked to perpetration of cyberobsessional pursuit (COP) and obsessive relational pursuit (ORI), indicating a correlation between online and offline stalking behaviors. Moreover, participants who engaged in COP were nearly six times more likely to also commit ORI.

Another study conducted by Marshall examined the impact of remaining Facebook friends with an ex-partner on post-breakup adjustment and growth. The research involved 464 participants who were asked to rate their levels of current distress, negative feelings, sexual desire, and longing for their ex-partner. The results showed that remaining Facebook friends with an ex-partner was associated with lower personal growth, despite reduced negative feelings, sexual desire, and longing for the ex-partner.

Interestingly, the study found that people who remained Facebook friends with their ex-partners reported less negative feelings, sexual desire, and longing for the former partner compared to those who had defriended them. However, this group also demonstrated lower personal growth. The researchers proposed that unbidden exposure to an ex-partner's mundane online activity through remaining Facebook friends may decrease any residual attraction to the ex-partner, whereas defriended exes may remain shrouded in an alluring mystique.

The study also highlighted the significance of Facebook surveillance, which was defined as checking an ex-partner's Facebook page and list of friends. Frequent Facebook surveillance was associated with greater current distress, negative feelings, sexual desire, and longing for the ex-partner, as well as lower personal growth. This finding suggests that weak-tie contact with an ex-partner through Facebook contributes to poorer post-breakup functioning over and above strong-tie contact.

In addition to Facebook, other social media platforms like Twitter and Instagram can also play a role in hindering emotional recovery after a breakup. A personal anecdote shared by a writer highlights the ease with which one can stumble upon an ex-partner's life updates, making it challenging to disconnect from the relationship.

The study's findings emphasize the importance of avoiding exposure to an ex-partner, both offline and online, as a means of facilitating healing and personal growth after a breakup. The research underscores the complexities of navigating modern relationships in the digital age, where social media can both unite and isolate individuals.

Demographically, the studies' participants consisted of 84 percent females, with a mean age of 21.36 years old. The majority of participants were American (87 percent), followed by Europeans (7 percent), Canadians (2 percent), and Latin Americans (2 percent). Most participants were currently pursuing an undergraduate degree (60 percent), and 48 percent were single.

The study employed a range of measures, including the Perceived Relationship Quality Components Inventory, the Breakup Distress Scale, and the Post-Traumatic Growth Inventory. The Cronbach's alpha coefficient, a measure of internal consistency, was used to evaluate the reliability of the scales. The researchers also collected data on participants' Facebook usage habits, including the number of friends and time spent on the platform on a typical day.

The study's limitations include its correlational design, which precludes causal inference. Future research should aim to explore the motivations behind remaining Facebook friends with an ex-partner and engaging in Facebook surveillance. The study's findings were published in the journal Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking in October 2012.

reference: – What with Facebook, Flickr, and other fine venues for stalking "exes," breakups aren't nearly as final as they once were. But for your own good, please stop following them around cyberspace. "Conventional wisdom, and even science, has it that cutting off contact with an ex makes for a smoother recovery," writes Tracy Clark-Flory at Salon. And she knows the struggle all too well, having tracked an ex from Flickr to Twitter until she realized that the ring on his finger wasn't "merely an engagement ring." Not only was he already married, but Clark-Flory had to see a live-tweeted photo of him standing in the aisle. "It’s one thing to realize that the man you once wanted to marry" has moved on, she writes, "and another to be a virtual witness to it." Clark-Flory digs up studies to make her case, like one that finds "Facebook stalking ... may obstruct the process of healing" and another in which 30% of college students admit to posting status updates "to taunt or hurt" an ex. The only problem: "It’s never been easier to secretly keep tabs on exes," writes Clark-Flory, "and it’s never been harder not to."

final_summary:
In the aftermath of the 2012 Benghazi attack, which resulted in the deaths of four U.S. personnel, the Obama administration faced intense scrutiny over its handling of the crisis. According to a recent House Benghazi report, State Department officials held an emergency conference call on the night of the attack, where they discussed the possibility of deploying Marines to the scene in Cairo, Egypt. However, concerns were raised about the potential impact of the Marines' uniforms on regional diplomacy, with Under Secretary of State for Management Patrick Kennedy expressing worries that the uniforms could exacerbate tensions in The United States and the Middle East.

Despite these concerns, the military ultimately decided not to deploy to Benghazi. Instead, the focus shifted to evacuating American personnel from the area. The report reveals that the State Department's initial response to the attack was marked by confusion and a lack of coordination, with officials struggling to provide accurate information to the public through the White House.

As the day progressed, the administration's narrative surrounding the attack began to shift. Initially, Secretary of State Hillary Clinton pointed to an anti-Islamic video as the catalyst for the violence, suggesting that it had sparked a spontaneous protest that turned deadly in Egypt and Benghazi. However, internal emails and memos reveal that this account was not entirely accurate. In private communications, officials acknowledged that the attack was likely the result of a premeditated terrorist strike, rather than a protest gone awry.

The discrepancy between the public and private narratives raises questions about the administration's motives and decision-making process. While Clinton and other officials publicly tied the Benghazi attack to the video, they privately recognized that the violence was a terrorist attack with no connection to a protest or video. The report highlights the tension between the administration's desire to downplay the role of terrorism and its need to provide a coherent explanation for the attack.

The report also criticizes Clinton for failing to adequately address the growing threat of extremism in Libya, despite receiving warnings from within the State Department. The final GOP-authored House Benghazi report, released on Tuesday, includes new details of a two-hour video teleconference call the night of the attack, which was led by White House Chief of Staff Denis McDonough and attended by Clinton and top Defense officials.

Representative Elijah Cummings, the ranking Democrat on the House Benghazi panel, has accused the Republican-led committee of attempting to hide exculpatory evidence and preventing him and other Democrats from accessing key documents. The controversy surrounding the Benghazi attack has become a contentious issue in the lead-up to the presidential election, with Clinton facing renewed scrutiny over her handling of the crisis.

reference: – The Republican-led House Benghazi committee released its final report Tuesday after two years of investigations. The 800-page report lays out the committee's findings on the 2012 attacks in Libya that left four Americans dead. Here's what you need to know: Benghazi is "unlikely to be a potent tool for weakening Clinton" in the general election, as the committee and report have little credibility and aren't seen as serious by many, the Los Angeles Times reports. Easily distilling the massive report, the Washington Post lays out its five "most serious accusations," including that the CIA "missed warning signs" and "misread how dangerous Libya ... was at the time." New evidence of “culpability or wrongdoing” on Clinton's part is lacking in the report, despite it being “one of the longest, costliest, and most bitterly partisan congressional investigations in history," according to the New York Times. Given a lack of new evidence against Clinton, the Weekly Standard uses the report as a tool for blaming Obama. “The Obama administration knowingly provided the American people a false story about the Benghazi attack, its causes, and its consequences," the Standard states. How various government agencies responded to the Benghazi crisis is one of the more interesting aspects of the report. According to Politico, a conference call in the midst of the attack included a conversation about whether Marines should wear their uniforms if deployed. Marines changed in and out of their uniforms multiple times while waiting for an answer and were never deployed. According to the Hill, a Democratic lawmaker on the House Benghazi committee calls it "one of the saddest exercises I've ever engaged in" during 20 years in Congress. He accuses Republicans of hiding the final report from Democrats to avoid having it fact-checked. Zeroing in on what it calls the "most revealing paragraph" in the report, Vox concludes there's a pattern of spinning "non-damning facts as damning" for the Obama administration. The paragraph in question admits there were no US forces close enough to prevent the attack but insists that, in itself, was a failure on the part of the White House. "I think it's time to move on," the AP quotes Clinton as saying after the release of the report, which she says includes nothing not previously discovered by an accountability board.

final_summary:
Eli Rosenbaum, a dedicated Nazi hunter and director of the Justice Department's special investigation unit, has spent his career tracking down and bringing to justice individuals who committed atrocities during World War II. Born into a Jewish family, Rosenbaum's interest in the subject began when he was a child, watching a dramatic reenactment of the Auschwitz trial on television, which aired on a Sunday evening. This sparked a lifelong passion for uncovering the truth about the Holocaust and holding perpetrators accountable.

Rosenbaum's work involves investigating and prosecuting former Nazis who settled in the United States after the war, often using scarce documentation and relying on witnesses who are increasingly difficult to find due to the passage of time. As the number of potential suspects dwindles, Rosenbaum faces mounting pressure to complete his cases within a shrinking timeframe. In 1949, Palij emigrated to the United States as a refugee of World War II, and in 1957, he was granted United States citizenship. However, despite this, Rosenbaum remains resolute in his pursuit of justice, driven by a sense of responsibility to the victims of the Holocaust.

One such case involves Jakiw Palij, a 92-year-old man living in a modest apartment in Jackson Heights, Queens, New York. Palij, who worked as a guard at the Trawniki concentration camp in Poland, has been the subject of a prolonged deportation attempt by the Justice Department. However, despite a federal judge's order to deport him in 2004, Palij remains in the United States, citing his advanced age and poor health as reasons why he should not be removed.

Palij claims that he was coerced into working for the Nazis, but experts dismiss this claim, pointing out that individuals who worked at the camps were typically chosen for their loyalty and brutality. Rabbi Marvin Hier, dean and founder of the Simon Wiesenthal Center, notes that guards at the camps were often eager participants in the atrocities, rather than reluctant conscripts.

In contrast to Palij's claims of innocence, Rosenbaum describes Palij's actions as contributing directly to the slaughter of thousands of people at the Trawniki camp. According to Rosenbaum, Palij's role as a guard prevented others from escaping and ultimately led to the massacre of up to 7,000 people. The U.S. government has stripped Palij of his citizenship, but he remains in the United States, awaiting deportation to a country that will accept him.

Rosenbaum's work is supported by the U.S. Holocaust Memorial Museum, which provides access to valuable documentation on war crimes. Peter Black, a senior historian at the museum, praises Rosenbaum's dedication to his mission, saying that he has become obsessed with achieving justice for the victims of the Holocaust. Despite the many frustrations and setbacks that have arisen throughout his career, Rosenbaum remains committed to his work, driven by a sense of responsibility to honor the memories of those who suffered during the Holocaust.

As the world grapples with the legacy of World War II, Rosenbaum's work serves as a reminder of the importance of accountability and justice. By pursuing every last Nazi, Rosenbaum believes that he is doing the least that can be done to honor the victims of the Holocaust. With time running out, Rosenbaum continues to push forward, determined to bring closure to the families of those who suffered at the hands of the Nazis.

reference: – During some three decades with Justice Department, Eli Rosenbaum has earned a well-deserved reputation as a Nazi hunter. He has worked on 137 cases involving suspected Nazis, and in all but 30 of them, the accused lost citizenship or was deported. Now, CNN reports, just a single active case remains. But when it comes to Jakiw Palij, who lives in Queens, NY, deportation isn’t likely. In fact, a federal judge ordered the 92-year-old deported in 2004, but the European countries he could be sent to won't take him. Palij, CNN notes, will likely die here. "What Mr. Palij did prevented other people from reaching old age," says Rosenbaum, who now also oversees more recent war-related crimes as the DOJ's director of Human Rights Enforcement Strategy and Policy. Palij is accused of being a guard at the Trawniki death camp in German-occupied Poland—"in the end," Rosenbaum says, "everyone who was held there was massacred." Palij, who, per the New York Times, came to the US in 1949 as a refugee using falsified immigration papers, has denied any wrongdoing. In 2003, he told the Times that the Nazis coerced him into service patrolling bridges and roads at night. "We knew they would kill me and my family if I refused," he says. "I did it to save their lives, and I never even wore a Nazi uniform." The founder of the Simon Wiesenthal Center downplayed that line of argument: "To work at those camps, you had to be eager to be there. They only took people they knew were loyal and brutal and not sympathetic to pleas of the inmates." In a 2013 interview with NPR, Rosenbaum described questioning the suspects about their alleged Nazi pasts as "surreal ... these people look close to harmless." (Joseph Goebbels' "love nest" is proving to be a tough sell for Berlin.)

final_summary:
Steve Wynn's plans to sell a pair of Picasso paintings at Christie's auction house were dealt a significant blow when one of the pieces, a 1943 self-portrait titled "Le Marin," suffered damage during the final stages of preparation for the sale on Friday. The painting, valued at $70 million, was set to be sold alongside two other artworks, including a 1964 portrait of a woman with a cat, "Femme au chat assise dans un fauteuil," and Andy Warhol's 1963 "Double Elvis [Ferus Type]," which is still scheduled to go up for auction on May 17. 

The damaged Picasso painting, "Le Marin," was part of a trio of works consigned to Christie's by Wynn, with an estimated total value of as much as $135 million. However, the sale of the second Picasso piece, "Femme au chat assise dans un fauteuil," was also withdrawn from the auction by mutual agreement between the seller and Christie's on Monday. The latter artwork was valued at $25 million to $35 million.

This latest setback comes a dozen years after Wynn accidentally damaged another Picasso masterpiece, "Le Reve," with his elbow in 2006. The incident left a hole the size of a silver dollar in the painting, which was valued at $139 million prior to the damage. After repairs, the painting's value was reduced to $85 million, according to Wynn's lawsuit against Lloyd's of London.
